title: Acute Coronary Syndrome
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: acute coronary syndrome guideline number: guid-3203-c001 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to improve the health of the patient with myocardial ischemia and provide medical interventions to minimize the effects of a cardiac event. acute coronary syndrome covers clinical conditions ranging from angina, unstable angina, non-q-wave myocardial infarction, q-wave myocardial infarction, and non-st elevation myocardial infarction (nstemi). this guideline is for patients not going directly to the cath lab for immediate intervention. ii. department guideline: a. assess and manage airway, breathing and circulation. b. provide supplemental oxygen to maintain oxygen saturation > 93%. c. initiate cardiac monitoring, pulse ox, and serial vital signs. apply end tidal co2 monitor to all intubated patients. record readings. d. obtain/evaluate 12 lead ecg and interpret findings. assess for dysrhythmias and treat accordingly. perform a repeat ecg if clinically indicated. i. if stemi indicated by ecg, refer to guid3203-c010 st elevation myocardial infarction e. ensure administration of aspirin 162-324 mg po (chewable) unless contraindicated by a true allergy or previously given within last 8 hours. f. establish iv access. (consider 2 sites.) g. if symptoms persist, consider administering nitroglycerine (ntg) 0.4 mg sl every 5 minutes x 3 doses if hemodynamically stable. use caution in inferior wall mi or right ventricular infarct in v4r. i. if symptoms are relieved by sl ntg or if pain persists, consider initiating a nitroglycerin infusion at 10 mcg/min, titrate until pain free, keeping map > 65 mmhg or sbp > 100 mmhg. do not exceed 200 mcg/min. ii. ntg is contraindicated if patient has received drugs for erectile dysfunction such as sildenafil (viagra) or vardenafil (levitra) within the past 24 hours and 72 hours for tadalafil (cialis). (consult medical direction). h. if severe pain persists, consider administering: i. morphine 0.1 mg/kg slow iv/io may repeat every 10 minutes prn ii. fentanyl 0.5-2 mcg/kg slow iv/io may repeat every 10 minutes prn page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: acute coronary syndrome guideline number: guid-3203-c001 iii. monitor respiratory status and consider etco2 monitoring i. if the patient has not received low molecular weight heparin during the previous 12 hours and heparin therapy has not been initiated, consider administration of heparin after consulting with md. i. heparin bolus of 60 units/kg (max 5,000 units) followed by heparin infusion at 18 units/kg (max 1,000 units per hour). j. if patient's destination is not the cath lab, all iv medications such as low dose nitroglycerin and heparin should be continued. k. if glycoprotein iib/iiia inhibitor/integrilin therapy has been initiated, continue infusion per referring physician's protocol. l. if hypotension is present, and there is no evidence of pulmonary edema, consider an iv fluid challenge of 250 ml 0.9% ns. repeat as necessary. m. if hypotension persists, consider using vasopressors per guid3203-m011 hypotension. n. if patient is being transferred on an intra-aortic balloon pump refer to guid3203-pr023 intra- aortic balloon pump. iii. documentation: a. ems charts iv. references: a. american heart association, 2015. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Asystole & Pulseless Electrical Activity
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: asystole and pulseless electrical activity guideline number: guid-3203-c002 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: successful treatment of asystole/pea with a return of spontaneous circulation ii. definitions: a. pea - pulseless electrical activity b. cpr - cardiopulmonary resuscitation iii. department guideline: a. initiate cpr per american heart association (aha) guideline, administer oxygen, apply etco2 monitor if advanced airway present, and maintain etco2 > 10 mmhg. i. if available, an fda approved cardiac compression/mechanical cpr device may be applied and utilized per manufacturer's instructions b. apply cardiac monitor, confirm rhythm in two leads. perform rhythm analysis every 2 minutes between cycles of cpr. c. consider possible causes and treat accordingly. ("h's and t's: hypovolemia, hypoxia, hypo/hyperkalemia, hypoglycemia, hypothermia, acidosis, drug overdose, cardiac tamponade, tension pneumothorax, thrombosis, trauma, and toxins). d. establish iv/io access. e. administer the following medication: i. epinephrine (1:10,000) 1 mg iv/io push, repeat every 3-5 minutes. f. secure airway and ventilate with 100% oxygen. avoid hyperventilation. g. consider the administration of blood products for traumatic arrest. refer to guid3203-pr025 emergency uncrossmatched blood product administration. h. consider sodium bicarbonate 1 meq/kg iv/io push for suspected tricyclic antidepressant overdose, suspected hyperkalemia, or end-stage renal disease i. administer correct treatment for suspected drug overdoses per guid3203-m019 toxic ingestion/exposure. j. if rosc is achieved prior to interfacility transfer and the patient previously had a rhythm that was defibrillated, consult with physician regarding targeted temperature management per guid3203- c009 target temperature management. k. always consider the latest recommendations from the aha page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: asystole and pulseless electrical activity guideline number: guid-3203-c002 iv. documentation: ems charts v. references: american heart association guidelines, 2015. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Atrial Fibrillation & Atrial Flutter
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: atrial fibrillation and atrial flutter guideline number: guid-3203-c003 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: control the ventricular response of the rapid heart rate by stabilizing the abnormal heart rhythm through therapeutic interventions. ii. definitions: a. afib- atrial fibrillation b. a-flutter- atrial flutter iii. department guideline: a. maintain and manage airway, breathing, and circulation. b. if the patient is not symptomatic with stable vital signs, monitor closely. c. consider applying hands-free defibrillator pads to patient's bare chest if patient is symptomatic. may proceed directly to cardioversion if patient becomes unstable. d. if patient is hemodynamically unstable (sbp < 90 mmhg, altered level of consciousness, severe chest pain, pulmonary edema): i. have the patient perform vagal maneuvers while preparing to cardiovert. ii. perform synchronized cardioversion per guid3203-pr004. for narrow irregular rhythm, initial recommended dose: 120-200 j biphasic. increase subsequent shocks in stepwise fashion. iii. consider sedation or analgesia per guid3203-g005 analgesia and sedation management. e. if patient is symptomatic and hemodynamically stable, consider the following: i. metoprolol 5 mg iv/io push over 2 minutes. may repeat x 2 every 5 minutes until a total of 15 mg. obtain an optimal heart rate of < 120 bpm. **contraindicated in patients with congestive heart failure, heart block, valvular failure, or cocaine use** -or- ii. diltiazem 0.25 mg/kg (max dose 20 mg) iv/io push over 2 minutes. if not effective in 15 minutes, a second dose of 0.35 mg/kg (max dose 25 mg) iv/io may be given. **contraindicated in patients with congestive heart failure or wide complex rhythm** 1. if persistent tachycardia above 120 bpm after two boluses, begin diltiazem infusion at 5mg/h and titrate to 15 mg/hr to maintain a hr of < 120 bpm. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: atrial fibrillation and atrial flutter guideline number: guid-3203-c003 -or- iii. if qrs is wide (greater than 12ms): amiodarone 150 mg iv bolus over 10 minutes followed by amiodarone infusion at 1 mg/min. iv. consider expert consultation. iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: a. american heart association, 2015. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Bradycardia
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: bradycardia guideline number: guid-3203-c004 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: improve cardiac output in patients with impaired sinus node function or atrial/av nodal conduction causing a disturbance in the heart's normal rhythm. ii. definitions: a. sinus bradycardia (hr <60/min, regular rhythm). b. first degree heart block (prolonged pr interval > 0.20 sec). c. second degree heart block type 1 [wenckebach] (progressive lengthening of the pr interval followed by a dropped qrs complex). d. second degree heart block type 2 (pr interval constant and set with no lengthening, some p waves will not be followed by a qrs complex). e. third degree heart block (both the atrial and ventricular rhythms are regular but independent of each other, disassociated). iii. department guideline: a. assess and manage airway, breathing, and circulation. b. apply cardiac monitor, confirm brady-dysrhythmia. c. obtain 12 lead ecg if available and time permits. identify type of av block if present. d. provide supplemental oxygen to maintain saturation > 93%. e. establish iv/io access. f. if patient is not symptomatic with stable vital signs, monitor closely. g. if patient is symptomatic, differentiate between signs and symptoms that are caused by slow rate versus those that are unrelated. i. symptoms include chest discomfort or pain, shortness of breath, decreased level of consciousness, weakness, fatigue, light-headedness, dizziness, and presyncope or syncope. ii. signs include hypotension, orthostasis, diaphoresis, pulmonary congestion on physical exam or chest x-ray, congestive heart failure page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: bradycardia guideline number: guid-3203-c004 iii. consider atropine 1 mg iv/io, may repeat every 3-5 minutes (max. dose 0.04 mg/kg up to 3 mg). atropine not likely to be effective in second degree type ii and third-degree av blocks. h. if atropine is ineffective, prepare patient for transcutaneous external pacing per guid3203- pr022. start pacing with the following settings: ma 50, hr 60. increase ma until capture is obtained. i. considering sedation and/or analgesia per guid3203-g005 analgesia and sedation management. i. alternative treatments for rate-accelerating effects include i. norepinephrine iv 0.05-1 mcg/kg/min titrate q5 min to effect ii. epinephrine infusion 0.025-0.5 mcg/kg/min iv and titrate q5 min to effect iii. dopamine infusion 5-20 mcg/kg/min iv and titrate q5 min to effect. j. if hypotension persists, consider initiating vasopressors per guid3203-m011 hypotension. k. consider alternative drugs for beta-blocker or calcium channel blocker overdoses. see guid3203- m019 toxic ingestion/exposure. l. consider expert consultation from sending or receiving physician. m. consider the latest recommendations from the american heart association. iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: a. american heart association, 2015. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Congested Heart Failure & Pulmonary Edema
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: congestive heart failure & pulmonary edema guideline number: guid-3203-c005 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: obtain and maintain sufficient cardiac output to maintain perfusion and oxygenation during acute heart failure. ii. definitions: none iii. department guideline: a. assess and manage airway, breathing, and circulation i. lung sounds for evidence of pulmonary edema (rales/wheezes). ii. auscultation of heart tones - s3 gallop may be present iii. obtain ejection fraction historical data if possible. b. provide supplemental oxygen and assist ventilations as needed to reduce work of breathing. consider non-invasive positive pressure ventilation - trial of cpap or bipap if at a sending facility per guid3203-pr018 non-invasive positive pressure ventilation. c. if necessary, secure an advanced airway per guid3203-g008 rapid sequence induction for endotracheal intubation and guid3203-pr020 endotracheal intubation and ventilate with a transport ventilator per guid3203-pr024 ventilation with a mechanical ventilator. d. administer nitroglycerine (ntg) 0.4 mg sl every 5 minutes x 3 doses if hemodynamically stable. use caution in inferior wall mi or right ventricular infarct in v4r. i. consider initiating a nitroglycerin infusion at 10 mcg/min, titrate until breathing improves, keeping map > 65 mmhg or sbp > 100 mmhg. do not exceed 200 mcg/min. ii. ntg is contraindicated if patient has received drugs for erectile dysfunction such as sildenafil (viagra) or vardenafil (levitra) within the past 24 hours and 72 hours for tadalafil (cialis). (consult medical direction). e. consider furosemide 40 mg iv. f. for pain management or comfort consider i. morphine 0.1 mg/kg slow iv/io may repeat every 10 minutes prn or ii. fentanyl 0.5-2 mcg/kg slow iv/io may repeat every 10 minutes prn iii. monitor respiratory status and consider etco2 monitoring g. if hypotension persists, consider initiating vasopressors per guid3203-m011 hypotension. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: congestive heart failure & pulmonary edema guideline number: guid-3203-c005 h. consider inserting an indwelling urinary catheter to monitor urine output (if available at the sending facility and procedure does not unnecessarily delay transport). iv. documentation: ems charts v. references: a. american heart association, 2015. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

(consult medical direction). e. consider furosemide 40 mg iv. f. for pain management or comfort consider i. morphine 0.1 mg/kg slow iv/io may repeat every 10 minutes prn or ii. fentanyl 0.5-2 mcg/kg slow iv/io may repeat every 10 minutes prn iii. monitor respiratory status and consider etco2 monitoring g. if hypotension persists, consider initiating vasopressors per guid3203-m011 hypotension. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Narrow & Wide Complex Tachycardia
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: narrow & wide complex tachycardia guideline number: guid-3203-c006 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: successful treatment of the rapid heart rate by stabilizing the abnormal heart rhythm through therapeutic interventions. ii. definitions: iii. department guideline: a. assess and manage airway, breathing, and circulation. apply oxygen if hypoxemic. b. apply cardiac monitor and confirm rhythm. c. obtain 12 lead ecg if possible. d. apply hands-free defibrillator pads to patient's bare chest. e. if patient demonstrates rate-related cardiovascular compromise with signs and symptoms such as hypotension sbp < 90 mmhg, acutely altered mental status, shock, severe ischemic chest discomfort, acute heart failure/pulmonary edema occur at any point, or other signs of shock suspected to be due to a tachyarrhythmia, consider proceeding directly to cardioversion per guid3203-pr004. i. have the patient perform vagal maneuvers while preparing to cardiovert. ii. set j dose based on current recommended dosing. 1. for narrow rhythm: 50-100 j 2. for narrow irregular rhythm: 120-200 j 3. for wide regular complex: 100 j 4. for wide irregular: defibrillation dose, not synchronized 5. if no response after initial cardioversion, give 2nd dose of 200 j. subsequent doses should be higher than initial dose up to max joule dose of monitor. (other facility/ agency monitors maximum may be up to 360 j). iii. consider sedation or analgesia per guid3203-g005 analgesia and sedation management if map >65 mmhg. iv. obtain 12 lead ecg after cardioversion as soon as practical. f. establish iv/io access (preferably large bore catheter). page 1 of 3 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: narrow & wide complex tachycardia guideline number: guid-3203-c006 g. consider causes and correct before administration of adenosine (pain, dehydration, medication withdrawal, etc.). h. consider medication administration for heart rate > 150 bpm with symptoms but stable. i. narrow complex: if the patient is stable with a regular qrs complex < 0.12 ms i. attempt vagal maneuvers. ii. adenosine 6 mg rapid iv/io push followed by a 20 ml bolus of 0.9% ns. 1. use caution in patients with history of asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, particularly if they are actively bronchospastic as adenosine may cause bronchospasm. 2. initial dose may be reduced to 3mg in patients taking dipyridamole or carbamazepine or who have a transplanted heart. iii. if the rate does not convert within 1-2 minutes, administer adenosine 12 mg rapid iv/io push followed by a 20 ml bolus of 0.9% ns. iv. if adenosine is not effective, consider administering beta-blocker or calcium channel blocker: 1. metoprolol 5 mg iv/io push over 2 minutes. may repeat x 2 every 5 minutes until a total of 15 mg. obtain an optimal heart rate of < 100 bpm. **avoid with patients in chf, heart block, valvular failure, or cocaine use** -or- 2. diltiazem 0.25 mg/kg iv/io push over 2 minutes. if not effective in 15 minutes, a second dose of 0.35 mg/kg iv/io may be given. a. if persistent tachycardia above 120 bpm after two boluses, begin diltiazem infusion at 5mg/h and titrate to 15 mg/hr to maintain a hr of < 120 bpm. j. wide complex: if the patient is stable with a regular qrs complex > 0.12 ms i. consider adenosine 6 mg rapid iv/io push followed by a 20 ml bolus of 0.9% ns if the rhythm is regular and monomorphic. ii. if adenosine is not effective or indicated, consider antiarrhythmic infusions: 1. amiodarone 150 mg iv bolus over 10 minutes followed by amiodarone infusion at 1 mg/min. amiodarone is the preferred initial agent for wide complex tachycardia -or- 2. procainamide infusion at 20-50 mg/min until arrhythmia is suppressed, hypotension ensues, qrs duration increases by 50% or maximum dose of 17 mg/kg is administered. follow with procainamide infusion at 1-4 mg/min if rhythm is suppressed. avoid in patients with prolonged qt. -or- 3. lidocaine 0.5-1.5 mg/kg iv/io bolus may repeat every 5-10 minutes at 0.5-0.75 mg/kg (max. dose 3 mg/kg). follow with lidocaine infusion at 1-2 mg/min, titrate up to 4 mg/min for effect if rhythm is suppressed. iii. consider magnesium sulfate 1-2 g iv/io over 5-20 minutes for torsades de pointes or known hypomagnesemia (chronic alcohol/diuretic use). iv. if hyperkalemia is suspected, consider administering page 2 of 3 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: narrow & wide complex tachycardia guideline number: guid-3203-c006 1. calcium chloride 1 gram iv/io over 10 minutes. 2. sodium bicarbonate 8.4% 50 meq iv/io. k. always consider the latest recommendations from the american heart association. iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: american heart association, 2015. page 3 of 3 © orlando health 2017

pages.text: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: narrow & wide complex tachycardia guideline number: guid-3203-c006 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: successful treatment of the rapid heart rate by stabilizing the abnormal heart rhythm through therapeutic interventions. ii. definitions: iii. department guideline: a. assess and manage airway, breathing, and circulation. apply oxygen if hypoxemic. b. apply cardiac monitor and confirm rhythm. c. obtain 12 lead ecg if possible. d. apply hands-free defibrillator pads to patient's bare chest. e. if patient demonstrates rate-related cardiovascular compromise with signs and symptoms such as hypotension sbp < 90 mmhg, acutely altered mental status, shock, severe ischemic chest discomfort, acute heart failure/pulmonary edema occur at any point, or other signs of shock suspected to be due to a tachyarrhythmia, consider proceeding directly to cardioversion per guid3203-pr004. i. have the patient perform vagal maneuvers while preparing to cardiovert. ii. set j dose based on current recommended dosing. 1. for narrow rhythm: 50-100 j 2. for narrow irregular rhythm: 120-200 j 3. for wide regular complex: 100 j 4. for wide irregular: defibrillation dose, not synchronized 5. if no response after initial cardioversion, give 2nd dose of 200 j. subsequent doses should be higher than initial dose up to max joule dose of monitor. (other facility/ agency monitors maximum may be up to 360 j). iii. consider sedation or analgesia per guid3203-g005 analgesia and sedation management if map >65 mmhg. iv. obtain 12 lead ecg after cardioversion as soon as practical. f. establish iv/io access (preferably large bore catheter). page 1 of 3 © orlando health 2017

1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: narrow & wide complex tachycardia guideline number: guid-3203-c006 g. consider causes and correct before administration of adenosine (pain, dehydration, medication withdrawal, etc.). h. consider medication administration for heart rate > 150 bpm with symptoms but stable. i. narrow complex: if the patient is stable with a regular qrs complex < 0.12 ms i. attempt vagal maneuvers. ii. adenosine 6 mg rapid iv/io push followed by a 20 ml bolus of 0.9% ns. 1. use caution in patients with history of asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, particularly if they are actively bronchospastic as adenosine may cause bronchospasm. 2. initial dose may be reduced to 3mg in patients taking dipyridamole or carbamazepine or who have a transplanted heart. iii. if the rate does not convert within 1-2 minutes, administer adenosine 12 mg rapid iv/io push followed by a 20 ml bolus of 0.9% ns. iv. if adenosine is not effective, consider administering beta-blocker or calcium channel blocker: 1. metoprolol 5 mg iv/io push over 2 minutes. may repeat x 2 every 5 minutes until a total of 15 mg. obtain an optimal heart rate of < 100 bpm. **avoid with patients in chf, heart block, valvular failure, or cocaine use** -or- 2. diltiazem 0.25 mg/kg iv/io push over 2 minutes. if not effective in 15 minutes, a second dose of 0.35 mg/kg iv/io may be given. a. if persistent tachycardia above 120 bpm after two boluses, begin diltiazem infusion at 5mg/h and titrate to 15 mg/hr to maintain a hr of < 120 bpm. j. wide complex: if the patient is stable with a regular qrs complex > 0.12 ms i. consider adenosine 6 mg rapid iv/io push followed by a 20 ml bolus of 0.9% ns if the rhythm is regular and monomorphic. ii. if adenosine is not effective or indicated, consider antiarrhythmic infusions: 1. amiodarone 150 mg iv bolus over 10 minutes followed by amiodarone infusion at 1 mg/min. amiodarone is the preferred initial agent for wide complex tachycardia -or- 2. procainamide infusion at 20-50 mg/min until arrhythmia is suppressed, hypotension ensues, qrs duration increases by 50% or maximum dose of 17 mg/kg is administered. follow with procainamide infusion at 1-4 mg/min if rhythm is suppressed. avoid in patients with prolonged qt. -or- 3. lidocaine 0.5-1.5 mg/kg iv/io bolus may repeat every 5-10 minutes at 0.5-0.75 mg/kg (max. dose 3 mg/kg). follow with lidocaine infusion at 1-2 mg/min, titrate up to 4 mg/min for effect if rhythm is suppressed. iii. consider magnesium sulfate 1-2 g iv/io over 5-20 minutes for torsades de pointes or known hypomagnesemia (chronic alcohol/diuretic use). iv. if hyperkalemia is suspected, consider administering page 2 of 3 © orlando health 2017

1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: narrow & wide complex tachycardia guideline number: guid-3203-c006 1. calcium chloride 1 gram iv/io over 10 minutes. 2. sodium bicarbonate 8.4% 50 meq iv/io. k. always consider the latest recommendations from the american heart association. iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: american heart association, 2015. page 3 of 3 © orlando health 2017

title: Ventricular Fibrillation & Pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: ventricular fibrillation and pulseless ventricular tachycardia guideline number: guid-3203-c007 issue date: 01/01/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: successful treatment of ventricular fibrillation and pulseless ventricular tachycardia with a return of spontaneous circulation. ii. definitions: a. vf - ventricular fibrillation b. vt - ventricular tachycardia c. rosc - return of spontaneous circulation d. cpr - cardiopulmonary resuscitation iii. department guideline: a. initiate cpr, administer oxygen, apply etco2 monitor if advanced airway present, and maintain etco2 > 10 mmhg. i. if available, an fda approved cardiac compression/mechanical cpr device may be applied and utilized per manufacturer's instructions b. apply cardiac monitor, confirm vf or vt, and prepare for defibrillation. c. defibrillate per guid3203-pr008 for persistent vf/vt 120-200 j for biphasic. resume cpr immediately after defibrillation. i. for refractory vf/vt after >3 standard defibrillation attempts, consider dual sequential defibrillation (dsed) if a second monitor is available (see guid3203-pr008) d. establish iv/io access (consider 2 sites). e. approximately every 2 minutes, reassess cardiac rhythm and assess rosc. if no rosc and a shockable rhythm, repeat defibrillation at 360 j. continue compressions. f. administer epinephrine (1:10,000) 1 mg iv/io push, repeat every 3-5 minutes. g. secure advanced airway and ventilate per guid3203-pr020 endotracheal intubation with 100% oxygen h. administer one of the following antidysrhythmic: i. amiodarone 300 mg iv/io, may repeat 150 mg in 3-5 minutes -or- ii. lidocaine 1-1.5 mg/kg iv/io may repeat every 5-10 minutes at 0.5-0.75 mg/kg (max. dose 3 mg/kg). page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: ventricular fibrillation and pulseless ventricular tachycardia guideline number: guid-3203-c007 i. consider magnesium sulfate 1-2 grams iv/io over 2 minutes for torsades de pointes or known hypomagnesemia (chronic alcohol use or diuretic use). j. consider continuous iv/io infusion of the antidysrhythmic that proved to be effective in converting rhythm. i. amiodarone infusion at 1 mg/min x 6 hours ii. lidocaine infusion at 1-2 mg/min titrate up to 4mg/min for effect. k. consider sodium bicarbonate 8.4% 50 meq iv/io for suspected hyperkalemia in dialysis patients or tricyclic antidepressant overdoses. l. if rosc, refer to guid3203-c009 - targeted temperature management. m. always consider the latest recommendations from the american heart association. iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: a. american heart association, 2015. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation Therapy (ECMO)
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: extracorporeal membrane oxygenation therapy (ecmo) guideline number: guid-3203-c008 issue date: 6/4/2021 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ecmo) therapy is an invasive strategy for treatment of severe pulmonary and/or cardiac disease utilizing a "heart-lung" bypass device to allow for gas exchange. patients requiring ecmo are critically ill and resource intensive, requiring specialized care and equipment. patients are cannulated by surgeons and usually managed by perfusionists who have expertise in the device. venous-arterial (va) ecmo "bypasses" both the heart and lungs, and is used for severe cardiogenic shock, venous-venous (vv) ecmo "bypasses" the lungs only and is used for acute respiratory distress syndrome, massive pulmonary embolism, pulmonary contusions, and other issues. ii. definitions: a. ecmo - extracorporeal membrane oxygenation iii. department guideline: a. equipment: i. cardiohelp console (~22 lbs.) ii. two ecmo circuits (~10 lbs.) - one for primary, one for backup. (if patient is already on cardiohelp console, we will only bring one as a backup) iii. transport backup (~15 lbs.) - contains tubing, connectors, clamps, iv lines, prep sticks etc. b. indications: i. acute respiratory or cardiovascular deterioration unresponsive to therapy 1. rarely, this includes prolonged resuscitation during cardiac arrest c. procedure: i. establish care defined by guid3203-g002 - general patient care. ii. ecmo specialist (perfusionist or trained rn) 1. primary responsibility prior to transport: ensure all equipment and blood products on checklist are available, functional, and loaded at time of departure 2. primary responsibility during transport: management of ecmo circuit during all phases of transport iii. medical flight crew page 1 of 3 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: extracorporeal membrane oxygenation therapy (ecmo) guideline number: guid-3203-c008 1. patient assessment a. assess extremity blood flow b. assess all lines, tubes, and catheters 2. primary responsibility during transport: non-circuit related clinical care 3. administration of medications, fluids, and blood products per guid3203-g002 management of previously initiated and continuous iv medication, guid3203- g005 analgesia and sedation management, guid3203-g007 propofol (diprivan), guid3203-pr025 emergency blood product administration, and guid3203-pr026 warming of blood products and intravenous fluids a. consider changing sedation and pain medication to less protein-bound and lipophilic drugs (i.e., ativan and diluadid if available). 4. ventilator set-up, management and troubleshooting per guid3203-pr024 ventilation with a mechanical ventilator a. if patient is being administered aerosolized reproterol (flolan) and the appropriate equipment is available, continue at current dosing in consultation with sending/admitting physician or medical control 5. if present, iabp set-up, management and troubleshooting per guid3203-pr023 intra-aortic balloon pump. 6. assist with monitoring and blood gas analysis 7. treat hypotension per guid3203-m011 - hypotension in consultation with perfusionist and accepting physician. d. trouble shooting i. ecmo system trouble shooting performed by ecmo perfusionist ii. ventilator, monitor, iv pumps and any additional equipment troubleshooting performed by medical flight crew. iv. documentation: ems charts a. q5 minute vitals i. heart rate, blood pressure (arterial or nibp), etco2, spo2, and respiratory rate b. ecmo system values charted at: i. initiation of care ii. transfer of care iii. change in patient condition 1. blood flow rate, pump speed, circuit speed, temperature c. ecmo patient specific assessment charting. i. ecmo catheter location and appearance page 2 of 3 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: extracorporeal membrane oxygenation therapy (ecmo) guideline number: guid-3203-c008 ii. additional monitor lines iii. iv access lines v. references: a. extracorporeal life support organization (elso) guidelines for ecmo transport b. orlando health ecmo criteria guideline guid-1620-0900 page 3 of 3 © orlando health 2017

title: Targeted Temperature Management
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: targeted temperature management guideline number: guid-3203-c009 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: induced therapeutic hypothermia (post rosc) guid-3203-c009 revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to provide neuroprotection for adult, non-traumatic, non-hemorrhagic, post-cardiac arrest patients once return of spontaneous circulation is achieved. target temperature management has been demonstrated to improve outcomes in certain patients after resuscitation from cardiac arrest, but early initiation of this treatment has not proven beneficial. consult with accepting physician prior to administering this treatment. ii. definations: a. vf - ventricular fibrillation b. vt - ventricular tachycardia c. pea - pulseless electrical activity d. cpr - cardio-pulmonary resuscitation e. acls - advanced cardiac life support f. gcs - glasgow coma scale iii. department guideline: a. continue strict temperature management initiated by sending physician (the temperature goal is 33°c). b. inclusion criteria (requires all four components) 1. medical cardiac arrest : vf, pulseless vt, pea, & asystole. 2. downtime (5-20 minutes): downtime is defined as: the time from the onset of cardiac arrest to the initiation of acls. bystander cpr is not included. 3. comatose patients (gcs < 9): patients who do not respond appropriately to verbal commands after return of spontaneous circulation. agitated and combative patients are comatose by this definition and should be cooled. 4. patients greater than the age 18 are eligible. c. exclusion criteria (any of the following) 1. cpr for more than 45 minutes. 2. un-witnessed asystole or pea, refractory cardiogenic or septic shock despite intravenous fluids and vasopressors. 3. severe coagulopathy (with clinical evidence of bleeding). page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: targeted temperature management guideline number: guid-3203-c009 4.other causes of coma. 5. known or suspected pregnancy (consider target temp of 36 degrees). 6. do not resuscitate (dnr) status. 7. other acute brain injury including traumatic brain injury, acute stroke, overdose, or status epilepticus. 8. acute major trauma with significant bleeding due to concerns of coagulopathy. 9. initial assessment temperature < 32°c. d. ensure patient is intubated and on mechanical ventilation if possible. sao2 goal 94-96%. keep hob > 30°. e. obtain and document a complete neurological exam to include gcs, pupillary size, and response to light, and oculocephalic reflex (dolls eyes). f. consider analgesia & sedation management per guid3203-g005 analgesia and sedation management or guid3203-g008 propofol infusion to maintain richmond agitation and sedation scale (rass) scale -4. g. monitor temperature by one of the following measures: rectal, foley catheter, or esophageal monitoring device. document temperature every 15 minutes. h. obtain or verify recent blood glucose level. i. if patient begins to shiver, first ensure adequate sedation. 1. consider rocuronium 50 mg iv/io for paralysis. 2. wrap head, hands, and feet with towels or blankets. j. if hypotension persists, consider using vasopressors per guid3203-m011 hypotension. iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: a. american heart association, 2015. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: ST Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI)
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: st elevation myocardial infarction (stemi) guideline number: guid-3203-c010 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to improve the health and stability of the patient with an acute st elevation myocardial infarction through timely recognition of specific electrocardiogram (ecg) abnormalities and early therapeutic intervention to reduce the overall extent of cardiac injury. ii. definitions: a. stemi - st-elevation myocardial infarction iii. department guideline: a. provide supplemental oxygen to maintain saturation > 93%. b. initiate cardiac monitoring, pulse oximetry, and vital signs every 5 minutes. c. expedited transport to the nearest percutaneous coronary intervention (pci) capable hospital is of the utmost importance, do not delay transfer d. obtain/evaluate a 12 lead ecg and interpret the findings. assess for dysrhythmias and treat accordingly. e. ensure administration of aspirin 324 mg po (chewable) unless contraindicated by a true allergy. f. establish iv access (consider 2 sites). g. if hypotension is present, and there is no evidence of pulmonary edema, consider 250 ml fluid bolus (0.9 % normal saline or lactated ringers). repeat as necessary up to 500 ml. h. if symptoms persist, consider administering nitroglycerine (ntg) 0.4 mg sl every 5 minutes x 3 doses if hemodynamically stable. use caution in inferior wall mi or right ventricular infarct in v4r. i. if symptoms are relieved by sl ntg or if pain persists, consider initiating a nitroglycerin infusion at 10 mcg/min, titrate until pain free, keeping map > 65 mmhg or sbp > 100 mmhg. do not exceed 200 mcg/min. ii. ntg is contraindicated if patient has received drugs for erectile dysfunction such as sildenafil (viagra) or vardenafil (levitra) within the past 24 hours and 72 hours for tadalafil (cialis). (consult medical direction). i. for additional management for severe pain consider i. morphine 0.1 mg/kg slow iv/io may repeat every 10 minutes prn or ii. fentanyl 0.5-2 mcg/kg slow iv/io may repeat every 10 minutes prn page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: st elevation myocardial infarction (stemi) guideline number: guid-3203-c010 iii. monitor respiratory status and consider etco2 monitoring. j. for anxiety management ensure adequate oxygenation then see guid3203-g006 management for the anxiety/combative patient. k. for cocaine induced tachycardia, consider midazolam 0.05-0.1 mg/kg iv/io, may repeat every 3-5 minutes prn, maximum dose 5 mg l. if hypotension persists, consider using vasopressors per guid3203-m011 hypotension. m. for significant tachyarrhythmias, refer to guid3203-c006 narrow and wide complex tachycardia. n. if thrombolytic therapy has been initiated, continue infusion per referring physician's protocol. monitor closely for bleeding. o. if the patient has not received low molecular weight heparin during the previous 12 hours and heparin therapy has not been initiated, consider administration of heparin after consulting with md. i. heparin bolus of 60 units/kg (max 5,000 units) followed by heparin infusion at 18 units/kg (max 1,000 units per hour). p. if glycoprotein iib/iiia (integrilin) infusion has been initiated continue infusion per referring physician's protocol. q. document administration of other antiplatelet agents which may include i. ticagrelor (brilinta) 180 mg po/ng ii. prasugrel (effient) 60 mg po/ng iii. clopidogrel 600 mg po/ng in patients with high-risk bleeding or if others cannot be safely administered. r. if patient is not symptomatic, on low gtt doses of nitroglycerine or heparin, and flight time is < 10 minutes, consider discontinuing medications in favor of decreasing scene time if destination is a cardiac cath lab with interventional capabilities. iv. documentation: a. ems charts b. patient time of arrival to sending facility c. time of stemi on referring ekg d. thrombolytic therapy: amount infused prior to arrival and amount infused during transport. v. references: a. american heart association, 2015. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

pages.text: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: st elevation myocardial infarction (stemi) guideline number: guid-3203-c010 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to improve the health and stability of the patient with an acute st elevation myocardial infarction through timely recognition of specific electrocardiogram (ecg) abnormalities and early therapeutic intervention to reduce the overall extent of cardiac injury. ii. definitions: a. stemi - st-elevation myocardial infarction iii. department guideline: a. provide supplemental oxygen to maintain saturation > 93%. b. initiate cardiac monitoring, pulse oximetry, and vital signs every 5 minutes. c. expedited transport to the nearest percutaneous coronary intervention (pci) capable hospital is of the utmost importance, do not delay transfer d. obtain/evaluate a 12 lead ecg and interpret the findings. assess for dysrhythmias and treat accordingly. e. ensure administration of aspirin 324 mg po (chewable) unless contraindicated by a true allergy. f. establish iv access (consider 2 sites). g. if hypotension is present, and there is no evidence of pulmonary edema, consider 250 ml fluid bolus (0.9 % normal saline or lactated ringers). repeat as necessary up to 500 ml. h. if symptoms persist, consider administering nitroglycerine (ntg) 0.4 mg sl every 5 minutes x 3 doses if hemodynamically stable. use caution in inferior wall mi or right ventricular infarct in v4r. i. if symptoms are relieved by sl ntg or if pain persists, consider initiating a nitroglycerin infusion at 10 mcg/min, titrate until pain free, keeping map > 65 mmhg or sbp > 100 mmhg. do not exceed 200 mcg/min. ii. ntg is contraindicated if patient has received drugs for erectile dysfunction such as sildenafil (viagra) or vardenafil (levitra) within the past 24 hours and 72 hours for tadalafil (cialis). (consult medical direction). i. for additional management for severe pain consider i. morphine 0.1 mg/kg slow iv/io may repeat every 10 minutes prn or ii. fentanyl 0.5-2 mcg/kg slow iv/io may repeat every 10 minutes prn page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017

1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: st elevation myocardial infarction (stemi) guideline number: guid-3203-c010 iii. monitor respiratory status and consider etco2 monitoring. j. for anxiety management ensure adequate oxygenation then see guid3203-g006 management for the anxiety/combative patient. k. for cocaine induced tachycardia, consider midazolam 0.05-0.1 mg/kg iv/io, may repeat every 3-5 minutes prn, maximum dose 5 mg l. if hypotension persists, consider using vasopressors per guid3203-m011 hypotension. m. for significant tachyarrhythmias, refer to guid3203-c006 narrow and wide complex tachycardia. n. if thrombolytic therapy has been initiated, continue infusion per referring physician's protocol. monitor closely for bleeding. o. if the patient has not received low molecular weight heparin during the previous 12 hours and heparin therapy has not been initiated, consider administration of heparin after consulting with md. i. heparin bolus of 60 units/kg (max 5,000 units) followed by heparin infusion at 18 units/kg (max 1,000 units per hour). p. if glycoprotein iib/iiia (integrilin) infusion has been initiated continue infusion per referring physician's protocol. q. document administration of other antiplatelet agents which may include i. ticagrelor (brilinta) 180 mg po/ng ii. prasugrel (effient) 60 mg po/ng iii. clopidogrel 600 mg po/ng in patients with high-risk bleeding or if others cannot be safely administered. r. if patient is not symptomatic, on low gtt doses of nitroglycerine or heparin, and flight time is < 10 minutes, consider discontinuing medications in favor of decreasing scene time if destination is a cardiac cath lab with interventional capabilities. iv. documentation: a. ems charts b. patient time of arrival to sending facility c. time of stemi on referring ekg d. thrombolytic therapy: amount infused prior to arrival and amount infused during transport. v. references: a. american heart association, 2015. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Flight Physiology & Patient Oxygenation
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: flight physiology & patient oxygenation guideline number: guid-3203-g001 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: the effects of altitude on patients will be minimized during air transport. patient oxygenation will be maintained during flight altitude. ii. definitions: a. iv - intravenous iii. department guideline: a. all medical transport team members should be aware of the 8 stressors of flight. these not only affect the patient, but all members of the flight team as well. i. decreased partial pressure of oxygen ii. decreased barometric pressure iii. thermal changes iv. decreased humidity v. noise vi. vibration vii. fatigue viii. g-forces b. all patients transported shall receive continuous pulse oximetry. c. supplemental oxygen shall be administered to all patients transported utilizing an appropriate delivery device and liter flow rate to maintain saturations > 92%. d. calculate the amount of oxygen required for every transport. e. infusion rates for intravenous fluids being infused via free-flow method may be affected by changes in altitude. all vasoactive medication infusions will be maintained via iv pumps. free- flowing infusions will be monitored closely to ensure appropriate rates are maintained. f. invasive line considerations: any air bubbles which exist within the intravenous/intra-arterial line systems will have increased volume at higher altitudes which could result in an increased probability of significant air embolus. g. do not occlude gastric tubes except for brief periods of time. allow equilibration of intra-gastric pressures with prevalent atmospheric pressure. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: flight physiology & patient oxygenation guideline number: guid-3203-g001 h. along with routine monitoring of vital signs, all tubes and lines should be re-assessed every fifteen minutes at minimum. i. general assessment of changes associated with flight: i. monitor patients for hypoxia. ii. monitor patients for the following: 1. barotitis media-trapped gas in the middle ear (ear pain). 2. barosinusitis-trapped gases in the sinuses (sinus pain). 3. barodentalgia-trapped gases in air pockets of the teeth (tooth pain). 4. gastrointestinal gas expansion. 5. pneumothoracies. iii. monitor patients for air trapped in medical devices: 1. air in iv lines. 2. air in iv bags (causing fluids to run faster if not on an infusion pump). 3. air in endotracheal tube cuffs, balloon pump catheters, & pulmonary artery catheters. iv. monitor patients for temperature changes and decreased humidity. 1. decreased humidity can cause mucous plugging, especially in neonates and pediatric patients who have small endotracheal tubes iv. references: none page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: General Patient Care
normalizedText: guideline title: general patient care guideline number: guid-3203-g002 page 1 of 3 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: regardless of the nature of the illness or injury, the following minimum patient care guidelines will be provided to all patients that are treated and transported by the air care team. ii. department guideline: a. all air care team aircraft will be staffed with a minimum of one registered nurse and one licensed paramedic. b. standard precautions and personal protective equipment (ppe) will be utilized for all patient care. medical crew will wear additional ppe according to other organizational or department standards if patient is on contact, droplet, or airborne precautions. c. consent for patient transport must be obtained by the patient and the authorization and consent form signed unless the patient is mentally or physically unable to sign. i. acceptable reasons for the patient not to sign include unconscious or unresponsive, cardiac/respiratory arrest, altered mental status, intellectual disability, physical disability that prohibits signing, a minor, a prisoner/inmate in custody, has a legal guardian appointed (not power of attorney unless meets criteria), or involuntary psychiatric transfer. d. an initial assessment will be performed on all patients to identify abnormalities and/or injuries. e. all patients will be connected to the cardiac monitor, pulse oximeter, and blood pressure monitor as soon as practical and remain on these monitoring devices until care is turned over at the receiving care facility. f. the following will be monitored and recorded: heart rate, blood pressure (non-invasive or invasive), pulse oximetry, end-tidal carbon dioxide (etco2, if indicated), and respiratory rate a minimum of every 10 minutes for inter-facility flights and every 5 minutes for scene flights. the frequency of monitoring vital signs will be advanced as determined by patient's condition. g. supplemental oxygen will be placed on the patient as appropriate to their condition prior to flight for those patients not currently on oxygen to maintain oxygen saturations > 92%. h. patients administered parenteral narcotics or sedative agents must have continuous cardiac and spo2 monitoring. end tidal co2 monitoring should be used all non-intubated patients with a gcs < 14 who receive sedation or analgesia. guideline title: general patient care guideline number: guid-3203-g002 page 2 of 3 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline i. all patients shall have a patent airway prior to transport. patients who are unable to protect their own airway should have their airway secured. i. ecg, pulse oximetry, etco2 monitoring is mandatory for all advanced airway procedures to include intubation, supraglottic airway, or cricothyrotomy. 1. intubated patients will have their tube placement confirmed prior to departure from the scene or facility by confirmation of etco2 detection, either qualitative or quantitative. 2. continuous etco2 monitoring is mandatory on all intubated patients. 3. intubated patients ideally should be ventilated utilizing the transport ventilator. in some circumstances, such as very short flight time or patient inability to tolerate mechanical ventilation, ventilations via bag-valve would be an acceptable alternative. 4. intubated patients should have a gastric tube placed if not contraindicated and if time permits. j. establish nibp monitoring except for patients with invasive monitoring. establish invasive pressure monitoring on all patients with arterial lines. k. intravenous (iv) or intraosseous (io) access is to be considered mandatory unless the attempts at access delay essential care or if it is inappropriate for the patient's condition (e.g., epiglottitis). if unable to gain iv access, consider io access for those critically ill patients where access is mandatory. i. all iv/io devices will be checked for patency prior to departure and throughout transport from the scene or sending facility. l. trauma patients and patients with potential for hypotension ideally will have two large bore iv lines. m. all iv medications hung prior to and during transport will be properly labeled with the time the drug was started, the name of the drug, the dosage, and the concentration of the solution. n. all iv infusions will be regulated with the use of an iv infusion pump. the only exceptions will be the use of maintenance iv fluids without additives (for adults only) and the use of an iv for rapid fluid resuscitation and blood replacement. o. invasive lines will be transduced and monitored as appropriate. p. all pediatric patients will have their weights verified by using a length-based resuscitation tape. q. all patients with altered mental status will have a blood glucose evaluation unless a blood glucose level from the referring facility is normal. r. all pediatric patients will have a blood glucose done unless a blood glucose level from the referring facility is normal. s. all patients will be assessed for pain. t. all pediatric and neonatal patients will have temperature documentation. u. for trauma situations, a pediatric patient is defined as a person fifteen (15) years or age or younger. v. all patients will be monitored for comfort and warmth. guideline title: general patient care guideline number: guid-3203-g002 page 3 of 3 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline w. all medication administration will be performed in compliance with orlando health patient care policy and procedures, including double verification of high-risk substances and observation of the "5 r's" (right drug, right patient, right route, right time, right documentation) x. all patients will be reassessed after each intervention and medication administration and their response will be documented. y. all patients will be secured to the aircraft stretcher with 3 straps and shoulder harness and if necessary, will be chemically or physically restrained for safety of patient and flight team. the neonatal transport team will secure the infant in their isolette. z. all patients will be provided hearing protection. aa. all equipment shall be secured before takeoff. bb. the team will ensure patient/family receives information regarding transport. cc. the team will ensure patient privacy is respected. dd. the team will ensure patient information is kept confidential. ee. the team is responsible for transporting complete patient records including original or copies of patient radiology scans with the patient as received by sending nurse/institution. if results are pending, the medical transport team should request that they be faxed to the receiving facility. ff. if transferring a pediatric patient to the pediatric intensive care unit, an updated report should be called to the receiving physician prior to departure from the unit of the sending facility. gg. the team will contact the emergency department (ed) via radio with a patient report and estimated time of arrival for all patient's arriving from a scene call or for interfacility transfers to the ed at least 5 minutes prior to arrival. hh. orders provided by the physician should be followed unless, in the judgment of the team, they endanger the patient. if a receiving physician gives orders to perform a procedure that is not covered in the air care medical treatment protocols but is within the scope of practice of a registered nurse or paramedic and the individual has been trained to perform said procedure, perform the procedure in accordance with standards set for the level of certification. to request any other orders not covered in the air care medical treatment protocols, or clarify any clinical concerns call medical control (dr. christopher hunter, 914-552-8954). ii. the team will provide complete a complete report and provide a completed air care team handoff sheet to the receiving nurse. jj. a patient care record will be completed following each patient flight in the current department electronic health record. iii. documentation: iv. references: none

title: Management of Previously Initiated & Continuous IV Medication
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: management of previously initiated & continuous iv medication guideline number: guid-3203-g003 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to maximize the quality of care delivered to patients during transport. ii. department guideline: a. determine the recent, current, or anticipated use of iv medications. the team should evaluate the medications the patient is receiving and determine the reasons the referring physician is utilizing the medication. identify any parameters that the physician has been utilizing in the use of these medications including pain scores, blood pressure, heart rates or sedation levels. b. consider continuing the medication as per the referring physician's guidelines unless contra- indicated by changes in the patient condition, discovery of adverse reactions, or other contraindications. the transport team should evaluate whether the medication is being given at the correct dose and route. verify concentration, drip rates, and dosage of all previously mixed continuous medications prior to transport. c. consider discontinuing the medication if they identity potential or actual problems as identified in the above section. d. contact medical control for consultation at any time to review the medication regimes in question. iii. documentation: a. ems charts iv. references: none page 1 of 1 © orlando health 2017

title: Nausea/Vomiting/Motion Sickness
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: nausea / vomiting / motion sickness guideline number: guid-3203-g004 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: identification of patient prone to motion sickness and to provide treatment and comfort to control nausea. ii. definitions: none iii. department guideline: a. always have suction equipment immediately available. be prepared to place patient in the recovery position if illness/injury and size permits. b. provide motion sickness bag to patients if needed. c. for patients who are known to be sensitive to motion, consider administering medication prior to transport. d. for patients nauseated or vomiting during transport, the following medications may be considered i. adults: ondansetron 4 mg iv/io/im. repeat once if needed. ii. pediatrics: ondansetron 0.1 mg/kg iv/io (maximum dose 4 mg) iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: none page 1 of 1 © orlando health 2017

pages.text: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: nausea / vomiting / motion sickness guideline number: guid-3203-g004 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: identification of patient prone to motion sickness and to provide treatment and comfort to control nausea. ii. definitions: none iii. department guideline: a. always have suction equipment immediately available. be prepared to place patient in the recovery position if illness/injury and size permits. b. provide motion sickness bag to patients if needed. c. for patients who are known to be sensitive to motion, consider administering medication prior to transport. d. for patients nauseated or vomiting during transport, the following medications may be considered i. adults: ondansetron 4 mg iv/io/im. repeat once if needed. ii. pediatrics: ondansetron 0.1 mg/kg iv/io (maximum dose 4 mg) iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: none page 1 of 1 © orlando health 2017

title: Analgesia and Sedation Management
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: analgesia and sedation management guideline number: guid-3203-g005 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 2/7/2017, 6/8/2021 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to provide adequate pain relief to patients in a safe and compassionate manner. ii. definitions: none iii. department guideline: a. assess patient for pain using an appropriate pain scale based on patient's clinical condition and age: 1 (minimal) to 10 (severe) objective number scale for adults, adult nonverbal pain scale (nvps), wong baker faces for pediatric, flaac (face, legs, activity, cry, consolability scale) for infants. b. attempt suitable non-pharmacological interventions when possible, prior to the administration of medications. c. use caution with all pharmacological agents as they may cause respiratory depression and hypotension. d. consider etco2 monitoring and record readings. i. end tidal co2 monitoring must be used on all intubated patients. ii. end tidal co2 monitoring should be used all non-intubated patients with a gcs < 14 who receive sedation or analgesia. e. monitor blood glucose levels. f. ensure reversal agents are available to the administration of opiate medications i. naloxone: 1. adults: 0.4-2 mg iv/io/im/sq or 2 mg in (divided equally between nostrils). repeat as needed every 3 minutes 2. pediatrics: a. < 5 yo or < 20 kg: 0.1 mg/kg iv/io/im/sq/in q2-3 min prn. maximum single dose 2 mg. repeat as needed every 3 minutes b. > 5 yrs. or > 20 kg: 2 mg iv/io/im/sq/in (divide in doses equally between nostrils). repeat as needed every 3 minutes page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: analgesia and sedation management guideline number: guid-3203-g005 g. consider intranasal administration in patients with seizures, burns, pediatric patients, or those with a history of autism. h. consider the following medications for analgesia management: i. fentanyl 0.5-2 mcg/kg slow iv/io, may repeat every 10 minutes ii. fentanyl intra-nasal (in) 1. adults: 100 mcg in. administer half dose in each nostril. 2. peds: 1.5 mcg/kg in. administer half dose in each nostril. maximum dose 100 mcg. iii. morphine 1. adults: 0.1 mg/kg slow iv/io, may repeat every 10 minutes prn 2. peds: 0.05-0.2 mg/kg slow iv/io, may repeat every 10 minutes prn iv. ketamine 1. adults: 0.1-0.3 mg/kg slow iv/io, may repeat every 10 minutes prn, maximum total dose of 50 mg 2. peds: 0.1-0.3 mg/kg slow iv/io, may repeat every 10 minutes prn, maximum total dose of 25 mg i. consider the following medications for sedation management: i. midazolam 0.05-0.1 mg/kg iv/io, may repeat every 3-5 minutes prn, maximum dose 5 mg ii. midazolam intranasal 1. adults: 5 mg in. administer half dose in each nostril. 2. peds: 0.2 mg/kg. administer half dose in each nostril. maximum dose 5 mg. iii. ketamine 1. adults: 1-2 mg/kg slow iv/io. may repeat every 10 minutes prn. maximum total dose 200 mg. 2. peds: 1-2 mg/kg slow iv/io, may repeat every 10 minutes prn. maximum total dose of 200 mg. 3. monitor for tachydysrhythmias 4. consider midazolam 0.05-0.1 mg/kg iv for signs of emergence reaction (agitation). iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

pages.text: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: analgesia and sedation management guideline number: guid-3203-g005 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 2/7/2017, 6/8/2021 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to provide adequate pain relief to patients in a safe and compassionate manner. ii. definitions: none iii. department guideline: a. assess patient for pain using an appropriate pain scale based on patient's clinical condition and age: 1 (minimal) to 10 (severe) objective number scale for adults, adult nonverbal pain scale (nvps), wong baker faces for pediatric, flaac (face, legs, activity, cry, consolability scale) for infants. b. attempt suitable non-pharmacological interventions when possible, prior to the administration of medications. c. use caution with all pharmacological agents as they may cause respiratory depression and hypotension. d. consider etco2 monitoring and record readings. i. end tidal co2 monitoring must be used on all intubated patients. ii. end tidal co2 monitoring should be used all non-intubated patients with a gcs < 14 who receive sedation or analgesia. e. monitor blood glucose levels. f. ensure reversal agents are available to the administration of opiate medications i. naloxone: 1. adults: 0.4-2 mg iv/io/im/sq or 2 mg in (divided equally between nostrils). repeat as needed every 3 minutes 2. pediatrics: a. < 5 yo or < 20 kg: 0.1 mg/kg iv/io/im/sq/in q2-3 min prn. maximum single dose 2 mg. repeat as needed every 3 minutes b. > 5 yrs. or > 20 kg: 2 mg iv/io/im/sq/in (divide in doses equally between nostrils). repeat as needed every 3 minutes page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017

1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: analgesia and sedation management guideline number: guid-3203-g005 g. consider intranasal administration in patients with seizures, burns, pediatric patients, or those with a history of autism. h. consider the following medications for analgesia management: i. fentanyl 0.5-2 mcg/kg slow iv/io, may repeat every 10 minutes ii. fentanyl intra-nasal (in) 1. adults: 100 mcg in. administer half dose in each nostril. 2. peds: 1.5 mcg/kg in. administer half dose in each nostril. maximum dose 100 mcg. iii. morphine 1. adults: 0.1 mg/kg slow iv/io, may repeat every 10 minutes prn 2. peds: 0.05-0.2 mg/kg slow iv/io, may repeat every 10 minutes prn iv. ketamine 1. adults: 0.1-0.3 mg/kg slow iv/io, may repeat every 10 minutes prn, maximum total dose of 50 mg 2. peds: 0.1-0.3 mg/kg slow iv/io, may repeat every 10 minutes prn, maximum total dose of 25 mg i. consider the following medications for sedation management: i. midazolam 0.05-0.1 mg/kg iv/io, may repeat every 3-5 minutes prn, maximum dose 5 mg ii. midazolam intranasal 1. adults: 5 mg in. administer half dose in each nostril. 2. peds: 0.2 mg/kg. administer half dose in each nostril. maximum dose 5 mg. iii. ketamine 1. adults: 1-2 mg/kg slow iv/io. may repeat every 10 minutes prn. maximum total dose 200 mg. 2. peds: 1-2 mg/kg slow iv/io, may repeat every 10 minutes prn. maximum total dose of 200 mg. 3. monitor for tachydysrhythmias 4. consider midazolam 0.05-0.1 mg/kg iv for signs of emergence reaction (agitation). iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Anxiety/Combative Patient Management
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: anxiety/combative patient management guideline number: guid-3203-g006 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 2/7/2017, 6/8/2021 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to relieve anxiety, maximize patient care, and ensure safe transport of patient and transport team. ii. definitions: iii. department guideline: a. attempt verbal reassurance. b. apply appropriate extremity restraints to any patient observed to be agitated or with the potential to become agitated per guid3203-pr021 - restraints. c. apply & record end-tidal co2 (etco2) monitoring on all patients requiring chemical/physical restraint. d. use the minimal number of physical and chemical restraints necessary to safely transport the patient. e. monitor for tachydysrhythmias, airway competence, oxygenation, and ventilation. f. if patient does not respond to verbal reassurance, the following medications can be considered: i. midazolam 0.05-0.1 mg/kg iv/io, may repeat every 3-5 minutes prn, maximum dose 5 mg ii. midazolam intranasal 1. adults: 5 mg in. administer half dose in each nostril. 2. peds: 0.2 mg/kg. administer half dose in each nostril. maximum dose 5 mg. iii. haloperidol 5 mg iv/im ~ or ~ iv. ketamine 1. adults: 1 mg/kg slow iv/io. may repeat every 10 minutes prn. maximum total dose 200 mg. 2. peds: 1 mg/kg slow iv/io, may repeat every 10 minutes prn. maximum total dose of 200 mg. 3. monitor for tachydysrhythmias page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: anxiety/combative patient management guideline number: guid-3203-g006 4. consider midazolam 0.05-0.1 mg/kg iv for signs of emergence reaction (agitation). iv. documentation: a. ems charts b. documentation of least invasive measure before drug administration v. references: a. lin j, figuerado y, montgomery a, et al., efficacy of ketamine for initial control of acute agitation in the emergency department: a randomized study. am j emerg med 2021 44:306-11. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Propofol (Diprivan)
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: propofol (diprivan) guideline number: guid-3203-g007 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: this policy provides a guideline for the regulation and management of propofol (diprivan) during air care team transports. ii. definitions: a. mcp (medical command physician) - accepting physician of record or air care medical director b. pediatric- less than 16 years old c. sbp- systolic blood pressure iii. department guideline: a. propofol infusions can only be administered by trained rns to patients that are intubated and have continuous ekg, blood pressure monitoring, etco2 monitoring, and pulse oximetry in place. b. if initiating infusion i. propofol 10 mcg/kg/min iv, titrate by 10 mcg/kg/min q5 min to achieve a sedation-agitation scale value of 2-3 (see attached chart below). maximum infusion rate 5-50 mcg/kg/min. 1. any rate higher than 50 mcg/kg/min must be approved by an mcp c. if continuing an existing infusion (for example, inter-facility transfer) i. continue infusion at encountered rate (even if it is above 50 mcg/kg/min) as long as patient is sedated and sbp > 90 mmhg ii. for adult patients, the rate may be titrated up to 50 mcg/kg/min as needed to achieve sedation-agitation scale value of 2-3. iii. for pediatric patients, propofol doses cannot be increased from the encountered rate without an mcp order. pediatric doses encountered may be 100-150 mcg/kg/min. d. if sbp < 90 mmhg, reduce drip slowly allowing blood pressure to stabilize prior to readjusting again. e. if patient remains hypotensive, despite multiple decreases in drip rate, discontinue propofol drip and notify referring physician of continued hypotension. attempt to maintain needed sedation by administering other sedation medications per guid3203-g006 - analgesia and sedation management. if blood pressure dose not increase with a decrease in rate, assess for other causes of hypotension and treat per guid3203-m001 - hypotension. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: propofol (diprivan) guideline number: guid-3203-g007 f. if patient becomes agitated and sbp > 90 mmhg, titrate by 10mcg/kg/min increments, up to 50 mcg/kg/min, monitoring bp after each adjustment. g. if adult or pediatric pt remains agitated on 50 mcg/kg/min and sbp > 90 mmhg, administer fentanyl 0.5-2 mcg/kg slow iv/io, may repeat every 10 minutes h. consider administering rocuronium 0.6-1 mg/kg iv to facilitate oxygenation and ventilation in appropriately sedated (sas ≤ 3) patients with increased airway pressures or poor ventilator tolerance. i. utilize referring physician or mcp for guidance especially in the care of pediatric patients. sedation-agitation scale (sas) score level of sedation-agitation response 7 dangerous agitation pulling at endotracheal tube, thrashing, climbing over bed rails. 6 very agitated does not calm, requires restraints, and bites endotracheal tube. 5 agitated attempts to sit up but calms to verbal instructions. 4 calm and cooperative obeys commands. 3 sedated difficult to rouse, obeys simple commands. 2 very sedated rouses to stimuli. does not obey commands. 1 unarousable minimal or no response to noxious stimuli. iv. documentation: a. ems charts patient documentation software b. physicians order sheet v. references: a. propofol infusion protocol for neurotrauma patients >16 years of age form #5872-100883 b. orlando health continuous infusion standard dilutions and dosing guidelines page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

analgesia and sedation management. if blood pressure dose not increase with a decrease in rate, assess for other causes of hypotension and treat per guid3203-m001 - hypotension. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017
