title: Rapid Sequence Induction (RSI) for Endotracheal (ET) Intubation
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: rapid sequence induction (rsi) for endotracheal (et) intubation guideline number: guid-3203-g008 issue date: 6/1/2013 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 09/09/2014, 2/7/2017, 6/8/2021, 8/6/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: rapid sequence intubation (rsi) is the preferred approach to endotracheal intubation for any patient with a gag reflex or muscle tone. special care should be taken to evaluate the indication for rsi and the potential for difficulties securing the airway. a bag-valve mask hooked to oxygen, a gum elastic bougie or endotracheal tube introducer, and a supraglottic airway device must always be kept easily accessible to the provider intubating the patient for rescue purposes. video laryngoscopy should be used for all endotracheal intubations unless it is not operationally feasible. ii. definitions: a. act- air care team b. bvm- bag valve mask c. ett- endotracheal tube d. gcs - glasgow coma scale e. rsi- rapid sequence intubation f. sbp - systolic blood pressure iii. department guideline: a. patient selection i. the following patients shall be intubated using rsi: 1. actual or potential airway compromise due to depressed sensorium (gcs of 8 or less) or whose combativeness threatens the airway, spinal cord stability, or transport safety 2. patients who demonstrate a high probability of airway compromise during transport (i.e. smoke inhalation, severe facial trauma with bleeding) 3. patients who need ventilator assistance or airway protection ii. the following patient should not be intubated using rsi: 1. patients that cannot be manually ventilated 2. any patient who is maintaining a patent airway or has spontaneous respirations of at least 12 breaths per minute iii. the following patients should not be given succinylcholine for intubation: 1. penetrating eye injuries or history of glaucoma page 1 of 4 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: rapid sequence induction (rsi) for endotracheal (et) intubation guideline number: guid-3203-g008 2. neurological disease (myasthenia gravis or multiple sclerosis) 3. severe burn or trauma greater than 48 hours old 4. history of malignant hyperthermia 5. history of renal failure iv. prior to rsi administration, careful consideration needs to be given to any patient where there is a concern for a difficult airway v. induction agents may lower blood pressure. ensure you are resuscitating the patient to maintain perfusion and oxygenation throughout the procedure. b. procedure i. gather equipment and prepare patient per guid3203-pr020 - endotracheal intubation i. pre-oxygenate with high flow oxygen by mask. if available, simultaneously oxygenate with nasal cannula. do not manually ventilate the patient unless respiratory effort is ineffective, as this may cause gastric distention with vomiting and aspiration ii. ensure patient is connected to cardiac monitoring. obtain bp, hr, and oxygen saturation iii. pre-medicate pediatric patients < 5 years of age who are not markedly tachycardic (> 160 bpm) with atropine 0.02 mg/kg iv, maximum dose 0.4 mg, to prevent bradycardia associated with laryngoscopy. iv. push dose pressor (pdp, for adult patients only): if the patient has a systolic blood pressure less than 90 mmhg after 250ml-500ml fluid bolus, administer phenylephrine 200 mcg iv push prior to administering induction and paralytic agents. 1. phenylephrine is contraindicated if the heart rate is < 60 bpm 2. may repeat every two minutes if the sbp remains less than 90 mmhg on repeat vital signs 3. pdps provide a transient improvement in pressure meant to be a bridge to safely perform rsi and continue resuscitation, they may only be used for documented hypotension 5 minutes before to 5 minutes after the rsi procedure. a. for persistent hypotension more than 5 minutes after rsi procedure, treat per guid3203-m011 hypotension or guid3203-pr025a/b emergency blood product administration 4. pdps may only be administered from approved prefilled syringes obtained from pharmacy - it is prohibited for any rn or paramedic to mix their own pdp v. rsi requires both an induction agent and a paralytic agent be given in sequence as below. do not administer paralytic agents for intubation to a patient that has not been given an induction medication first. patients with a gcs of 3 or in cardiac arrest may be intubated without induction or paralytic medication. vi. for induction of sedation, administer either: 1. etomidate 0.3 mg/kg iv/io **or** 2. ketamine 1-4.5 mg/kg (start at 2mg/kg for adults) iv/io. consider when hypotension is present. page 2 of 4 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: rapid sequence induction (rsi) for endotracheal (et) intubation guideline number: guid-3203-g008 vii. next, for muscle paralysis prior to intubation administer either: 1. adults: succinylcholine 1-1.5 mg/kg iv/io pediatrics: succinylcholine 2 mg/kg iv/io **or** 2. rocuronium 0.6-1.2 mg/kg iv/io 3. administer over 30 seconds and wait until fasciculation stops viii. consider removing cervical collar (if present) while providing in-line manual immobilization of the head and neck ix. perform intubation per guid3203-pr020 - endotracheal intubation 1. if unsuccessful, supply o2 with bvm for 30-60 seconds and re-attempt intubation 2. if patient begins to clench teeth or move, administer second dose of succinylcholine prior to second intubation attempt 3. treat bradycardia during intubation by temporarily halting attempt and hyperventilating the patient with the bvm and 100% oxygen. if patient remains bradycardic, consider atropine 0.5 mg iv (adult) or 0.02 mg/kg iv (pediatric) to a max single dose of 0.5 mg and a maximum total dose of 2 mg 4. if endotracheal intubation fails after 2 attempts and you are unable to adequately ventilate the patient with a bvm, consider a supraglottic airway device see guid3203-pr016 - king lts-d. otherwise, continue to ventilate with bvm. 5. if the supraglottic airway device insertion is unsuccessful and the patient is still unable to be ventilated with a bvm, consider cricothyroidotomy using the h&h emergency cricothyroidotomy kit. see guid3203-007 - cricothyroidotomy-surgical. x. once intubation is completed, inflate balloon (if present), confirm tube placement with a waveform etco2 detector. xi. assess epigastric sounds and breath sounds. secure tube. xii. all advanced airways (ett, king lt, igel, laryngeal mask airway [lma]) must be monitored with continuous waveform capnography during act transport. 1. if no waveform is present and brief equipment troubleshooting does not result in a four phase capnographic waveform, remove the advanced airway device and attempt reinsertion or ventilate patient with bvm xiii. reapply cervical collar and complete spinal immobilization for transport xiv. assure that all intubated patients have protective devices applied to wrists and/or ankles to protect patient and crew xv. for continued sedation during transport: 1. midazolam 0.05-0.1 mg/kg iv/io, may repeat every 3-5 minutes prn, maximum dose 5 mg. maintain sbp > 90 mmhg 2. if sbp < 90 mmhg, consider ketamine a. adults: 1-2 mg/kg slow iv/io. may repeat every 15 minutes prn. maximum total dose 200 mg. monitor for tachydysrhythmias page 3 of 4 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: rapid sequence induction (rsi) for endotracheal (et) intubation guideline number: guid-3203-g008 xvi. consider rocuronium 0.6-1.2 mg/kg iv/io (adult) or 0.6 mg/kg iv/io (peds) for ett/airway protection and prolonged paralysis during transport if patient is intubated and has adequate sedation. **do not give a paralytic drug if patient cannot be sedated** xvii. for pain control and comfort, manage per guid3203-g005 - analgesia and sedation management xviii. provide post-intubation care per guid3203-pr020 - endotracheal intubation xix. monitor for complications including vomiting, cardiac dysrhythmias (bradycardia, pvcs, and v-fib), malignant hyperthermia, initial fasciculations, failed intubation, and aspiration of stomach contents iv. documentation: a. patient report/ems charts b. narcotic log/pstrax v. references: a. frakes, m.a., (2004). clinical features of patients intubated by a flight team. air medical journal 23:3:37-39. b. poste, j.c., et al, (2004). air medical transport of severely head-injured patients undergoing paramedic rapid sequence intubation. air medical journal 23:4:-40. c. smith, c. e., et al, (2002). prehospital tracheal intubating conditions during rapid sequence intubation: rocuronium versus vecuronium. air medical journal 21:1:26-32. d. wang, h.e., et al (2001). prehospital rapid sequence intubation. prehospital emergency care 5:40- 48. page 4 of 4 © orlando health 2017

endotracheal intubation 1. if unsuccessful, supply o2 with bvm for 30-60 seconds and re-attempt intubation 2. if patient begins to clench teeth or move, administer second dose of succinylcholine prior to second intubation attempt 3. treat bradycardia during intubation by temporarily halting attempt and hyperventilating the patient with the bvm and 100% oxygen. if patient remains bradycardic, consider atropine 0.5 mg iv (adult) or 0.02 mg/kg iv (pediatric) to a max single dose of 0.5 mg and a maximum total dose of 2 mg 4. if endotracheal intubation fails after 2 attempts and you are unable to adequately ventilate the patient with a bvm, consider a supraglottic airway device see guid3203-pr016 - king lts-d. otherwise, continue to ventilate with bvm. 5. if the supraglottic airway device insertion is unsuccessful and the patient is still unable to be ventilated with a bvm, consider cricothyroidotomy using the h&h emergency cricothyroidotomy kit. see guid3203-007 - cricothyroidotomy-surgical. x. once intubation is completed, inflate balloon (if present), confirm tube placement with a waveform etco2 detector. xi. assess epigastric sounds and breath sounds. secure tube. xii. all advanced airways (ett, king lt, igel, laryngeal mask airway [lma]) must be monitored with continuous waveform capnography during act transport. 1. if no waveform is present and brief equipment troubleshooting does not result in a four phase capnographic waveform, remove the advanced airway device and attempt reinsertion or ventilate patient with bvm xiii. reapply cervical collar and complete spinal immobilization for transport xiv. assure that all intubated patients have protective devices applied to wrists and/or ankles to protect patient and crew xv. for continued sedation during transport: 1. midazolam 0.05-0.1 mg/kg iv/io, may repeat every 3-5 minutes prn, maximum dose 5 mg. maintain sbp > 90 mmhg 2. if sbp < 90 mmhg, consider ketamine a. adults: 1-2 mg/kg slow iv/io. may repeat every 15 minutes prn. maximum total dose 200 mg. monitor for tachydysrhythmias page 3 of 4 © orlando health 2017

1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: rapid sequence induction (rsi) for endotracheal (et) intubation guideline number: guid-3203-g008 xvi. consider rocuronium 0.6-1.2 mg/kg iv/io (adult) or 0.6 mg/kg iv/io (peds) for ett/airway protection and prolonged paralysis during transport if patient is intubated and has adequate sedation. **do not give a paralytic drug if patient cannot be sedated** xvii. for pain control and comfort, manage per guid3203-g005 - analgesia and sedation management xviii. provide post-intubation care per guid3203-pr020 - endotracheal intubation xix. monitor for complications including vomiting, cardiac dysrhythmias (bradycardia, pvcs, and v-fib), malignant hyperthermia, initial fasciculations, failed intubation, and aspiration of stomach contents iv. documentation: a. patient report/ems charts b. narcotic log/pstrax v. references: a. frakes, m.a., (2004). clinical features of patients intubated by a flight team. air medical journal 23:3:37-39. b. poste, j.c., et al, (2004). air medical transport of severely head-injured patients undergoing paramedic rapid sequence intubation. air medical journal 23:4:-40. c. smith, c. e., et al, (2002). prehospital tracheal intubating conditions during rapid sequence intubation: rocuronium versus vecuronium. air medical journal 21:1:26-32. d. wang, h.e., et al (2001). prehospital rapid sequence intubation. prehospital emergency care 5:40- 48. page 4 of 4 © orlando health 2017

title: Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: abdominal aortic aneurysm guideline number: guid-3203-m001 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: recognition of the patient with an aortic aneurysm that is expanding, ruptured, or caused an aortic dissection; provide and maintain adequate oxygenation, ventilation, and end-organ tissue perfusion; provide expeditious transport to the nearest appropriate medical facility. ii. definitions: a. aaa - abdominal aortic aneurysm iii. department guideline: a. establish care as defined by guid3203-g002 - general patient care. b. establish two large-bore peripheral intravenous catheters in patients who are symptomatic. c. symptomatic, expanding aaas may be managed conservatively unless directed by the accepting physician. i. if the patient has pain and/or anxiety, treat as per guid3203-g005 - analgesia and sedation management or guid3203-g006 - management for the anxiety/combative patient. ii. if the patient remains tachycardic and hypertensive after analgesia and anxiolytics consider short-acting anti-hypertensives per guid3203-m010 - hypertensive emergency. d. ruptured aaas are a surgical emergency i. if patient displays signs of inadequate perfusion in the setting of a ruptured aortic aneurysm, begin resuscitation with ns per guid3203-m011 - hypotension. resuscitate to sbp of 80-100 mmhg. ii. allow permissive hypotension to maintain patient mental status. limit fluid infusion. over resuscitation may result in increased bleeding. iii. consider transfusion of blood products per guid3203-pr002 - blood product administration. 1. reversal agents should also be considered for patients who are anticoagulated. e. if patient has a contained ruptured aaa or an aortic dissection, treat per guid3203-m018 thoracic aortic dissection i. goals: adequate end-organ perfusion with sbp < 120 mmhg and hr < 60 bpm. ii. if sbp > 120 mmhg and hr > 60 bpm administer beta blockers page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: abdominal aortic aneurysm guideline number: guid-3203-m001 1. esmolol bolus 500 mcg/kg/min iv over 1 minute followed by esmolol infusion at 50 mcg/kg/min. titrate every 5 minutes by repeating 500 mcg/kg/min bolus over 1 minute and then increasing infusion by 50 mcg/kg/min. maximum dose 300 mcg/kg/min 2. labetalol 10-20 mg iv over 2 minutes. may repeat every 10 minutes until goal blood pressure obtained or maximum total dose of 300mg. 3. if sbp remains > 120 mmhg after β-blockers, or hr < 60 bpm prior to beta blockers administer: a. nicardipine infusion at 5 mg/h. increase by 2.5 mg/h iv every 5-15 minutes to maximum of 15 mg/h. b. nitroprusside (50 mg/250ml d5w) infusion at 0.3 mcg/kg/min. the infusion can be increased by 0.5 mcg/kg/min every 5 minutes until desired bp is reached. max dose 10 mcg/kg/min (avoid in renal failure). i. treat reflex tachycardia from nitroprusside with labetalol or esmolol as above f. as the management of the patient with a symptomatic aaa is operative and extremely time dependent, the transport team should minimize scene time by performing all non-critical interventions while en route to receiving facility. iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Diabetic Ketoacidosis (Adult)
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: diabetic ketoacidosis (adult) guideline number: guid-3203-m002 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to recognize the patient with diabetic ketoacidosis (dka) and to maintain adequate oxygenation/ventilation/perfusion, replace deficits, and correct acidosis without causing cerebral edema or severe electrolyte imbalances. provide expeditious transport to the nearest appropriate medical facility. ii. definitions: a. dka - diabetic ketoacidosis iii. department guideline: a. establish care as defined by guid3203-g002 - general patient care. b. assess fluid replacement need. patients with dka have an average of 5-7 l of fluid deficit. i. initially patients should receive a 1000 ml 0.9% ns bolus over 1 hour followed by 500 ml of 0.9% ns per hour for the next 3-4 hours. ii. when plasma glucose reaches 300 mg/dl, fluid should be changed to d5ns with continued insulin therapy. c. evaluate the need for potassium replacement therapy prior to departing the referring facility. normal potassium level in an acidotic patient will require potassium replacement as the acidosis is corrected. see guid3203-m006 electrolyte disorders - potassium imbalance. d. for patients with decreased cardiac contractility and vasodilation that may impair tissue perfusion with a ph <6.9, consider sodium bicarbonate infusion 50 meq in 250 ml d5w over 1 hour. e. if fluid resuscitation completed, discuss regular insulin administration with sending or receiving physician. i. consider regular insulin (100 units/100ml ns) infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hr. ii. check a blood glucose level every 30 minutes if patient is receiving insulin therapy. iii. the insulin drip should be continued at the current rate until ketonemia and acidosis have been corrected. iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: page 1 of 1 © orlando health 2017

title: Allergic Reaction/Anaphylaxis
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: allergic reaction/anaphylaxis guideline number: guid-3203-m004 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: recognition of a patient presenting with anaphylaxis; provide and maintain adequate oxygenation, ventilation, and end-organ tissue perfusion; and to provide emergent care and expeditious transport to the nearest appropriate medical facility. ii. definitions: a. mild reaction: itching/hives b. moderate reaction: dyspnea, wheezing, chest tightness, facial swelling c. severe reaction: < 90 mmhg, map < 65, stridor, severe respiratory distress, vomiting/diarrhea iii. department guideline: a. establish care as defined by guid3203-g002 - general patient care. b. while assessing the patient, attempt to determine the etiology of the allergic reaction. i. if medication infusion reaction suspected, stop the infusion. c. in cases of severe distress, administer epinephrine: i. epinephrine (1:1,000) 0.5 mg im in the anterior thigh q 5-15 min (or sooner if rapidly developing shock) up to 3 doses. 1. massage injection site vigorously for 30-60 seconds d. obtain large bore iv/io access e. if patient is hypotensive or displays signs of inadequate tissue perfusion, administer a fluid bolus of 250 ml of 0.9% normal saline. repeat as necessary. i. if patient remains hypotensive after im epinephrine and multiple fluid boluses, consider initiating an epinephrine (4mg/250ml ns or d5w) infusion at 0.1mcg/kg/min and titrate every 2-3 min by 0.05mcg/kg/min. maintenance rate 0.025-0.5 mcg/kg/min. 1. if patient is on beta-blocker therapy and appears refractory to epinephrine treatment, consider glucagon, 1-5 mg slow iv bolus 2. for additional management refer to guid3203-m011 - hypotension. f. perform a rapid assessment of the patient's airway i. intubation should be performed if stridor or respiratory distress due to upper airway compromise, significant edema of tongue, oropharyngeal tissues, or voice alteration per guid3203-g008 - rapid sequence induction for endotracheal intubation. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: allergic reaction/anaphylaxis guideline number: guid-3203-m004 ii. emergency cricothyroidotomy can be considered to secure airway if severe upper airway edema prevents access to glottic opening with the use of a video laryngoscope per guid3203-pr007 - cricothyrotomy - surgical. g. if the patient is experiencing dyspnea (with or without wheezing), administer i. high flow oxygen ii. inhaled bronchodilators and repeat as needed: albuterol 2.5mg/3ml nss and ipratropium bromide 0.02% 0.5mg/2.5ml via neb per guid3203-pr012 - inhalation therapy. iii. do not delay endotracheal intubation if patient has rapidly evolving airway obstruction. h. administer antihistamine i. diphenhydramine 25-50 mg slow iv/io/im ii. consider methylprednisolone 125 mg iv when hemodynamically stable. i. consider h2 blocker if at sending facility: famotidine 20 mg po or 0.25 mg/kg iv over 2 minutes. iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: a. cheng, a., canadian paediatric society, acute care committee. (2011). emergency treatment of anaphylaxis in infants and children. paediatr child health, 16(1), 35-40. b. weingart, s. d. & borshiff, d. c. (2018). the resuscitation crisis manual. leeuwin press. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Magnesium Imbalance
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: magnesium imbalance guideline number: guid-3203-m005 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to recognize the patient with a magnesium imbalance and to provide and maintain adequate oxygenation/ventilation and tissue perfusion. to provide emergent treatment, correct the imbalance and provide expeditious transport to the nearest appropriate medical facility. ii. definitions: none iii. department guideline: a. establish care as defined by guid3203-g002 - general patient care. b. hypomagnesemia: i. treatment depends on the degree of deficiency and the clinical effects. oral replacement is appropriate for mild symptoms, while iv replacement is indicated for severe clinical effects. discuss with sending or receiving physician regarding treatment. ii. if magnesium level confirmed < 1.5 or patient is symptomatic (neuromuscular irritability, hyperactive deep tendon reflexes, muscle cramps, muscle fibrillation, trousseau and chvostek signs, dysarthria, and dysphagia from esophageal dysmotility, cns hyperexcitability, irritability, combativeness, disorientation, psychosis, ataxia, vertigo, nystagmus), consider magnesium sulfate 1 gram iv over 30 minutes. iii. if cardiac dysrhythmias or seizures are present, administer magnesium sulfate 1-2 grams iv push over 2 minutes treat seizures with benzodiazepines per guid3203-m015 - seizures. c. hypermagnesemia: i. calcium can be effective in reversing the physical findings associated with hypermagnesemia such as nausea, vomiting, skin flushing, weakness, lightheadedness, depressed levels of consciousness, disappearance of deep tendon reflexes, hypotension, vasodilation, dysrhythmias, including atrial fibrillation, and intraventricular conduction delay. use caution if patient is on digitalis. for symptomatic adult patients: 1. [prefered] calcium gluconate 1-2 grams iv over 3 min iv slowly from sending facility. a. (if the patient is on digitalis, more caution is required as calcium can increase the toxic effects of digitalis. add the appropriate dose to 100ml ns and infuse over 20-30 minutes.) - or - b. calcium chloride 1 gram iv slowly over 10 minutes, preferentially administered page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: magnesium imbalance guideline number: guid-3203-m005 through central line or in the setting of acute decompensation/ cardiac arrest. monitor for local irritation at the injection site. iv. documentation: ems charts v. references: page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Potassium Imbalance
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: potassium imbalance guideline number: guid-3203-m006 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to recognize the patient with a potassium imbalance and to provide and maintain adequate oxygenation/ventilation, tissue perfusion, and cardiac rhythm. to provide emergent treatment, correct the imbalance and provide expeditious transport to the nearest appropriate medical facility. ii. definitions: a. hypokalemia - a potassium (k+) serum level < 3.5 meq/l i. mild: 3.1-3.4 meq/l, moderate: 2.5-3 meq/l, severe: < 2.5 meq/l b. hyperkalemia - a potassium (k+) serum level > 5.5 meq/l i. mild: 5.5 - 6 meq/l, moderate: 6.1 - 7 meq/l, severe: >7 meq/l iii. department guideline: a. establish care as defined by guid3203-g002 - general patient care. b. hyperkalemia : i. assess for clinical signs or symptoms of a hyperkalemic emergency 1. most serious: muscle weakness, paralysis, cardiac conduction abnormalities, cardiac arrythmias. 2. review ecg for: a tall peaked t wave with a shortened qt interval, followed by progressive lengthening of the pr interval and qrs duration. the p wave may disappear, and the qrs widens further to a sine wave. conduction abnormalities, such as bundle branch blocks and arrhythmias (sinus bradycardia, sinus arrest, slow idioventricular rhythms, ventricular tachycardia, ventricular fibrillation, pseudo-st-elevation myocardial infarction, pseudo- brugada patterns, and asystole). 3. patients with moderate hyperkalemia (> 5.5 meq/l) who have significant renal impairment and marked, ongoing tissue breakdown (e.g., rhabdomyolysis or crush injury, tumor lysis syndrome), ongoing potassium absorption (e.g., from substantial gastrointestinal bleeding), or a significant non-anion gap metabolic acidosis or respiratory acidosis. 4. consult with sending provider if patient has severe hyperkalemia with suspicious ekg changes. this needs to be addressed prior to departure for safe transport. ii. calcium is indicated if k+ > 7 meq/l or when ecg shows changes (loss of p waves & prolonged qrs duration. 1.[prefered] calcium gluconate 1-2 grams iv over 3 min iv slowly from sending facility. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: potassium imbalance guideline number: guid-3203-m006 a. (if the patient is on digitalis, more caution is required as calcium can increase the toxic effects of digitalis. add the appropriate dose to 100ml ns and infuse over 20-30 minutes.) - or - b. calcium chloride 1 gram iv slowly over 10 minutes, preferentially administered through central line or in the setting of acute decompensation/ cardiac arrest. monitor for local irritation at the injection site. 1. if at sending facility, consider dextrose 50% 25 grams iv followed by regular insulin 5 units iv to shift potassium into the intracellular space. 2. check glucose level every 30 minutes and treat hypoglycemia per guid3203-m021 hypoglycemia. iii. consider sodium bicarbonate 8.4% 50 meq iv/io. iv. consider albuterol 2.5-5 mg in 3 ml of ns via nebulizer, may repeat. v. if patient has signs of diabetic ketoacidosis, initiate therapy as outlined in guid3203-m002 - adult diabetic ketoacidosis c. hypokalemia: i. if patient is severely bradycardic or manifesting cardiac arrhythmias, consider pharmacologic therapy or cardiac pacing per guid3203-c004 - bradycardia and guid3203-pr022 - transcutaneous external pacing. ii. if k+ level is < 2.5 meq/l, consult sending/receiving physician for replacement orders. consider potassium 10-20 meq/100ml iv at a rate not to exceed 10 meq/hour through a peripheral iv access and 20meq/h through a central venous access or io. iii. serum k+ is difficult to replenish if serum magnesium is also low. review magnesium level and treat per guid3203-m005 magnesium imbalance. iv. documentation: ems charts v. references: a. littmann l, gibbs ma. (2018). electrocardiographic manifestations of severe hyperkalemia. j electrocardiol, 51, 814. b. mount db. disorders of potassium balance. (2015). in: brenner and rector's the kidney, 10th ed, w.b. saunders & company, philadelphia, p. 559. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

pages.text: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: potassium imbalance guideline number: guid-3203-m006 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to recognize the patient with a potassium imbalance and to provide and maintain adequate oxygenation/ventilation, tissue perfusion, and cardiac rhythm. to provide emergent treatment, correct the imbalance and provide expeditious transport to the nearest appropriate medical facility. ii. definitions: a. hypokalemia - a potassium (k+) serum level < 3.5 meq/l i. mild: 3.1-3.4 meq/l, moderate: 2.5-3 meq/l, severe: < 2.5 meq/l b. hyperkalemia - a potassium (k+) serum level > 5.5 meq/l i. mild: 5.5 - 6 meq/l, moderate: 6.1 - 7 meq/l, severe: >7 meq/l iii. department guideline: a. establish care as defined by guid3203-g002 - general patient care. b. hyperkalemia : i. assess for clinical signs or symptoms of a hyperkalemic emergency 1. most serious: muscle weakness, paralysis, cardiac conduction abnormalities, cardiac arrythmias. 2. review ecg for: a tall peaked t wave with a shortened qt interval, followed by progressive lengthening of the pr interval and qrs duration. the p wave may disappear, and the qrs widens further to a sine wave. conduction abnormalities, such as bundle branch blocks and arrhythmias (sinus bradycardia, sinus arrest, slow idioventricular rhythms, ventricular tachycardia, ventricular fibrillation, pseudo-st-elevation myocardial infarction, pseudo- brugada patterns, and asystole). 3. patients with moderate hyperkalemia (> 5.5 meq/l) who have significant renal impairment and marked, ongoing tissue breakdown (e.g., rhabdomyolysis or crush injury, tumor lysis syndrome), ongoing potassium absorption (e.g., from substantial gastrointestinal bleeding), or a significant non-anion gap metabolic acidosis or respiratory acidosis. 4. consult with sending provider if patient has severe hyperkalemia with suspicious ekg changes. this needs to be addressed prior to departure for safe transport. ii. calcium is indicated if k+ > 7 meq/l or when ecg shows changes (loss of p waves & prolonged qrs duration. 1.[prefered] calcium gluconate 1-2 grams iv over 3 min iv slowly from sending facility. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017

1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: potassium imbalance guideline number: guid-3203-m006 a. (if the patient is on digitalis, more caution is required as calcium can increase the toxic effects of digitalis. add the appropriate dose to 100ml ns and infuse over 20-30 minutes.) - or - b. calcium chloride 1 gram iv slowly over 10 minutes, preferentially administered through central line or in the setting of acute decompensation/ cardiac arrest. monitor for local irritation at the injection site. 1. if at sending facility, consider dextrose 50% 25 grams iv followed by regular insulin 5 units iv to shift potassium into the intracellular space. 2. check glucose level every 30 minutes and treat hypoglycemia per guid3203-m021 hypoglycemia. iii. consider sodium bicarbonate 8.4% 50 meq iv/io. iv. consider albuterol 2.5-5 mg in 3 ml of ns via nebulizer, may repeat. v. if patient has signs of diabetic ketoacidosis, initiate therapy as outlined in guid3203-m002 - adult diabetic ketoacidosis c. hypokalemia: i. if patient is severely bradycardic or manifesting cardiac arrhythmias, consider pharmacologic therapy or cardiac pacing per guid3203-c004 - bradycardia and guid3203-pr022 - transcutaneous external pacing. ii. if k+ level is < 2.5 meq/l, consult sending/receiving physician for replacement orders. consider potassium 10-20 meq/100ml iv at a rate not to exceed 10 meq/hour through a peripheral iv access and 20meq/h through a central venous access or io. iii. serum k+ is difficult to replenish if serum magnesium is also low. review magnesium level and treat per guid3203-m005 magnesium imbalance. iv. documentation: ems charts v. references: a. littmann l, gibbs ma. (2018). electrocardiographic manifestations of severe hyperkalemia. j electrocardiol, 51, 814. b. mount db. disorders of potassium balance. (2015). in: brenner and rector's the kidney, 10th ed, w.b. saunders & company, philadelphia, p. 559. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Gastrointestinal Bleeding
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: gastrointestinal bleeding guideline number: guid-3203-m007 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 6/8/2021 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to recognize the patient with gastrointestinal bleeding and to provide and maintain adequate oxygenation/ventilation and tissue perfusion. to provide expeditious transport to the nearest appropriate medical facility. ii. definitions: none iii. department guideline: a. establish care as defined by guid3203-g002 - general patient care b. assess for signs of hypovolemia/shock, hypotension, tachycardia, decreased pulse pressure, angina, syncope, weakness, and confusion. "coffee ground" emesis, hematemesis, or melena suggest a proximal lesion and hematochezia suggest a distal lesion. c. obtain history of medications (nsaids/aspirin), alcohol use, and anticoagulants d. if signs of inadequate perfusion, treat per guid3203-m011 - hypotension e. if emergency blood products are indicated, treat per guid3203-pr002 - blood product administration. f. consider naso-gastric tube (ng) placement if significant gi bleeding per guid3203-pr010 gastric tube insertion. connect to low intermittent suction. avoid over-vigorous suction to prevent mucosal trauma. i. if patient has known or suspected esophageal varices, confirm with receiving physician prior to placing ng tube g. if balloon tamponade (minnesota or blakemore tube) is in place, tracheal intubation should be placed and secured pre guid3203-pr020 - endotracheal intubation. h. continue administration of the following: i. pantoprazole 80 mg iv bolus followed by pantoprazole infusion at 8 mg/hour. ii. octreotide 50 mcg iv bolus followed by octreotide infusion at 50 mcg/h for bleeding esophageal varices. iii. vasopressin infusion 0.2-0.4 units/min for refractory hypotension i. consider premedicating patient with zofran 4 mg iv for nausea/vomiting and treating per guid3203-g004 - nausea/vomiting/motion sickness. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: gastrointestinal bleeding guideline number: guid-3203-m007 iv. documentation: ems charts v. references: page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Bronchospasm (Asthma & COPD)
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: bronchospasm (asthma & copd) guideline number: guid-3203-m008 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: bronchospasm m008 revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to recognize the patient with bronchospasm and to provide and maintain adequate oxygenation/ventilation and tissue perfusion. to provide appropriate treatment and expeditious transport to the nearest appropriate medical facility. ii. definitions: a. copd - chronic obstructive pulmonary disease iii. department guideline: a. establish care as defined by guid3203-g002 - general patient care. b. assess for adventitious breath sounds (wheezes, crackles), hyperresonance upon percussion, increased anterior and posterior chest diameter, nasal flaring, pursed lip breathing, accessory muscle use with breathing, and tachypnea. c. elicit a history to identify precipitating factors. obtain a history of intubations or hospitalizations (indicators of the severity of their disease). d. place patient in a position of comfort, usually high flower's position. e. if adequate spontaneous respirations, administer oxygen to maintain saturation >93%. f. if severe respiratory distress, administer high flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask (be cautious with oxygen administration in copd patients as they may retain co2). be prepared for advanced airway management per guid3203-pr020 endotracheal intubation. if intubation is indicated, the preferred induction agent is ketamine over etomidate. g. trial continuous positive airway pressure (cpap) or biphasic positive airway pressure (bi-pap) per guid3203-pr018 non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (nppv) if at sending facility. h. if acute dyspnea present (with or without wheezing) i. albuterol 2.5 mg in 3 ml of ns nebulized. repeat as necessary. ii. ipratropium 0.02% 0.5 mg (2.5ml) nebulized. may repeat for a total of three doses if patient in severe distress. i. consider steroids: methylprednisolone 125 mg iv. j. consider magnesium sulfate 2 grams iv over 10 minutes page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: bronchospasm (asthma & copd) guideline number: guid-3203-m008 k. if the patient is experiencing a severe life-threatening reaction or shows signs of shock treat per guid3203-m004 allergic reaction/anaphylaxis. consider epinephrine (1:1,000) 0.3-0.5 mg im in the anterior thigh. i. massage injection site vigorously for 30-60 seconds. ii. use with caution in patients with risk factors for or known cardiovascular disease. in these patients, administer epi only in cases of severe bronchospasm and when imminent threat to life exists. l. consider terbutaline 0.25 mg sc, repeat in 15 minutes (max dose 0.5 mg in 4 hours). iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: bronchospasm (asthma & copd) guideline number: guid-3203-m008 k. if the patient is experiencing a severe life-threatening reaction or shows signs of shock treat per guid3203-m004 allergic reaction/anaphylaxis. consider epinephrine (1:1,000) 0.3-0.5 mg im in the anterior thigh. i. massage injection site vigorously for 30-60 seconds. ii. use with caution in patients with risk factors for or known cardiovascular disease. in these patients, administer epi only in cases of severe bronchospasm and when imminent threat to life exists. l. consider terbutaline 0.25 mg sc, repeat in 15 minutes (max dose 0.5 mg in 4 hours). iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Heat Related Illness/Hyperthermia
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: heat related illness/hyperthermia guideline number: guid-3203-m009 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: therapeutic goal: to recognize the patient with heat related illness and to provide and maintain adequate oxygenation/ventilation and tissue perfusion. to provide adequate temperature control and expeditious transport to the nearest appropriate medical facility. ii. definitions: a. bp - blood pressure b. map - mean arterial pressure iii. department guideline: a. establish care as defined by guid3203-g002 - general patient care. b. remove the patient from the current environment and place in an area that is cool. c. provide supplemental oxygen. d. keep patient supine. e. establish 2 large bore iv access f. if the patient is hypotensive i. adults: 0.9% ns iv/io fluid boluses per guid3203-m011 - hypotension. ii. pediatric: 0.9% ns 10-20 ml/kg iv/io fluid boluses per guid3203-ped009 - pediatric hypotension. g. actively cool the patient: remove excess clothing, apply cool water to skin, increase movement of air over the patient, and/or apply cool packs to groin and axilla if available. h. perform a bedside glucose check and if glucose is <60 mg/dl and the patient is symptomatic, treat per guid3203-m021 - hypoglycemia. i. if cocaine/sympathomimetic toxicity strongly suspected with signs of hypertension, tachycardia, or agitation consider midazolam 0.05-0.1 mg/kg iv/io, may repeat every 3-5 minutes prn, maximum dose 5 mg j. if seizures occur, treat per guid3203-m015 - seizures. iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: page 1 of 1 © orlando health 2017

title: Hypertensive Emergency
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: hypertensive emergency guideline number: guid-3203-m010 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: hypertensive emergency is defined as an elevated blood pressure associated with acute central nervous system, cardiac, and/or renal dysfunction. the purpose is to first recognize the patient with a hypertensive emergency then provide and maintain adequate oxygenation, ventilation, and end-organ tissue perfusion and to provide adequate blood pressure control and expeditious transport to the nearest appropriate medical facility. ii. definitions: a. hr - heart rate b. map - mean arterial pressure c. sbp - systolic blood pressure d. dbp - diastolic blood pressure iii. department guideline: a. establish care as defined by guid3203-g002 - general patient care. b. assess patient for headache, visual changes, weakness, chest pain, palpitations, dyspnea, pedal edema, anuria, hematuria, seizures, and encephalopathy. complete a focused neurologic and cardiovascular exam to include bilateral upper extremity blood pressures. c. isolated asymptomatic hypertension with no evidence of end-organ damage does not require treatment. monitor closely for changes. d. consider obtaining a 12 lead ekg per guid3203-pr001 - 12-lead electrocardiogram. e. consider pain medication per guid3203-g005 analgesia and sedation management and apply etco2 monitoring. f. for bp > 220/120 mmhg with signs of end-organ damage, administer one of the following anti- hypertensives until either symptoms are alleviated or a 20-25% reduction in map is achieved. i. labetalol 10-20 mg iv over 2 minutes. may repeat every 10 minutes until goal blood pressure obtained or maximum total dose of 300mg. avoid if hr < 60 bpm. ii. nicardipine infusion at 5mg/h. increase by 2.5mg/h iv every 5-15 minutes to maximum of 15mg/h. iii. hydralazine 10-20 mg iv q 20 minutes. max total dose 40mg. avoid in elevated intracranial pressure or myocardial infarctions. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: hypertensive emergency guideline number: guid-3203-m010 iv. nitroprusside (50 mg/250ml d5w) infusion at 0.3 mcg/kg/min. the infusion can be increased by 0.5 mcg/kg/min every 5 minutes until desired bp is reached. max dose 10 mcg/kg/min (avoid in renal failure). v. esmolol bolus 500 mcg/kg/min iv over 1 minute followed by esmolol infusion at 50 mcg/kg/min. titrate every 5 minutes by repeating 500 mcg/kg/min bolus over 1 minute and then increasing infusion by 50 mcg/kg/min. maximum dose 300 mcg/kg/min g. see appropriate guidelines for specific blood pressure control parameters for these conditions i. stroke/transient ischemic attack guid3203-m017 - stroke or transient ischemic attack ii. subarachnoid hemorrhage, non-traumatic per guid3203-m013 - subarachnoid hemorrhage iii. thoracic aortic dissection per guid3203-m018 - thoracic aortic dissection iv. abdominal aortic aneurysm (rupture or impending rupture) per guid3203-m001 - abdominal aortic aneurysm v. congestive heart failure & pulmonary edema, per guid3203-c005 - congestive heart failure and pulmonary edema h. avoid d5w or dextrose-based fluids. i. if patient is hypertensive due to sympathomimetic drug use (e.g., cocaine, methamphetamines, bath salts, ecstasy, etc.) treat with benzodiazepines and avoid beta-blockers i. midazolam 0.05-0.1 mg/kg iv/io, may repeat every 3-5 minutes prn, maximum dose 5 mg j. continuously monitor heart rate and blood pressure during all medication administration. k. consider obtaining atrial line placement if at sending facility and continue atrial blood pressure monitoring for patients being transported on antihypertensive medications iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Hypotension
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: hypotension guideline number: guid-3203-m011 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to recognize the patient with hypotension. to provide and maintain adequate oxygenation, ventilation, and tissue perfusion. to provide adequate blood pressure support and expeditious transport to the nearest appropriate medical facility. ii. definitions: iii. department guideline: a. establish care as defined by guid3203-g002 - general patient care. b. establish two large bore ivs c. maintain systolic blood pressure (sbp) > 90 mmhg and mean arterial pressure (map) > 65 mmhg d. administer a 250 ml fluid bolus (0.9 % normal saline or lactated ringers) and re-evaluate blood pressure. repeat boluses prn in the absence of signs and symptoms of volume overload (edema, jvd, pulmonary edema) e. if actively hemorrhaging, control the bleeding and consider infusing blood products per guid3203-pr002 - blood product administration or gui3203d-pr025 - emergency blood product administration. f. if the patient remains hypotensive, administer a continuous vasopressor or inotropic infusion i. norepinephrine 0.05-1 mcg/kg/min iv titrate q5 min to map > 65 mmhg this is the first- choice vasopressor for sepsis, neurogenic shock, cardiogenic shock, undifferentiated shock, and bradycardia with hypotension. ii. epinephrine 0.05-2 mcg/kg/min iv titrate q5 min to map > 65 mmhg. this is the first- choice vasopressor for anaphylaxis and can be considered for refractory septic shock or shock after rosc. iii. consider vasopressin 0.03 units/min iv infusion for septic shock or hypotensive gi bleed refractory to fluid therapy. iv. consider dopamine 2-20 mcg/kg/min iv titrate q5 min to effect for bradycardia or hypotension refractory to fluid therapy. v. consider inotropic support with dobutamine 2-20 mcg/kg/min iv titrate q5 min to effect for cardiogenic shock refractory to norepinephrine alone. vi. consider phenylephrine 0.5-5mcg/kg/min iv titrate q5 min to map > 65 mmhg for known spinal shock. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: hypotension guideline number: guid-3203-m011 g. consider insertion of indwelling urinary catheter with urometer at the referring facility to accurately monitor urine output if available. h. consider obtaining atrial line placement if at sending facility and continue atrial blood pressure monitoring for patients being transported on antihypertensive medications iv. documentation: ems charts v. references: page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Hypothermia
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: hypothermia guideline number: guid-3203-m012 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: recognize hypothermia and provide/maintain adequate oxygenation, ventilation, end-organ tissue perfusion, temperature control. provide expeditious transport to the nearest appropriate facility. ii. definitions: none iii. department guideline: a. establish care as defined by guid3203-g002 - general patient care b. remove patient from cold environment to somewhere warm. remove wet clothing. handle patient gently to avoid subsequent dysrhythmias. c. attempt to obtain a history regarding the circumstances surrounding the cold exposure. d. perform a blood glucose check from a venous access site and if glucose is < 60 mg/dl, and patient is symptomatic, treat per guid3203-m021 - hypoglycemia. e. if possible, assess core body temperature in bladder, rectum, or with esophageal probe. f. administer warmed iv fluids per guid3203-pr026 warming of blood products & intravenous fluids. g. if core temperature is between 34-36°c: gradually re-warm using blankets, heat packs (with barrier between pack and patient), and external warming devices (if available). h. if core temperature is between 30-34°c: actively re-warm the trunk of the body. do not attempt to re-warm extremities. i. if patient in cardiac arrest, and the core temperature is < 30°c i. continue cpr; however, withhold all iv medications. ii. if the patient is in ventricular fibrillation or ventricular tachycardia, limit the number of defibrillation attempts to a maximum of three. j. if the patient is in cardiac arrest, and the core temperature is > 30°c i. assess for bradycardia. continue cardiopulmonary resuscitation. administer iv medications as clinically indicated, prolonging the standard intervals at which they are administered. ii. if ventricular fibrillation or ventricular tachycardia, repeat defibrillation as the core temperature rises. iv. documentation: a. ems charts page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: hypothermia guideline number: guid-3203-m012 v. references: page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Subarachnoid Hemorrhage, Non-traumatic
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: subarachnoid hemorrhage, non-traumatic guideline number: guid-3203-m013 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: non-traumatic subarachnoid hemorrhage m013 revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to recognize the patient with non-traumatic subarachnoid hemorrhage (sah) and to provide and maintain adequate oxygenation/ventilation and tissue perfusion. to provide emergent treatment, prevent secondary brain injury, and expeditious transport to the nearest appropriate medical facility. ii. definitions: a. cpp - cerebral perfusion pressure = map-icp b. gcs - glasgow coma scale c. icp - intracranial pressure d. map - mean arterial pressure e. sbp - systolic blood pressure iii. department guideline: a. establish care as defined by guid3203-g002 - general patient care. b. preform a complete and timely neurological exam. c. consider ottawa subarachnoid hemorrhage rule for scene suspected sah: age ≥ 40 yo, neck pain/stiffness, limited neck flexion on exam, witnessed loss of consciousness, onset during exertion, and thunderclap headache. d. maintain hob at 30-degree angle if spinal cord injury is not suspected. e. treat pain and/or anxiety per guid3203-g005 - analgesia and sedation management. f. treat nausea/vomiting per guid3203-g004 - motion sickness/nausea. g. in the intubated patient, short acting agents such as propofol are ideal. h. target sbp < 140 mmhg unless otherwise directed by the receiving physician. preferred medications: i. nicardipine infusion at 5mg/h. increase by 2.5mg/h iv every 5-15 minutes to maximum of 15mg/h. ii. esmolol bolus 500 mcg/kg/min iv over 1 minute followed by esmolol infusion at 50 mcg/kg/min. titrate every 5 minutes by repeating 500 mcg/kg/min bolus over 1 minute and then increasing infusion by 50 mcg/kg/min. maximum dose 300 mcg/kg/min iii. labetalol 10-20 mg iv over 2 minutes. may repeat every 10 minutes until goal blood pressure obtained or maximum total dose of 300mg. avoid if hr < 60 bpm. page 1 of 3 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: subarachnoid hemorrhage, non-traumatic guideline number: guid-3203-m013 i. treat hypotension aggressively. if map < 70mmhg with mental status changes, refer to per guid3203-m011 - hypotension. j. if patient taking anticoagulants, consider reversal agents. i. attempt to determine timing of last anticoagulant dose ii. obtain copies of appropriate baseline coagulation studies such as: pt, aptt, inr, teg, anti-xa iii. continue appropriately ordered reversal agent for patients on anticoagulant medications (anti-inhibitor coagulant complex [feiba], idarucizumab [praxbind], vitamin k, coagulation factor xa recombinant [andexanet alpha]) per transferring or receiving facility orders. iv. for patients taking warfarin with an inr > 1.4, consider with sending physician 1. vitamin k 1-2.5 mg po or 10 mg iv if no prosthetic heart valve 2. feiba 1000 units/20 ml iv over 15 minutes 3. fresh frozen plasma 2 units iv over 15 minutes v. for patients taking oral factor xa inhibitors such as apixaban (eliquis), edoxaban (sayvaysa), or rivaroxaban (xarelto), consider with sending physician 1. feiba 2000 units/40 ml iv over 15 minutes 2. tranexamic acid 1 gram iv over 10 minutes vi. if feiba was ordered at sending facility: 1. wait for the feiba medication before transferring the patient. if the patient is at the point of herniating, call the receiving physician to discuss if they would prefer you to transfer without delay. 2. the iv access used for feiba is dedicated to only feiba for time of administration. 3. ensure the md/rn at receiving facility is aware of the time that feiba was given. 4. observe for infusion reactions, thromboembolic events, & allergic reactions. vii. for patients taking dabigatran (pradaxa), consider with sending physician 1. idarucizumab 2.5 grams iv every 10 minutes x 2 doses k. for interfacility transfers only and proven by ct scan: if signs of cerebral herniation caused by increased intracranial pressure from known intracranial hemorrhage i. signs and symptoms include unilateral dilated pupil, nonreactive pupil, decreased in gcs of 2 or more in a patient with a gcs less than 9, and/or spontaneous posturing, ct scan showing herniation or significant midline shift. ii. assess for other causes of hypoxia, hypotension, or over-sedation. iii. hyperventilate to an etco2 of 30-35 mmhg iv. administer hypertonic 3% saline 500 ml iv over 10 minutes for adult patients. v. if a sending facility prefers, consider obtaining and administering mannitol 1 gram/kg iv over 5-10 minutes using a filter on iv tubing. l. if seizures are present, treat per guid3203-m015 - seizures. m. for confirmed intracranial hemorrhage in the immediate post-hemorrhagic phase, administer levetiracetam 1 gram iv over 2-5 minutes as a prophylactic anticonvulsant n. document nimodipine dose if previously received from sending facility. iv. documentation: page 2 of 3 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: subarachnoid hemorrhage, non-traumatic guideline number: guid-3203-m013 a. ems charts v. references: page 3 of 3 © orlando health 2017

title: Pulmonary Embolism
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pulmonary embolism guideline number: guid-3203-m014 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to recognize the patient with pulmonary embolism (pe) and to provide and maintain adequate oxygenation, ventilation, and tissue perfusion. to provide emergent treatment and transport. ii. definitions: iii. department guideline: a. establish care as defined by guid3203-g002 - general patient care. b. assess patient for: i. massive/submassive pe: hypotension, poor perfusion to the extremities, tachycardia, tachypnea, hypoxia, signs of right heart strain (right axis ekg, bedside sonogram/ct findings, elevated troponin). ii. acute pulmonary infarction: localized tenderness, signs of pleural effusion, and diminished breath sounds iii. embolism without infarction: tachypnea, tachycardia, pleuritic pain, crackles, or wheeze iv. multiple pulmonary emboli or thrombi: pulmonary hypertension and cor pulmonale a. treat hypoxia with supplemental oxygen (goal spo2 > 90%) i. if severe respiratory distress treat per guid3203-g008 - rapid sequence intubation b. treat hypotension per guid3203-m011 - hypotension. c. consider anticoagulation therapy. obtain orders and medication from sending/receiving physician if no contraindications. heparin iv bolus 80 units/kg (max 5000 units) followed by heparin infusion at 18 units/kg/hr (max 1300 units/hr). do not start heparin if the patient has received lovenox. d. for persistent hypotension despite management with the preceding measures, consider thrombolytic therapy (per sending physician) prior to departing the referring facility. e. treat pain and/or anxiety per guid3203-g005 - analgesia and sedation management iv. documentation: ems charts v. references: page 1 of 1 © orlando health 2017

title: Seizures
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: seizures guideline number: guid-3203-m015 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to recognize the patient with a seizure disorder and prevent active seizing. assure adequate oxygenation, ventilation, tissue perfusion, and provide expeditious transport to the appropriate medical facility. ii. definitions: iii. department guideline: a. establish care as defined by guid3203-g002 - general patient care. b. prepare a safe environment for the patient. c. perform a blood glucose check. if < 60 mg/dl treat per guid3203-m021 hypoglycemia. d. if patient is actively seizing i. midazolam 5 mg iv/in or 10 mg im. repeat as necessary q 2-5 minutes up to 20 mg. -or- ii. lorazepam 2 mg iv/im (if available). repeat as necessary q 2-5 minutes up to 6 mg. e. if multiple doses of benzodiazepines given and patient is still in status epilepticus, consider initiating one of the following anticonvulsant drugs i. levetiracetam 60 mg/kg iv. maximum dose 4.5 grams over 5 minutes. this is the preferred second line agent after benzodiazepines. for doses >1000 mg, dilute vials in 100 ml of 0.9% ns and administer over 15 minutes. ii. phenytoin 15-20 mg/kg iv infusion at 25-50 mg/min iii. fosphenytoin 15-20 mg pe/kg iv infusion at 100-150 mg pe/min f. if seizure activity continues, consider pentobarbital 10-20 mg/kg iv infused over 30 minutes, followed by pentobarbital infusion at 1mg/kg/hr g. consider rapid sequence intubation per guid3203-g008 rapid sequence intubation. i. if intubation required, use short acting neuro-muscular blockers as long acting neuromuscular blockade will mask physical evidence of seizures. ii. consider placement of an orogastric or nasogastric tube per guid3203-pr010 gastric tube insertion to decrease risk of aspiration. iv. documentation: ems charts v. references: page 1 of 1 © orlando health 2017

general patient care. b. prepare a safe environment for the patient. c. perform a blood glucose check. if < 60 mg/dl treat per guid3203-m021 hypoglycemia. d. if patient is actively seizing i. midazolam 5 mg iv/in or 10 mg im. repeat as necessary q 2-5 minutes up to 20 mg. -or- ii. lorazepam 2 mg iv/im (if available). repeat as necessary q 2-5 minutes up to 6 mg. e. if multiple doses of benzodiazepines given and patient is still in status epilepticus, consider initiating one of the following anticonvulsant drugs i. levetiracetam 60 mg/kg iv. maximum dose 4.5 grams over 5 minutes. this is the preferred second line agent after benzodiazepines. for doses >1000 mg, dilute vials in 100 ml of 0.9% ns and administer over 15 minutes. ii. phenytoin 15-20 mg/kg iv infusion at 25-50 mg/min iii. fosphenytoin 15-20 mg pe/kg iv infusion at 100-150 mg pe/min f. if seizure activity continues, consider pentobarbital 10-20 mg/kg iv infused over 30 minutes, followed by pentobarbital infusion at 1mg/kg/hr g. consider rapid sequence intubation per guid3203-g008 rapid sequence intubation. i. if intubation required, use short acting neuro-muscular blockers as long acting neuromuscular blockade will mask physical evidence of seizures. ii. consider placement of an orogastric or nasogastric tube per guid3203-pr010 gastric tube insertion to decrease risk of aspiration. iv. documentation: ems charts v. references: page 1 of 1 © orlando health 2017

title: Sepsis
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: sepsis guideline number: guid-3203-m016 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to provide adequate airway management, hemodynamic support with fluid resuscitation and vasoactive medications as needed, as well as continue antibiotic treatment. ii. definitions: a. map - mean arterial pressure b. cvp - central venous pressure c. pa catheter - pulmonary artery catheter d. pcwp - pulmonary capillary wedge pressure e. svr - systemic vascular resistance f. co - cardiac output g. ci - cardiac index iii. department guideline: a. establish care as defined by guid3203-g002 - general patient care. b. obtain history including recent lab work/meds. note initial lactate. c. assess patient's core temperature if available, neurological status, and peripheral perfusion. d. assess hemodynamic status. if pa catheter is present, obtain or record last pcwp, svr, co/ci and monitor waveform. e. if febrile, administer acetaminophen 10-15 mg/kg po/pr (max 1,000mg) from sending facility. f. if necessary, secure an advanced airway per guid3203-g008 rapid sequence induction for endotracheal intubation and guid3203-pr020 endotracheal intubation and ventilate with a transport ventilator per guid3203-pr024 ventilation with a mechanical ventilator. g. if hypoxia persists in an intubated/mechanically ventilated patient, consider adding peep to improve spo2 unless patient is profoundly hypotensive. h. maintain map >65 mmhg and cvp > 8 mmhg (cvp > 12 mmhg if mechanically ventilated). i. if signs of inadequate perfusion, begin resuscitation with 0.9% ns or lactated ringers per guid3203-m011 - hypotension. initial volume resuscitation goals begin at 30 ml/kg in septic shock unless contraindicated. ii. monitor fluid intake and urine output if foley catheter is in place. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: sepsis guideline number: guid-3203-m016 iii. if cvp adequate/unknown, but map is low despite fluid resuscitation, start vasopressors guid3203-m011 - hypotension. 1. norepinephrine infusion at 0.05-1 mcg/kg/min, titrate q5 min to map > 65 mmhg. if bp not responsive to norepinephrine, consider adding: 2. epinephrine infusion at 0.05-2 mcg/kg/min, titrate q5 min to map > 65 mmhg 3. vasopressin iv 0.03 units/min 4. for hypotension refractory to vasopressors, consider hydrocortisone 100 mg iv from sending facility i. broad spectrum antimicrobials should be administered and continued by the medical transport team. consult with the sending/receiving physician regarding appropriate antimicrobials. j. verify blood glucose level. if <60mg/dl treat per guid3203-m021 - hypoglycemia. iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Stroke or Transient Ischemic Attack
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: stroke guideline number: guid-3203-m017 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to recognize the patient presenting with acute stroke or tia; to provide and maintain adequate oxygenation, ventilation, and end-organ tissue perfusion; and to provide emergent care and expeditious transport to the nearest appropriate medical facility. ii. definitions: a. cpp - cerebral perfusion pressure b. etco2 - end-tidal carbon dioxide c. gcs - glasgow coma scale d. icp - intracranial pressure e. loc - level of consciousness f. nihss - national institutes of health stroke scale g. tia - transient ischemic attack h. tpa - tissue plasminogen activator (alteplase), a thrombolytic agent iii. pathophysiology: a. stroke should be considered in any patient presenting with an acute neurologic deficit (focal or global) or altered level of consciousness (loc). no feature distinguishes ischemic from hemorrhagic stroke, although nausea, vomiting, headache, and change in loc are more common in hemorrhagic strokes. common symptoms of stroke include abrupt onset of hemiparesis, monoparesis, or quadriparesis; monocular or binocular visual loss; visual field deficits; diplopia; dysarthria; ataxia; vertigo; aphasia; or sudden decrease in loc. b. establishing the time of onset of symptoms is of paramount importance when considering possible thrombolytic and interventional therapy. c. definitive care for ischemic stroke is reperfusion therapy, whether it be a systemic thrombolytic given intravenously, local thrombolytic given intra-arterially, or clot retrieval performed via endovascular intervention. iv. physical exam a. air care team members must be able to perform a brief but accurate neurologic examination using the cincinnati stroke scale and/or the national institutes of health stroke scale (nihss) and establish a neurologic baseline should the patient's condition improve or deteriorate. page 1 of 5 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: stroke guideline number: guid-3203-m017 cincinnati stroke scale cpss normal abnormal facial droop the patient shows teeth or smiles. both sides of face move equally. one side does not move as well as other. arm drift the patient closes their eyes and extends both arms straight for 10 seconds. both arms move the same, or both arms do not move at all. one arm either does not move, or one arm drifts down compared to the other. speech the patient repeats, "the sky is blue in cincinnati". the patient says correct words with no slurring of words. the patient slurs words, says the wrong words, or is unable to speak. v. department guideline: a. establish care as defined by guid3203-g002 - general patient care. b. complete a physical neurological exam to include time of onset of symptoms, pupil size and reaction, and cincinnati stroke scale and/or the nihss. i. if at a sending facility, obtain baseline nihss score and monitor for neurological changes. i. examination should include detecting extracranial causes and distinguishing stroke from stroke mimics (i.e. hypoglycemia, seizure, systemic infection, brain tumor, toxic/metabolic disorders, positional vertigo, conversion disorder). c. administer oxygen via nasal cannula if o2 saturation is < 92% or if respiratory complaints. d. secure peripheral iv access. (preferred large-bore access in the antecubital fossa for ct scan). e. keep hob at 30 degrees and neck midline if possible. f. if necessary, secure an advanced airway per guid3203-g008 rapid sequence induction for endotracheal intubation and guid3203-pr020 endotracheal intubation and ventilate with a transport ventilator per guid3203-pr024 ventilation with a mechanical ventilator. i. consider short-acting induction and muscle relaxant agents. ii. try to avoid peep >5 cm h2o to minimize increases in icp. iii. ventilate to maintain paco2 35-40 mmhg. g. perform a glucose check and if glucose is < 60 mg/dl, administer medications per guid3203- m021 hypoglycemia. h. if sbp < 90 mmhg, map < 65 mmhg, or signs of inadequate perfusion, begin resuscitation with 0.9% ns per guid3203-m011 - hypotension. i. obtain family/contact information for the person who last saw the patient normally for the receiving facility in case additional information is needed regarding the history of events or for a relative who can assist with medical decision making. j. hypertensive blood pressure management i. for scene suspected ischemic or hemorrhagic stroke page 2 of 5 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: stroke guideline number: guid-3203-m017 1. allow permissive hypertension up to sbp 220 mmhg until ct scan confirms diagnosis. ii. for inter-facility confirmed stroke (transfers) 1. confirmed hemorrhagic stroke a. goal sbp 120-140 mmhg and map > 65 mmhg i. nicardipine infusion at 5mg/h. increase by 2.5mg/h iv every 5-15 minutes to maximum of 15mg/h. titrate to goal above ii. labetalol 10-20 mg iv over 2 minutes. may repeat every 10 minutes until goal blood pressure obtained or maximum total dose of 300mg. avoid if hr < 60 bpm. b. consider pain medication per guid3203-g005 analgesia and sedation management and apply etco2 monitoring. c. if icp monitor in place, maintain cpp between 70-100 mmhg. 2. confirmed ischemic stroke a. if tpa is not administered: permissive hypertension sbp up to 220 mmhg b. if tpa is administered: goal sbp 140-180 mmhg / dbp<105 i. nicardipine infusion at 5mg/h. increase by 2.5mg/h iv every 5-15 minutes to maximum of 15mg/h. titrate to goals above ii. labetalol 10-20 mg iv over 2 minutes. may repeat every 10 minutes until goal blood pressure obtained or maximum total dose of 300mg. avoid if hr < 60 bpm. c. if hypertension persists, contact receiving physician or treat patient per guid3203-m010 - hypertensive emergency. d. administer vasopressors for hypotension per guid3203-m011 - hypotension. k. if patient is receiving tpa: i. review orders with sending rn and continue infusion en-route ii. monitor for signs/symptoms/complications, specifically intracranial hemorrhage iii. discontinue tpa if: 1. development of new severe headache 2. development of significant bleeding (observed) 3. development of angioedema 4. development of sudden hypotension (suggesting internal hemorrhage) 5. blood pressure > 180/105 despite aggressive intervention 6. sudden change in mental status defined as decrease in gcs of ≥ 3 points from baseline eye and verbal or > 2 points for motor response iv. flush tpa infusion tubing at the end of infusion to administer the complete dose of thrombolytic with 0.9% ns. l. for hemorrhagic stroke patients taking anticoagulants i. attempt to determine timing of last anticoagulant dose page 3 of 5 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: stroke guideline number: guid-3203-m017 ii. obtain copies of appropriate baseline coagulation studies such as: pt, aptt, inr, teg, anti-xa iii. continue appropriately ordered reversal agent for patients on anticoagulant medications (anti-inhibitor coagulant complex [feiba], idarucizumab [praxbind], vitamin k, coagulation factor xa recombinant [andexanet alpha]) per transferring or receiving facility orders. iv. for patients taking warfarin with an inr > 1.4, consider with sending physician 1. vitamin k 1-2.5 mg po or 10 mg iv if no prosthetic heart valve 2. feiba 1000 units/20 ml iv over 15 minutes 3. fresh frozen plasma 2 units iv over 15 minutes v. for patients taking oral factor xa inhibitors such as apixaban (eliquis), edoxaban (sayvaysa), or rivaroxaban (xarelto), consider with sending physician 1. feiba 2000 units/40 ml iv over 15 minutes 2. tranexamic acid 1 gram iv over 10 minutes vi. if feiba was ordered at sending facility: 1. wait for the feiba medication before transferring the patient. if the patient is at the point of herniating, call the receiving physician to discuss if they would prefer you to transfer without delay. 2. the iv access used for feiba is dedicated to only feiba for time of administration. 3. ensure the md/rn at receiving facility is aware of the time that feiba was given. 4. observe for infusion reactions, thromboembolic events, & allergic reactions. vii. for patients taking dabigatran (pradaxa), consider with sending physician 1. idarucizumab 2.5 grams iv every 10 minutes x 2 doses m. for evidence of significantly elevated intracranial pressure and impending cerebral herniation: i. if patient has gcs  8 plus at least one of the following: 1. unilateral fixed and dilated pupil 2. unilateral paralysis 3. decerebrate posturing a. administer hypertonic 3% saline i. adult: 3% ns 500 ml iv over 10-30 minutes b. if interfacilty and sending facility prefers, consider obtaining and administering mannitol 1 gram/kg iv over 5-10 minutes using a filter on iv tubing if adequate blood pressures. c. controlled hyperventilation can be considered to decrease etco2 30-35 mmhg d. for active seizures, treat per guid3203-m015 - seizures. i. if there are no active seizures and time permits, administer: 1. adult: levetiracetam 1 gram iv over 2-5 minutes as a prophylactic anticonvulsant n. transport patient to the nearest comprehensive stroke center (as certified by the joint commission or dnv gl). page 4 of 5 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: stroke guideline number: guid-3203-m017 i. interfacility transport of patients known to require thrombectomy or scene calls with high likelihood of large vessel occlusion may be transported to the nearest thrombectomy- capable primary stroke center if there are operational barriers to reaching a comprehensive stroke center. 1. central florida comprehensive stroke centers: advent health-orlando, advent health-tampa, bayfront health st. petersburg, brandon regional hospital, lakeland regional medical center, ocala regional medical center, orlando regional medical center, osceola regional medical center, uf health shands gainesville, st. joseph's hospital tampa, tampa general hospital 2. central florida thrombectomy capable stroke centers: halifax medical center, holmes regional medical center, winter haven hospital ii. if patient meets both trauma alert and stroke alert criteria, transport to a facility which is a both a comprehensive stroke center and state approved trauma center. iii. any patient with suspicion for ruptured aneurysm or subarachnoid hemorrhage (thunderclap headache, altered mental status, non-focal neuro exam) should be transported to the nearest comprehensive stroke center o. notify receiving facility as soon as possible with a "stroke alert" to activate stroke team. p. if seizures are present, treat per guid3203-m015 - seizures. q. treat nausea/vomiting per guid3203-g004 - motion sickness/nausea. r. avoid d5w or dextrose-based fluids. vi. documentation: ems charts vii. references: a. cook am, jones gm, hawryluk gwj, et al. guidelines for the acute treatment of cerebral edema in neurocritical care patients. neurocritical care. 2020. b. frontera ja, lewin jj, rabinstein aa, et.al. guideline for reversal of antithrombotics in intracranial hemorrhage: executive summary. a statement for healthcare professionals from the neurocritical care society and the society of critical care medicine. crit care med. 2016;44:2251-7. c. holbrook a, schulman s, witt dm, et.al. evidence-based management of anticoagulant therapy. antithrombotic therapy and prevention of thrombosis, 9th ed: american college of chest physicians evidence-based clinical practice guidelines. chest. 2012;141(2)(suppl):e152s-184s. d. meyran, d., cassan, p., avau, b., singletary, e., & sideman, d. a. (2020). stroke recognition for first air providers: a systematic review and meta-analysis. cureus. nov; 12(11): e11386. e. warfarin reversal. ormc surgical critical care guideline. updated 11/29/2017. page 5 of 5 © orlando health 2017

pages.text: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: stroke guideline number: guid-3203-m017 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to recognize the patient presenting with acute stroke or tia; to provide and maintain adequate oxygenation, ventilation, and end-organ tissue perfusion; and to provide emergent care and expeditious transport to the nearest appropriate medical facility. ii. definitions: a. cpp - cerebral perfusion pressure b. etco2 - end-tidal carbon dioxide c. gcs - glasgow coma scale d. icp - intracranial pressure e. loc - level of consciousness f. nihss - national institutes of health stroke scale g. tia - transient ischemic attack h. tpa - tissue plasminogen activator (alteplase), a thrombolytic agent iii. pathophysiology: a. stroke should be considered in any patient presenting with an acute neurologic deficit (focal or global) or altered level of consciousness (loc). no feature distinguishes ischemic from hemorrhagic stroke, although nausea, vomiting, headache, and change in loc are more common in hemorrhagic strokes. common symptoms of stroke include abrupt onset of hemiparesis, monoparesis, or quadriparesis; monocular or binocular visual loss; visual field deficits; diplopia; dysarthria; ataxia; vertigo; aphasia; or sudden decrease in loc. b. establishing the time of onset of symptoms is of paramount importance when considering possible thrombolytic and interventional therapy. c. definitive care for ischemic stroke is reperfusion therapy, whether it be a systemic thrombolytic given intravenously, local thrombolytic given intra-arterially, or clot retrieval performed via endovascular intervention. iv. physical exam a. air care team members must be able to perform a brief but accurate neurologic examination using the cincinnati stroke scale and/or the national institutes of health stroke scale (nihss) and establish a neurologic baseline should the patient's condition improve or deteriorate. page 1 of 5 © orlando health 2017
