title: Thoracic Aortic Dissection
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: thoracic aortic dissection guideline number: guid-3203-m018 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to recognize the patient with a thoracic aortic dissection and prevent further extension of the dissection. to minimize the sheer forces placed on the aortic wall. ii. definitions: a. sbp - systolic blood pressure b. hr - heart rate iii. department guideline: a. establish care as defined by guid3203-g002 - general patient care b. obtain and record the classification: type a or type b c. bring any available ct scan reports and images from sending facility. d. assess for distal pulses and neurological deficits. obtain bilateral upper extremities bps. e. treat pain per guid3203-g005 analgesia and sedation management and anxiety per guid3203- g006 anxiety (combative patient management). f. goals: adequate end-organ perfusion with sbp < 120 mmhg and hr < 60 bpm. g. if sbp > 120 mmhg and hr > 60 bpm administer beta blockers i. esmolol bolus 500 mcg/kg/min iv over 1 minute followed by esmolol infusion at 50 mcg/kg/min. titrate every 5 minutes by repeating 500 mcg/kg/min bolus over 1 minute and then increasing infusion by 50 mcg/kg/min. maximum dose 300 mcg/kg/min ii. labetalol 10-20 mg iv over 2 minutes. may repeat every 10 minutes until goal blood pressure obtained or maximum total dose of 300mg. h. if sbp remains > 120 mmhg after β-blockers, or hr < 60 bpm prior to beta blockers administer: i. nicardipine infusion at 5 mg/h. increase by 2.5 mg/h iv every 5-15 minutes to maximum of 15 mg/h. ii. nitroprusside (50 mg/250ml d5w) infusion at 0.3 mcg/kg/min. the infusion can be increased by 0.5 mcg/kg/min every 5 minutes until desired bp is reached. max dose 10 mcg/kg/min (avoid in renal failure). 1. treat reflex tachycardia from nitroprusside with labetalol or esmolol as above i. if hypotension with signs of end-organ hypoperfusion (change in mental status, oliguria, etc) i. discontinue all anti-hypertensive medications. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: thoracic aortic dissection guideline number: guid-3203-m018 ii. administer iv fluid as per guid3203-m011 - hypotension and consider the use of blood products per guid3203-pr002 blood product administration or guid3203-pr025 emergency blood product administration. iii. consider contacting managing physician for treatment guidelines for hypotension and use of vasopressors. j. patients with associated mi confirmed by ecg i. proceed as above with β-blockade. ii. avoid thrombolytics, heparin, and aspirin. iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Overdose, Poisoning, Toxic Exposure
normalizedText: guideline title: overdose, poisoning, & toxic exposure guideline number: guid-3203-m019 page 1 of 3 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to recognize the patient with an ingestion or exposure to a toxic substance and to provide and maintain adequate oxygenation/ventilation and tissue perfusion. to provide emergent treatment and expeditious transport to the nearest appropriate medical facility. toxic ingestion or exposure should be considered in the differential diagnosis of any unexplained symptoms or signs. ii. definitions: iii. department guideline: a. establish care as defined by guid3203-g002 - general patient care. b. attempt to obtain exact time of ingestion, amount and type of ingestion c. ensure the safety of emergency responders. d. have all patients decontaminated prior to treatment, as necessary. i. if eye or skin contamination: irrigate with copious amounts of ns.9% or tap water ii. dry chemicals should be brushed off prior to decontamination. e. treatment is supportive unless a specific toxicologic symptom complex is diagnosed. f. treat dysrhythmias according to guid3203-c001-c010 - cardiac guidelines. g. verify blood glucose check and if glucose is < 60 mg/dl and the patient is symptomatic, treat per guid3203-m021 - hypoglycemia. h. if patient displays signs of inadequate perfusion treat per guid3203-m011 - hypotension. i. treat seizures per guid3203-m015 - seizures. j. for suspected opiate overdose i. adult & pediatric > 20 kg: naloxone 0.4 to 2 mg iv/im or 2 mg in. repeat q 3-5 minutes. fentanyl overdoses may require high total doses (8-10 mg) ii. pediatric <20kg: naloxone 0.01-0.1 mg/kg iv/in. maximum single dose 2mg. k. consider contacting local poison control center 1-800-222-1222. administer known antidote per direction of medical control or poison control center. l. consider sedation if agitated per the guid3203-g006 management for the anxiety/ combative patient. m. see chart below for specific management and discuss with sending/receiving physician. iv. documentation: guideline title: overdose, poisoning, & toxic exposure guideline number: guid-3203-m019 page 2 of 3 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline a. ems charts v. references: specific drug toxicities drug or poison source signs/symptoms management with physician order acetaminophen ingestion of 150 mg/kg or 7.5 g is potentially toxic tylenol, (comtrex, contact, darvocet-n, nyquil, sinutab) - initially little to no s & s - malaise, nausea, & vomiting. - late signs: abdominal pain & signs of hepatic injury/necroses. - serum levels: draw 4 hours after ingestion. - n-acetylcysteine (mucomyst) 150 mg/kg loading followed by 50 mg/kg next 4hrs iv asa toxic dose 200-300 mg/kg, 500 mg/kg potentially lethal aspirin, many otc analgesics, oil of wintergreen - gastritis, respiratory alkalosis, metabolic acidosis, ards, late cns depression - activated charcoal 1 gram/kg po up to 50 gms - treat dehydration, replace k+ - hemodialysis if decreased loc, pulmonary manifestations, and acid base imbalance - use caution w/ tracheal intubation & reserve for respiratory failure. anticholinergics antihistamines, tca's, allerest, phenothiazines, sominex, contac, - agitation, anxiety, and hallucinations, dilated pupils, seizures, tachycardia, decreased secretions - symptomatic therapy - physostigmine 1-2 mg iv over 20 min very short acting total dose of 6mg can be given - treat cardiac arrhythmias β-blockers lopressor, propanolol - bradycardia with avb, hypotension, decreased loc, hypoglycemia, seizure, pulmonary edema - supportive care - for bradycardia: atropine 0.5-1mg iv - for hypotension: epinephrine 0.1-0.5 mcg/kg/min & glucagon 0.1 mg/kg iv (max 10mg) load followed by 2-5 mg/hr infusion. peds 0.1mg/kg iv. - consider high dose insulin therapy (1 unit/kg gtt) with glucose supplementation as needed to keep bgl > 100 - continue lipid emulsion therapy 0.25-0.5 ml/kg per hour if began by sending facility benzodiazepines valium, librium, dalmane - drowsiness, ataxia, nystagmus, slurred speech, respiratory depression, hypotension -supportive care. flumazenil contraindicated for benzo overdose due to risk of seizures calcium channel blockers amlodipine/norvasc, diltiazem/cardizem, tiazac, felodipine, isradipine, nicardipine, nifedipine/procardia, nisoldipine/sular, verapamil - bradycardia, hypotension, respiratory depression, n/v - supportive care - for hypotension: calcium chloride 1 g iv can repeat in 10 min and glucagon 0.1 mg/kg iv load followed by 2-5 mg/hr infusion - calcium gluconate 1 gram or 60mg/kg/dose slow iv over 5 minutes. may repeat every 10 minutes. - consider high dose insulin therapy (1 unit/kg gtt) with glucose supplementation as needed to keep bgl > 100 - continue lipid emulsion therapy 0.25-0.5 ml/kg per hour if began by sending facility carbon monoxide - common: headache, dizziness, nausea, vomiting, chest pain, weakness, and ams. - 100% oxygen via non-rebreather - intubate if unconscious guideline title: overdose, poisoning, & toxic exposure guideline number: guid-3203-m019 page 3 of 3 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline - severe: malaise, sob, ha, nausea, cp, irritability, ataxia, ams, loc, coma, death; tachycardia, impaired memory, tachypnea, hypotension, cognitive sensory disturbances; metabolic acidosis, arrhythmias, mi, and pulmonary edema cocaine & methamphetamine - vasoconstriction, tachycardia, htn, rf, increased myocardial o2 demand, coma, seizures, supraventricular & ventricular dysrhythmias, agitation, ha, intracranial hemorrhage, muscle pain, compartment syndrome, rhabdomyolysis, hyperkalemia - large doses of benzodiazepines - beta-blockers contraindicated - avoid succinylcholine digoxin lanoxicaps, lanoxin, - nausea, vomiting, hyperkalemia, brady dysrhythmias hr<60 or atrioventricular block, tachydysrhythmias v-tach v-fib, atrial tachycardia with 2:1 block - digibind is antidote - watch patient for hyperkalemia - do not give calcium chloride or calcium gluconate ethylene glycol as little as 2ml/kg is fatal dose antifreeze, coolant - cns depression, vomiting, abdominal pain, late high anion gap acidosis, seizure, coma, rf, calcium oxalate crystals in urine - supportive care - ethanol infusion start at 1000 mg/kg/hr for 1-2 hrs then 100 mg/kg/hr to keep ethanol level at 100-130 mg/dl organophosphates - muscarinic effects: bradycardia, bronch- orrhea, respiratory distress, visual disturbance, and sludge syndrome (salivation, lacrimation, urination, defecation, gastrointestinal, and emesis) - nicotinic effects: tachycardia, muscle fasciculation with weakness or paralysis, hypertension -cns effects- headache, ams, anxiety, lethargy, coma - atropine 2mg or 0.5mg/kg iv, repeated every 2 minutes until reversal of symptoms - pralidoxime (2-pam) 600mg (or 1 auto- injector im) or 20-50mg/kg iv for pediatric patients. may repeat x2 to total of 1,800mg. opiates dilaudid, fentanyl, methadone, demerol - pinpoint pupils, respiratory depression, coma, ards - supportive care - narcan 0.4-2 mg iv/in, may need infusion (0.4-0.8 mg/hr to max of 1.2 mg/hr) sodium channel blocker tricyclic antidepressant (tca) amitriptyline, dosepin, imipramine, protriptyline - anticholinergic symptoms, delirium to coma, dilated pupils, dry membranes, svt, conduction disturbances, prolonged qt, hypotension - serious toxicity usually within 6 hrs of ingestion - if arrhythmias & hypotension: sodium bicarb 50 meq in 250ml d5w iv over 1 hour may require infusion (keep ph @ 7.45-7.55) - hypotension: iv fluid bolus then norepinephrine or epinephrine infusion - benzodiazepines for seizures

pages.text: guideline title: overdose, poisoning, & toxic exposure guideline number: guid-3203-m019 page 1 of 3 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to recognize the patient with an ingestion or exposure to a toxic substance and to provide and maintain adequate oxygenation/ventilation and tissue perfusion. to provide emergent treatment and expeditious transport to the nearest appropriate medical facility. toxic ingestion or exposure should be considered in the differential diagnosis of any unexplained symptoms or signs. ii. definitions: iii. department guideline: a. establish care as defined by guid3203-g002 - general patient care. b. attempt to obtain exact time of ingestion, amount and type of ingestion c. ensure the safety of emergency responders. d. have all patients decontaminated prior to treatment, as necessary. i. if eye or skin contamination: irrigate with copious amounts of ns.9% or tap water ii. dry chemicals should be brushed off prior to decontamination. e. treatment is supportive unless a specific toxicologic symptom complex is diagnosed. f. treat dysrhythmias according to guid3203-c001-c010 - cardiac guidelines. g. verify blood glucose check and if glucose is < 60 mg/dl and the patient is symptomatic, treat per guid3203-m021 - hypoglycemia. h. if patient displays signs of inadequate perfusion treat per guid3203-m011 - hypotension. i. treat seizures per guid3203-m015 - seizures. j. for suspected opiate overdose i. adult & pediatric > 20 kg: naloxone 0.4 to 2 mg iv/im or 2 mg in. repeat q 3-5 minutes. fentanyl overdoses may require high total doses (8-10 mg) ii. pediatric <20kg: naloxone 0.01-0.1 mg/kg iv/in. maximum single dose 2mg. k. consider contacting local poison control center 1-800-222-1222. administer known antidote per direction of medical control or poison control center. l. consider sedation if agitated per the guid3203-g006 management for the anxiety/ combative patient. m. see chart below for specific management and discuss with sending/receiving physician. iv. documentation:

guideline title: overdose, poisoning, & toxic exposure guideline number: guid-3203-m019 page 2 of 3 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline a. ems charts v. references: specific drug toxicities drug or poison source signs/symptoms management with physician order acetaminophen ingestion of 150 mg/kg or 7.5 g is potentially toxic tylenol, (comtrex, contact, darvocet-n, nyquil, sinutab) - initially little to no s & s - malaise, nausea, & vomiting. - late signs: abdominal pain & signs of hepatic injury/necroses. - serum levels: draw 4 hours after ingestion. - n-acetylcysteine (mucomyst) 150 mg/kg loading followed by 50 mg/kg next 4hrs iv asa toxic dose 200-300 mg/kg, 500 mg/kg potentially lethal aspirin, many otc analgesics, oil of wintergreen - gastritis, respiratory alkalosis, metabolic acidosis, ards, late cns depression - activated charcoal 1 gram/kg po up to 50 gms - treat dehydration, replace k+ - hemodialysis if decreased loc, pulmonary manifestations, and acid base imbalance - use caution w/ tracheal intubation & reserve for respiratory failure. anticholinergics antihistamines, tca's, allerest, phenothiazines, sominex, contac, - agitation, anxiety, and hallucinations, dilated pupils, seizures, tachycardia, decreased secretions - symptomatic therapy - physostigmine 1-2 mg iv over 20 min very short acting total dose of 6mg can be given - treat cardiac arrhythmias β-blockers lopressor, propanolol - bradycardia with avb, hypotension, decreased loc, hypoglycemia, seizure, pulmonary edema - supportive care - for bradycardia: atropine 0.5-1mg iv - for hypotension: epinephrine 0.1-0.5 mcg/kg/min & glucagon 0.1 mg/kg iv (max 10mg) load followed by 2-5 mg/hr infusion. peds 0.1mg/kg iv. - consider high dose insulin therapy (1 unit/kg gtt) with glucose supplementation as needed to keep bgl > 100 - continue lipid emulsion therapy 0.25-0.5 ml/kg per hour if began by sending facility benzodiazepines valium, librium, dalmane - drowsiness, ataxia, nystagmus, slurred speech, respiratory depression, hypotension -supportive care. flumazenil contraindicated for benzo overdose due to risk of seizures calcium channel blockers amlodipine/norvasc, diltiazem/cardizem, tiazac, felodipine, isradipine, nicardipine, nifedipine/procardia, nisoldipine/sular, verapamil - bradycardia, hypotension, respiratory depression, n/v - supportive care - for hypotension: calcium chloride 1 g iv can repeat in 10 min and glucagon 0.1 mg/kg iv load followed by 2-5 mg/hr infusion - calcium gluconate 1 gram or 60mg/kg/dose slow iv over 5 minutes. may repeat every 10 minutes. - consider high dose insulin therapy (1 unit/kg gtt) with glucose supplementation as needed to keep bgl > 100 - continue lipid emulsion therapy 0.25-0.5 ml/kg per hour if began by sending facility carbon monoxide - common: headache, dizziness, nausea, vomiting, chest pain, weakness, and ams. - 100% oxygen via non-rebreather - intubate if unconscious

title: Malignant Hyperthermia
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: malignant hyperthermia guideline number: guid-3203-m020 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: recognize malignant hyperthermia, a life-threatening disorder of the skeletal muscle, which can be triggered by inhalational anesthetics and succinylcholine. ii. definitions: iii. department guideline: a. establish care as defined by guid3203-g002 - general patient care. b. assess for hypoxemia, tachycardia/cardiac dysrhythmias, muscle rigidity or failure of the masseter muscles to relax for intubation, hot dry skin/hyperthermia, hypotension, sustained tetanic muscle rigidity/contractions, hypermetabolism, muscle cell destruction, hyperkalemia, tachypnea from severe respiratory and metabolic acidosis, & cyanotic mottling of the skin. c. stop all paralytics/anesthetic agents. d. hyperventilate with 100% oxygen to flush volatile anesthetics and lower etco2. e. initiate immediate cooling. f. infuse 0.9% ns iv with a urinary output goal of >1 ml/kg/hr. g. if at a sending facility, consult with physician and request dantrolene 2.5mg/kg iv. i. administer rapidly through a large-bore iv if possible. ii. dantrium/revonto brand: each 20 mg vial of should be reconstituted by adding 60 ml of sterile water for injection and the vial shaken until the solution is clear (20 seconds). 1. reminder: this drug contains 3 grams of mannitol in each vial iii. ryanodex brand: each 250 mg vial should be reconstituted with 5 ml of sterile water and shaken to ensure orange-colored uniform, opaque suspension. iv. repeat as frequently & as needed until patient responds with a decrease in etco2, muscle rigidity, and/or heart rate. large doses (> 10 mg/kg) may be required if persistent rigidity. if giving large doses (> 10 mg/kg) without symptom resolution, consider alternative diagnoses. h. if actively transporting a patient, divert to the nearest facility with capability of administering immediate dose of dantrolene. iv. documentation: ems charts v. references: a. malignant hyperthermia association of the united states. (2020). www.mhaus.org page 1 of 1 © orlando health 2017

title: General Obstetric Patient Assessment
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 standing orders guideline title: general obstetric patient assessment guideline number: guid-3203-m022 issue date: 7/1/2024 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/23/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to provide guidelines for the assessment, treatment, and transport of a pregnant patient, including those in active labor and potential complications of pregnancy. the goal is to optimize the outcome for the mother and baby by minimizing the risk of in-flight deliveries. ii. considerations for transport a. for purposes of safety, the transport team will consider a viable pregnancy as any pregnancy over 20 weeks gestation based on the best gestational age available. b. in the case of a pregnant woman with an emergency medical condition, the woman is considered stabilized when she has delivered the child and placenta. until then, the woman and child are considered unstable. iii. definitions: a. active labor (medical definition): the period when the cervix dilates from four to eight centimeters. contractions usually come between three and five minutes apart, dilating the cervix approximately one centimeter per hour. b. active labor (emtala definition): emtala defines "active labor" as labor at a time when i. there is inadequate time to effect safe transfer to another hospital prior to delivery ii. a transfer may pose a threat to the health and safety of the patient or the unborn child. iii. this statutory definition renders irrelevant any medical definition of active labor. it has retrospective implications (e.g., if a woman delivers during transport), she was previously in active labor. iv. equipment v. department guideline: a. establish care as defined by guid-3203-g002 - general patient care. b. pregnancy & trauma: i. transport all pregnant patients meeting trauma alert or trauma red criteria to a state approved trauma center ii. trauma yellow or green can be transported to the emergency department of closest ob receiving facility iii. treat per guid-3203-t017 trauma in pregnancy page 1 of 6 © orlando health 2022 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 standing orders guideline title: general obstetric patient assessment guideline number: guid-3203-m022 c. obtain maternal baseline vital signs and fetal heart rate. d. obtain history from sending facility as soon as possible. this must include the estimated gestational age (ega) based on last menstral period or recent ultrasound and estimated date of confinement (edc). it must also include information about contractions: i. onset. ii. frequency. iii. intensity - mild, moderate, strong. iv. duration - timed from the moment uterus begins to tighten until it relaxes. e. obtain maternal history: i. non-ob: allergies, medical/surgical history, medications. ii. ob history: pre-natal care, presence of more than one fetus, placenta previa, history of pre-term labor (ptl), any complications with this pregnancy, rupture of membranes, presence of meconium in amniotic fluid, recent exposure to infections, blood type and rh, time and amount of last oral intake, vaginal bleeding, fetal activity/movement felt, etc. iii. previous complications with other pregnancies. iv. high-risk conditions (e.g., pre-eclampsia, abruption, placenta previa, etc.). v. gravida & parity: g# (number of pregnancies); p# (number of pregnancies carried to > 20 weeks. also expressed as g # p# # # #. for parity, numbers stand for term births, premature births, abortions, and living children (in that order). vi. number of previous cesarean sections. f. the transport team is obligated to ask the sending facility/physician for the results of the medical screening exam which will aid in the diagnosis of an emergency medical condition. this should include the time of the last cervical check, effacement (shortening and thinning of the cervix expressed as a %), dilation (enlargement of cervical opening, anywhere from 1 cm to 10 cm) and station (presenting part of fetus: # cm above (-) or below (+) an imaginary line between maternal ischial spines (birth is +4 to +5) of the pregnancy. because of the changing nature of conditions in pregnancy, it may be necessary to ask for a repeat pelvic exam if not recently done. g. the medical transport team does not perform pelvic exams or cervical checks. this information should be obtained from the sending physician. when indicated, vaginal examination should be done at referring facility within 15 minutes of team arrival by referring physician or repeated before you leave. h. consult with medical direction i. if delivery is imminent, advisability of maternal transport should be questioned, and act should contact the receiving physician and/or medical control. ii. if significant possibility of an in-transport delivery exists, consult with the receiving physician and/or medical control (conferencing an obstetrical specialty physician medical director as needed). consultation should be sought to aid in decision-making regarding transport vs. delivery at the sending facility. in general, transport teams should err on the side of caution before transporting such patients as the delivery of the infant in the sending facility is almost always in the best interest of the patient and unborn child when compared to an in-transport delivery. page 2 of 6 © orlando health 2022 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 standing orders guideline title: general obstetric patient assessment guideline number: guid-3203-m022 iii. the following patients are not recommended for transport without a consultation with the receiving physician and/or medical control (conferencing an obstetrical specialty medical director as needed) 1. the mother in active labor whose cervix is dilated more than 5 cm. it should be noted that rapid progression of labor can occur, especially in multiparous mothers, so the flight team and the physicians involved may choose to be more conservative than this in some patients in active labor. 2. the mother in active labor who is less than 37 weeks gestation with ruptured membranes. 3. the mother in active labor whose membranes are ruptured and meconium staining is present. 4. contact the receiving physician and/or physician medical director (conferencing an obstetrical specialty physician medical director as needed) with any fetal heart rates under 100 beats per minute. iv. in general, aircraft should not be used to transport patients in active labor unless there are special circumstances. ground transport should be utilized unless directed by medical control. i. general transport i. preserve patient's warmth and privacy. ii. confirm that appropriately sized resuscitation equipment (for both mother and infant) is available and functioning. iii. secure the pregnant patient by positioning the safety straps low on the pelvic girdle. straps across the abdomen can lead to indirect trauma to the fetus in the event of significant turbulence or decelerations forces. iv. if possible, ensure patient has empty bladder prior to departure. v. if delivery occurs during a flight, every attempt should be made to land the aircraft at the nearest emergency department. j. interventions i. if patient has adequate spontaneous respirations, administer supplemental oxygen to maintain spo2 > 93%. ii. connect to cardiac monitor, nibp, pulse oximeter and etco2 (when indicated to maintain etco2 35 - 45 mmhg). iii. obtain a set of vital signs and repeat every 10 minutes, or more frequently depending on presentation. iv. establish iv access x 2 and consider blood draw for lab studies. v. consider maintenance fluids of 0.9% ns/lr at 100 - 150 ml/hr iv. vi. consider use of blood products for ongoing hemorrhage and hemodynamic instability per guid-pr002 - blood product administration or guid-pr025 - emergency blood product administration. observe the patient for signs and symptoms of disseminated intravascular coagulopathy. page 3 of 6 © orlando health 2022 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 standing orders guideline title: general obstetric patient assessment guideline number: guid-3203-m022 vii. if the patient has a gcs of < 9 and/or has a deteriorating level of consciousness, consider advanced airway management. viii. nauseated patients may be treated with anti-emetics per guid-3203-g004 nausea vomiting motion sickness ix. consider ng or og tube for gastric decompression per guid-3203-pr010 gastric tube insertion x. treat pain and anxiety per guid-3203-g005 analgesia and sedation management and guid-3203-g006 anxiety- combative patient management 1. use cautiously if delivery is imminent secondary to fetal respiratory depression. 2. report to the receiving unit when opioids are administered to the patient. xi. prolapsed umbilical cord 1. elevate mother's hips and place in knee to chest position 2. encourage mother not to push 3. evaluate fetal heart rate/cord pulsation 4. cover cord with moist sterile dressing 5. elevate pressure off cord, do not attempt to push the cord back into vagina k. pre-term labor i. consider insertion of indwelling urinary catheter to ensure bladder emptying as bladder distension may induce uterine contractions. ii. consider group b strep prophylaxis; discuss with the receiving physician and/or medical control to determine appropriate treatment. iii. consider steroid administration with betamethasone 12 mg im for lung maturity for gestational age < 37 weeks; discuss with the receiving physician iv. consult the receiving physician and/or medical director to consider tocolytic therapy 1. terbutaline sulfate 0.25 mg sq every 15 - 30 minutes up to a maximum of 1 mg or terbutaline infusion 5 mcg/min iv to a maximum 80 mcg/min iv a. contraindications to terbutaline sulfate: cardiac history, maternal hr > 140, maternal bp < 90/60 mmhg, fetal heart rate > 160, signs of maternal pulmonary edema. b. if the patient is on a terbutaline infusion at the referring facility, contact the receiving ob physician regarding their management. 2. magnesium sulfate: a. infuse 4 g iv bolus over 20 - 30 minutes, followed by 2 - 4 g/hr iv infusion. b. patients receiving magnesium sulfate infusion must be closely monitored for toxicity (somnolence, muscular paralysis, respiratory depression, hypotension, or loss of dtrs) i. immediately discontinue magnesium sulfate infusion. ii. administer calcium gluconate 1 g of 10% solution over 1 - 2 minutes. l. post-partum hemorrhage i. ensure 2 large bore ivs are patent. page 4 of 6 © orlando health 2022 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 standing orders guideline title: general obstetric patient assessment guideline number: guid-3203-m022 ii. for atonic uterus: 1. firmly massage uterine fundus. 2. continue oxytocin infusion from receiving facility (general recommendation 20 units/1,000 ml ns or lr at 20 - 40 milliunits/min (60 - 120 ml/hr) or if no iv access, oxytocin 10 units im) iii. for visible lacerations, apply direct pressure. iv. administer txa per guid-3203-t016 - tranexamic acid for severe hemorrhage. v. if hypotensive, place patient in the left or right lateral recumbent position and treat per guid-3203-m011 - hypotension. vi. for hemorrhagic shock that does not respond to warmed fluid resuscitation, administer blood products according to guid-3203-pr002 - blood product administration or guid- 3203-pr025 - emergency blood product administration. m. eclampsia and pre-eclampsia i. definitive treatment can only be accomplished through delivery of the fetus(es). ii. seizure prevention should be accomplished through administration of magnesium sulfate 1. magnesium sulfate: a. infuse 4 g iv bolus over 20 - 30 minutes, followed by 2 - 4 g/hr iv infusion. b. patients receiving magnesium sulfate infusion must be closely monitored for toxicity (somnolence, muscular paralysis, respiratory depression, hypotension, or loss of dtrs) i. immediately discontinue magnesium sulfate infusion. ii. administer calcium gluconate 1 g of 10% solution over 1 - 2 minutes. iii. if sbp > 160 mmhg or dbp is > 110 mmhg, administer: 1. labetalol 20 mg slow iv push (over 2 min). may repeat every 10 minutes with additional doses of 40 mg and then 80 mg, and then may repeat the 80 mg x 2 for the maximum total dose of 300 mg until dbp <100. 2. if bp is not responsive to labetalol then obtain from receiving facility and administer hydralazine 5 mg iv repeated as needed every 20 minutes at a dose of 5 - 10 mg iv to a total dose of 40 mg. iv. seizure activity should be treated with: 1. supportive care first (maintain airway, administer supplemental oxygen as needed to maintain spo2 > 93%, prevent injury). 2. as a first line treatment, administer magnesium sulfate 4 g iv bolus, then an infusion of 2 g/hr iv. 3. for seizure lasting greater than 2 minutes, administer a. midazolam 5 mg iv/in or 10 mg im; repeat as necessary every 2 - 5 minutes. b. for persistent seizures continue magnesium sulfate infusion and treat per guid-3203-m015 seizures vi. documentation: ems charts page 5 of 6 © orlando health 2022 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 standing orders guideline title: general obstetric patient assessment guideline number: guid-3203-m022 vii. references: page 6 of 6 © orlando health 2022

general patient care. b. pregnancy & trauma: i. transport all pregnant patients meeting trauma alert or trauma red criteria to a state approved trauma center ii. trauma yellow or green can be transported to the emergency department of closest ob receiving facility iii. treat per guid-3203-t017 trauma in pregnancy page 1 of 6 © orlando health 2022

1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 standing orders guideline title: general obstetric patient assessment guideline number: guid-3203-m022 c. obtain maternal baseline vital signs and fetal heart rate. d. obtain history from sending facility as soon as possible. this must include the estimated gestational age (ega) based on last menstral period or recent ultrasound and estimated date of confinement (edc). it must also include information about contractions: i. onset. ii. frequency. iii. intensity - mild, moderate, strong. iv. duration - timed from the moment uterus begins to tighten until it relaxes. e. obtain maternal history: i. non-ob: allergies, medical/surgical history, medications. ii. ob history: pre-natal care, presence of more than one fetus, placenta previa, history of pre-term labor (ptl), any complications with this pregnancy, rupture of membranes, presence of meconium in amniotic fluid, recent exposure to infections, blood type and rh, time and amount of last oral intake, vaginal bleeding, fetal activity/movement felt, etc. iii. previous complications with other pregnancies. iv. high-risk conditions (e.g., pre-eclampsia, abruption, placenta previa, etc.). v. gravida & parity: g# (number of pregnancies); p# (number of pregnancies carried to > 20 weeks. also expressed as g # p# # # #. for parity, numbers stand for term births, premature births, abortions, and living children (in that order). vi. number of previous cesarean sections. f. the transport team is obligated to ask the sending facility/physician for the results of the medical screening exam which will aid in the diagnosis of an emergency medical condition. this should include the time of the last cervical check, effacement (shortening and thinning of the cervix expressed as a %), dilation (enlargement of cervical opening, anywhere from 1 cm to 10 cm) and station (presenting part of fetus: # cm above (-) or below (+) an imaginary line between maternal ischial spines (birth is +4 to +5) of the pregnancy. because of the changing nature of conditions in pregnancy, it may be necessary to ask for a repeat pelvic exam if not recently done. g. the medical transport team does not perform pelvic exams or cervical checks. this information should be obtained from the sending physician. when indicated, vaginal examination should be done at referring facility within 15 minutes of team arrival by referring physician or repeated before you leave. h. consult with medical direction i. if delivery is imminent, advisability of maternal transport should be questioned, and act should contact the receiving physician and/or medical control. ii. if significant possibility of an in-transport delivery exists, consult with the receiving physician and/or medical control (conferencing an obstetrical specialty physician medical director as needed). consultation should be sought to aid in decision-making regarding transport vs. delivery at the sending facility. in general, transport teams should err on the side of caution before transporting such patients as the delivery of the infant in the sending facility is almost always in the best interest of the patient and unborn child when compared to an in-transport delivery. page 2 of 6 © orlando health 2022

1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 standing orders guideline title: general obstetric patient assessment guideline number: guid-3203-m022 iii. the following patients are not recommended for transport without a consultation with the receiving physician and/or medical control (conferencing an obstetrical specialty medical director as needed) 1. the mother in active labor whose cervix is dilated more than 5 cm. it should be noted that rapid progression of labor can occur, especially in multiparous mothers, so the flight team and the physicians involved may choose to be more conservative than this in some patients in active labor. 2. the mother in active labor who is less than 37 weeks gestation with ruptured membranes. 3. the mother in active labor whose membranes are ruptured and meconium staining is present. 4. contact the receiving physician and/or physician medical director (conferencing an obstetrical specialty physician medical director as needed) with any fetal heart rates under 100 beats per minute. iv. in general, aircraft should not be used to transport patients in active labor unless there are special circumstances. ground transport should be utilized unless directed by medical control. i. general transport i. preserve patient's warmth and privacy. ii. confirm that appropriately sized resuscitation equipment (for both mother and infant) is available and functioning. iii. secure the pregnant patient by positioning the safety straps low on the pelvic girdle. straps across the abdomen can lead to indirect trauma to the fetus in the event of significant turbulence or decelerations forces. iv. if possible, ensure patient has empty bladder prior to departure. v. if delivery occurs during a flight, every attempt should be made to land the aircraft at the nearest emergency department. j. interventions i. if patient has adequate spontaneous respirations, administer supplemental oxygen to maintain spo2 > 93%. ii. connect to cardiac monitor, nibp, pulse oximeter and etco2 (when indicated to maintain etco2 35 - 45 mmhg). iii. obtain a set of vital signs and repeat every 10 minutes, or more frequently depending on presentation. iv. establish iv access x 2 and consider blood draw for lab studies. v. consider maintenance fluids of 0.9% ns/lr at 100 - 150 ml/hr iv. vi. consider use of blood products for ongoing hemorrhage and hemodynamic instability per guid-pr002 - blood product administration or guid-pr025 - emergency blood product administration. observe the patient for signs and symptoms of disseminated intravascular coagulopathy. page 3 of 6 © orlando health 2022

1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 standing orders guideline title: general obstetric patient assessment guideline number: guid-3203-m022 vii. if the patient has a gcs of < 9 and/or has a deteriorating level of consciousness, consider advanced airway management. viii. nauseated patients may be treated with anti-emetics per guid-3203-g004 nausea vomiting motion sickness ix. consider ng or og tube for gastric decompression per guid-3203-pr010 gastric tube insertion x. treat pain and anxiety per guid-3203-g005 analgesia and sedation management and guid-3203-g006 anxiety- combative patient management 1. use cautiously if delivery is imminent secondary to fetal respiratory depression. 2. report to the receiving unit when opioids are administered to the patient. xi. prolapsed umbilical cord 1. elevate mother's hips and place in knee to chest position 2. encourage mother not to push 3. evaluate fetal heart rate/cord pulsation 4. cover cord with moist sterile dressing 5. elevate pressure off cord, do not attempt to push the cord back into vagina k. pre-term labor i. consider insertion of indwelling urinary catheter to ensure bladder emptying as bladder distension may induce uterine contractions. ii. consider group b strep prophylaxis; discuss with the receiving physician and/or medical control to determine appropriate treatment. iii. consider steroid administration with betamethasone 12 mg im for lung maturity for gestational age < 37 weeks; discuss with the receiving physician iv. consult the receiving physician and/or medical director to consider tocolytic therapy 1. terbutaline sulfate 0.25 mg sq every 15 - 30 minutes up to a maximum of 1 mg or terbutaline infusion 5 mcg/min iv to a maximum 80 mcg/min iv a. contraindications to terbutaline sulfate: cardiac history, maternal hr > 140, maternal bp < 90/60 mmhg, fetal heart rate > 160, signs of maternal pulmonary edema. b. if the patient is on a terbutaline infusion at the referring facility, contact the receiving ob physician regarding their management. 2. magnesium sulfate: a. infuse 4 g iv bolus over 20 - 30 minutes, followed by 2 - 4 g/hr iv infusion. b. patients receiving magnesium sulfate infusion must be closely monitored for toxicity (somnolence, muscular paralysis, respiratory depression, hypotension, or loss of dtrs) i. immediately discontinue magnesium sulfate infusion. ii. administer calcium gluconate 1 g of 10% solution over 1 - 2 minutes. l. post-partum hemorrhage i. ensure 2 large bore ivs are patent. page 4 of 6 © orlando health 2022

1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 standing orders guideline title: general obstetric patient assessment guideline number: guid-3203-m022 ii. for atonic uterus: 1. firmly massage uterine fundus. 2. continue oxytocin infusion from receiving facility (general recommendation 20 units/1,000 ml ns or lr at 20 - 40 milliunits/min (60 - 120 ml/hr) or if no iv access, oxytocin 10 units im) iii. for visible lacerations, apply direct pressure. iv. administer txa per guid-3203-t016 - tranexamic acid for severe hemorrhage. v. if hypotensive, place patient in the left or right lateral recumbent position and treat per guid-3203-m011 - hypotension. vi. for hemorrhagic shock that does not respond to warmed fluid resuscitation, administer blood products according to guid-3203-pr002 - blood product administration or guid- 3203-pr025 - emergency blood product administration. m. eclampsia and pre-eclampsia i. definitive treatment can only be accomplished through delivery of the fetus(es). ii. seizure prevention should be accomplished through administration of magnesium sulfate 1. magnesium sulfate: a. infuse 4 g iv bolus over 20 - 30 minutes, followed by 2 - 4 g/hr iv infusion. b. patients receiving magnesium sulfate infusion must be closely monitored for toxicity (somnolence, muscular paralysis, respiratory depression, hypotension, or loss of dtrs) i. immediately discontinue magnesium sulfate infusion. ii. administer calcium gluconate 1 g of 10% solution over 1 - 2 minutes. iii. if sbp > 160 mmhg or dbp is > 110 mmhg, administer: 1. labetalol 20 mg slow iv push (over 2 min). may repeat every 10 minutes with additional doses of 40 mg and then 80 mg, and then may repeat the 80 mg x 2 for the maximum total dose of 300 mg until dbp <100. 2. if bp is not responsive to labetalol then obtain from receiving facility and administer hydralazine 5 mg iv repeated as needed every 20 minutes at a dose of 5 - 10 mg iv to a total dose of 40 mg. iv. seizure activity should be treated with: 1. supportive care first (maintain airway, administer supplemental oxygen as needed to maintain spo2 > 93%, prevent injury). 2. as a first line treatment, administer magnesium sulfate 4 g iv bolus, then an infusion of 2 g/hr iv. 3. for seizure lasting greater than 2 minutes, administer a. midazolam 5 mg iv/in or 10 mg im; repeat as necessary every 2 - 5 minutes. b. for persistent seizures continue magnesium sulfate infusion and treat per guid-3203-m015 seizures vi. documentation: ems charts page 5 of 6 © orlando health 2022
1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 standing orders guideline title: general obstetric patient assessment guideline number: guid-3203-m022 vii. references: page 6 of 6 © orlando health 2022

title: Pediatric General Management
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric general management guideline number: guid-3203-ped001 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to identify historical data relevant to the care of the pediatric patient population. ii. definitions: a. neonate: birth to 28 days b. infant: 1 month to 1 year c. child: 1 year to 17 years (except in the case of trauma < 16 years old) d. adult > or = to 18 years old e. note: the american heart association defines the pediatric patient as any prepubescent patient (e.g., absence of breast development in the female or pubic or axilla hair in the male). iii. department guideline: a. obtain a good history: i. obtain a concise history; identify appropriate informant (patient, family, practitioner). ii. gather past medical history: congenital problems, allergies, childhood illnesses or recent exposure to illness, current immunization status, medications/otcs, & previous hospitalizations/surgeries. iii. for infants: note if full term; breast/bottle fed, complications during pregnancy or any relevant nicu history. iv. obtain history of present illness: time of onset, initial presenting signs and symptoms, recent illnesses of child or other close family member, recent fluid intake and output, and accurate weight prior to illness and current weight and patient's response to therapy. v. obtain history of treatment prior to arrival and pt's response to therapy. vi. for inter-facility transfers obtain: vital signs including temperature, assessment parameters, radiographic studies, laboratory results 1. transport should not be delayed if information is not readily available b. obtain a good assessment: i. airway patency should be immediately assessed, and emergent intervention should be performed. key findings indicating significant respiratory compromise on exam are not limited to, but could include dysphagia, stridor, snoring, gurgling, tripod posturing. ii. assess the patient's respiratory status: page 1 of 3 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric general management guideline number: guid-3203-ped001 1. rate: pediatric patients have a higher respiratory rate at baseline due to a higher basal metabolic rate. a respiratory rate consistently > 60 breaths/minute in a child of any age is abnormal and is considered a "red flag". 2. oxygenation: appropriate oxygenation can be assessed by evaluating skin and mucous membrane color, mental status, muscular tone, oxygen saturation, and heart rate. the response of the pediatric cardiopulmonary system to hypoxia is bradycardia. 3. ventilation: assessment of ventilation begins with a thorough auscultation of breath sounds. etco2 may be assessed during transport period. baseline abg determinations may be available on interfacility transports. 4. work of breathing: it should be evaluated examining for the presence or absence of retractions, nasal flaring, grunting, cyanosis, or use of other accessory muscles. iii. assess circulation: 1. identify heart rate as appropriate for age. 2. if external hemorrhage is identified, bleeding should be controlled immediately with well- aimed direct pressure or other indicated techniques. 3. presence or absence of peripheral and central pulses, capillary refill time, skin color & temperature, and hypovolemia (skin turgor, lack of tearing, dry mucous membranes, sunken fontanels and eyes in infants). iv. assess central nervous system (cns) perfusion by level of consciousness & muscle tone. v. kidney perfusion can be assessed by urine output (in the absence of renal disease). adequate urinary output in an infant is 1-2 ml/kg/hr and in an older child and adolescent is 0.5-1 ml/kg/hr. c. obtain patient weight or use a broselow tape to estimate patient weight. d. for interfacility pediatric transfers, call receiving physician prior to departure to update them on current status and to discuss plan of care. e. interventions: i. if patient has adequate spontaneous respirations, administer supplemental oxygen to maintain spo2 > 93%. ii. connect to cardiac monitor, nibp, pulse oximeter and etco2 iii. obtain a set of vital signs with serial assessment. iv. establish an iv(s) with age appropriate iv size, rate and solutions. v. if unable to obtain iv access, io access should be obtained without further delay when indicated. vi. if the patient has a gcs of < 9 and/or has a deteriorating level of consciousness, consider advanced airway management. vii. nauseated patients may be treated per guid3203-g004 - nausea/vomiting/motion sickness viii. treat pain and anxiety per guid3203-g005 - analgesia and sedation management or guid3203- g006 anxiety- combative patient management. ix. consider placement of an oro/nasogastric tube for a child with gastric distention per guid3203- pr010 gastric tube insertion. x. for interfacility or long-range transports only, consider maintenance fluids if not already initiated. preferred maintenance fluid is d5ns: 1. 4 ml/kg/hr iv for the first 10 kg. page 2 of 3 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric general management guideline number: guid-3203-ped001 2. 2 ml/kg/hr iv for the second 10 kg. 3. 1 ml/kg/hr iv for each kg of body weight above 20 kg. 4. pediatric patient - maintenance fluids calculation table patient weight in kg ml per hour iv patient weight in kg ml per hour iv 5 20 30 70 10 40 35 75 15 50 40 80 20 60 45 85 25 65 50 90 special situation - suspected abuse: xi. for pediatric patients suffering traumatic injury, the provider must consider physical abuse as part of the differential diagnosis. however, other types of abuse, such as neglect or emotional abuse, do occur. there may be secondary medical findings, or the suspicion of abuse may be incidental to the chief complaint. for example, an infant being transported with wound sepsis after an inflicted burn, or a child with failure to thrive and a suspicious social situation. xii. the state of florida requires medical and pre-hospital personnel to report instances of suspected abuse to local child protection agencies. it may be required that each mandated provider generate a report after treating and potentially identifying a suspected abuse victim. each program must be familiar with the reporting process defined by their area. the medical transport team must obtain a careful history to compare reported mechanism of injury with observed or diagnosed injury patterns. inconsistency here or the "story not matching up," should lead the team to suspect potential abuse. this should be reported not only to local child protection entities, but also to the receiving facility and be documented in the patient care report. iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: a. pediatric advanced life support manual, 2020. american heart association. page 3 of 3 © orlando health 2017

title: Pediatric Allergic Reaction/Anaphylaxis
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric allergic reaction/anaphylaxis guideline number: guid-3203-ped002 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: recognition of a pediatric patient presenting with anaphylaxis; provide and maintain adequate airway control, oxygenation, ventilation, and end-organ tissue perfusion; and to provide emergent care and expeditious transport to the nearest appropriate medical facility. ii. definitions: a. mild reaction: itching/hives b. moderate reaction: dyspnea, wheezing, chest tightness, facial swelling c. severe reaction: < 90 mmhg, map < 65 mmhg, stridor, severe respiratory distress, vomiting/diarrhea iii. department guideline: a. establish care as defined by guid3203-g002 - general patient care & guid3203-ped001 - pediatric general management. i. assess and manage airway, breathing, and circulation. ii. provide continuous ekg and oximetry monitoring, frequent vital signs. b. while assessing the patient, attempt to determine the etiology of the allergic reaction. i. if medication infusion reaction suspected, stop the infusion. c. if the patient is experiencing a severe life-threatening reaction, or shows signs of shock consider i. epinephrine (1:1,000) 0.01 mg/kg im q15 min (0.01ml/kg) - max single dose 0.3 mg every 3-5 minutes or sooner. anterior thigh is preferred. 1. massage injection site vigorously for 30-60 seconds d. obtain vascular iv/io access and optimize volume resuscitation per guid3203-ped010 - pediatric hypotension. if the patient is hypotensive or displays signs of inadequate tissue perfusion, administer a fluid bolus of 20ml/kg 0.9% ns. repeat as necessary. i. if the patient remains hypotensive after multiple fluid boluses, consider initiating epinephrine (4 mg/250 ml d5w) infusion at 0.05 mcg/kg/min and titrate every 2-3 min by 0.05mcg/kg/min. max rate 2 mcg/kg/min. 1. if patient is on beta blockers and appears resistant to epinephrine treatment, consider glucagon 20 mcg/kg iv slow over 5 minutes. observe for vomiting. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric allergic reaction/anaphylaxis guideline number: guid-3203-ped002 e. perform a rapid assessment of the patient's airway i. intubation should be performed if stridor or respiratory distress due to upper airway compromise, significant edema of tongue, oropharyngeal tissues, or voice alteration per guid3203-g008 - rapid sequence induction for endotracheal intubation. ii. emergency cricothyroidotomy can be considered to secure airway if severe upper airway edema prevents access to glottic opening with the use of a video laryngoscope per guid- 320-pr007 - cricothyrotomy - surgical. f. if the patient is experiencing dyspnea (with or without wheezing), administer i. high flow oxygen ii. inhaled bronchodilators and repeat as needed: albuterol 2.5 mg/3 ml nss and ipratropium bromide 0.02% 0.5 mg/2.5 ml via neb. 1. may repeat as needed per guid3203-pr012 - inhalation therapy. iii. do not delay intubation if there is rapidly progressing airway edema g. consider antihistamine diphenhydramine 1 mg/kg ivp/io/im. max dose 50 mg. do not administer to premature infants or newborns. h. when hemodynamically stable, consider methylprednisolone 1 mg/kg iv. maximum dose 60mg or dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg iv or im (max dose 10mg) i. consider administering h2 blocker if at sending facility i. famotidine 0.5 mg/kg iv (maximum dose 20 mg) over 2 minutes iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: a. cheng, a., canadian paediatric society, acute care committee. (2011). emergency treatment of anaphylaxis in infants and children. paediatr child health, 16(1), 35-40. b. weingart, s. d. & borshiff, d. c. (2018). the resuscitation crisis manual. leeuwin press. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Pediatric Altered Mental Status (Non-traumatic)
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric altered mental status (non-traumatic) guideline number: guid-3203-ped003 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: identify and correct immediately reversible causes of altered mental status (ams) and provide safe and expeditious transport to the closest appropriate facility. ii. definitions: iii. department guideline: a. obtain good assessment: assess airway, breathing, and circulation as per guid3203-ped001 - pediatric general management. the ams patient presents with an altered level of consciousness that does not correspond with their baseline behavior. they may display inappropriate responses to verbal & physical stimuli. consider hpi, as it may provide indicators as to the underlying cause. b. consider possible causes i. toxic ingestion : question caregivers/referring staff about possibility of ingestion, access to substances. observe for specific toxidromes, including narcotic ingestion (pinpoint pupils, hypoventilation with ams). refer to guid3203-m019 - overdose, poisoning, & toxic exposure. ii. hypoxia : observe for airway patency, work of breathing (grunting, retractions, nasal flaring), tachypnea, tachycardia or bradycardia (late sign), cyanosis. abc's, pulse oximetry, or etco2 sampling may be useful. providers must rapidly identify of the need for ventilatory support based upon the findings discovered by largely clinical observation in the initial assessment. iii. hypovolemia : assess for signs of volume deficit including tachycardia, tachypnea, pallor, dry and/or pale mucous membranes, sunken fontanels, sunken eyes, poor skin turgor, and decreased urine output (indwelling urinary catheter, or number of wet diapers in a given time period). low bp is a late sign and should not be used to disregard the diagnosis of hypovolemia in children. iv. meningitis/meningoencephalitis : observe for fever, nuchal rigidity, petechial rash (non-blanching reddish-purple areas), and/or bulging fontanels. v. sepsis: assess for history of recent illness, suspected source, or physical portal of entry (wound, umbilical stump, etc.). clinical signs of sepsis include fever, hypothermia, mottling of skin, and signs of hypovolemia. review available lab work for increased wbc's, left shift, (+) cultures. vi. hypoperfusion of cardiac etiology : assess patient for heart rate and regularity. auscultate anterior chest for murmurs or adventitious cardiac sounds. palpate brachial and femoral pulses for equality (coarctation of the aorta may present with bounding upper extremity pulses and diminished/absent femorals). in cyanotic heart defects, cyanosis will persist despite appropriate oxygen administration and ventilation. review ekg for dysrhythmias. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric altered mental status (non-traumatic) guideline number: guid-3203-ped003 vii. neurotrauma: illicit history from caregivers/referring staff about recent trauma. assess for head injuries & palpate head for depressed areas or hematomas. observe for otorrhea, rhinorrhea, bulging fontanelles, or other evidence of basilar skull fractures. spinal immobilization is appropriate if neurotrauma is suspected. viii. seizure : seizures have numerous presentations. partial seizures can include absence episodes, abnormal vocalizations, or focal motor including head turning, eye deviation, tonic contractions, lip smacking, pedaling motions, etc. without or with a loss of consciousness (partial complex seizures) and also may secondarily generalize into tonic/clonic activity. ix. metabolic issues: 1. diabetic ketoacidosis (dka) : assess initial glucose level. the diagnosis of type i diabetes is often made when the patient presents with dka. extremely low etco2 in a spontaneously breathing patient is a sign of severe acidosis/dka. refer to guid3203-ped008 - pediatric diabetic ketoacidosis for additional pathophysiology and assessment parameters. 2. electrolyte abnormality : evaluate completed lab work, if available. sodium imbalances, result in significant alterations in mental status. x. intussusception : telescoping of the bowel into itself can occur at any location, most common is ileocecal. the most common presentation is acute discomfort followed by lethargy. the classic finding of "currant jelly stools" is a late sign indicating infarction of the bowel. xi. stroke and intracerebral hemorrhage : pediatric ischemic strokes and intra-cranial hemorrhages are rare. careful history taking may provide clues to the etiology of the presenting problem. suspect ischemic stroke if symptoms consistent (unilateral deficits, ams). prevalence of intra- cranial abnormalities is increased in those with a history of a hematologic disorder (e.g., sickle cell disease) or a compromised clotting cascade (vonwillebrand's disease & hemophilia). there is also an increased prevalence in infants born at home who did not receive postpartum vitamin k. c. assess and manage airway, breathing and circulation. d. initiate cardiac monitoring and follow pulse oximetry. e. establish an iv/io. f. if volume depletion is suspected, 0.9% ns 20 ml/kg iv per guid3203-ped009 - pediatric hypotension. g. if the blood sugar level is less than 60 mg/dl, treat per guid3203-m021 - hypoglycemia. h. if an opioid drug overdose is suspected, administer i. if > 20 kg: naloxone 0.1 mg/kg/dose iv/in/im/sc repeat every 2-3 min prn, max single dose 2 mg. ii. if < 20 kg: naloxone 0.01-0.1 mg/kg/dose iv/in/im/sc repeat every 2-3 min prn, max single dose 2 mg. i. if sepsis is suspected, refer to the guid3203-ped016 - pediatric sepsis/meningitis. iv. documentation: ems charts v. references: page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Pediatric Asystole
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric asystole guideline number: guid-3203-ped004 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to ensure prompt identification and intervention to restore a stable cardiac rhythm. ii. pathophysiology: asystole is the absence of electrical activity in the heart. the approach to asystole resuscitation is essentially the same as pulseless electrical activity (pea). despite dismal survival rates after out of hospital cardiac arrest, pediatric patients in asystole or pea have higher survival rates and better neurologic outcomes than adults. thus, aggressive resuscitation for longer periods of time are warranted. iii. definitions: a. aha - american heart association b. cpr - cardiopulmonary resuscitation iv. department guideline: a. initiate cpr per aha guideline with compression rate of 100-120/min. b. apply cardiac monitor. confirm asystole in two leads. asystole should not be defibrillated. i. perform rhythm analysis every 2 minutes between cycles of cpr. c. obtain or confirm iv/io access. d. manage airway and ensure adequate oxygenation and ventilation with positive pressure ventilation. i. initially administer o2 via bvm ii. secure airway and ventilate with 100% oxygen. intubate, if necessary, per guid3203-pr020 endotracheal intubation. iii. ventilate with 1 breath every 2 to 3 seconds (a rate of about 20-30 breaths per minute). avoid hyperventilation. iv. apply etco2 monitor if advanced airway present. e. administer i. epinephrine (1:10,000) 0.01 mg/kg (0.1 ml/kg) iv/io, max dose 1 mg. ii. epinephrine (1:1,000) 0.1 mg/kg (0.1 ml/kg) via ett, max dose 1 mg iii. re-dose every 3-5 minutes f. administer 0.9% ns or lactated ringers 20 ml/kg iv g. consider underlying causes (5 h's and t's): hypovolemia, hypoxia, h+ ions - acidosis, hyper/hypokalemia, hypothermia, hypoglycemia, toxic ingestion, cardiac tamponade, tension pneumothorax, thrombus - ami/pe, trauma. also: congenital heart disease and pulmonary hypertension. h. if cardiac arrest due to trauma page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric asystole guideline number: guid-3203-ped004 i. anticipate airway obstruction by dental fragments, blood, or other debris ii. minimize cervical spine motion iii. control external bleeding with pressure or per guid3203-pr006 tourniquet therapy or guid- pr028 hemostatic dressing - quickclot gauze iv. perform unilateral or bilateral needle decompressions for suspected tension pneumothoraxes per guid3203-pr017 needle thoracostomy. pneumothorax is rare in the pediatric population. v. transport infants and children with multisystem trauma to the closest pediatric trauma center vi. initiate volume resuscitation with blood products per guid3203-pr025 - emergency blood product administration vii. non-accidental trauma should be considered for any infant with unanticipated, unexplained cardiac arrest i. for deterioration during transport after rosc, consider mechanical problems with the airway, ventilation, and/or blood volume. dysrhythmias are rare, except as an agonal event. consider: extubation, plugged endotracheal tube, right main stem bronchus intubation, distended stomach causing impaired lung expansion, pneumothorax, inadequate minute ventilation, ventilator malfunction, disconnected or depleted oxygen supply, hypovolemic shock, or agitation. j. report broselow color and/or estimated weight to receiving center prior to arrival k. out of hospital cessation of resuscitation efforts should not be considered in pediatric patients without contacting medical control. l. always consider the latest recommendations from the american heart association. v. documentation: ems charts vi. references: a. pediatric advanced life support manual, 2020. american heart association. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Pediatric Bradycardia
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric bradycardia guideline number: guid-3203-ped005 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: ensure prompt identification and intervention to restore adequate cardiac output and a stable cardiac rhythm. ii. definitions: iii. department guideline: a. immediately assess for hypoxia, hypoperfusion, hypotension, hypothermia, altered mental status, & signs of shock. b. if hr is < 60 bpm with poor perfusion despite oxygenation and ventilation begin chest compressions c. if the patient is unstable, begin to resuscitate. if the patient is stable, attempt to identify the cause of the bradycardia and resolve it. d. manage the airway aggressively with high fio2 oxygen and ventilatory support with positive pressure ventilation. intubate, if necessary, per guid3203-g008 rapid sequence induction for endotracheal intubation and guid3203-pr020 endotracheal intubation. apply etco2 monitor. e. apply cardiac monitor, monitor pulse, blood pressure, and oximetry. f. if there are no signs of cardiopulmonary compromise, observe and perform frequent reassessments, obtain a 12-lead ecg, and treat underlying causes g. if from a sending facility obtain appropriate laboratory studies (potassium, glucose, ionized calcium, magnesium, blood gas for ph, toxicology screen. h. for symptomatic bradycardia that persists despite effective oxygenation and ventilation i. epinephrine (1:10,000) 0.01 mg/kg (0.1 ml/kg) iv/io, max dose 1 mg. ii. epinephrine (1:1,000) 0.1 mg/kg (0.1 ml/kg) via ett, max dose 1 mg iii. re-dose every 3-5 minutes. i. for increased vagal tone or primary av block consider: i. atropine 0.02 mg/kg iv/io, min dose 0.1 mg, max dose 0.5 mg. ii. atropine 0.04-0.06 mg/kg via ett iii. may repeat once in 5 minutes. larger doses may be required for organophosphate poisoning. j. for bradycardia caused by a complete heart block or abnormal sinus nose function or post-surgical correction of congenital disease consider transcutaneous pacing per guid3203-pr022 - transcutaneous external pacing (tcp). i. for tcp, provide sedation and/or analgesia if permissible per guid3203-g005 - analgesia and sedation management. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric bradycardia guideline number: guid-3203-ped005 k. consider underlying causes: i. hypoxia: high concentrated o2 with assisted ventilations ii. h+ ions - acidosis: provide ventilation to treat respiratory acidosis secondary to hypercarbia. consider sodium bicarb in severe metabolic acidosis with expert opinion iii. hyperkalemia: with expert consultation restore normal potassium concentration by stopping fluids that contain potassium. consider calcium chloride or gluconate, albuterol, sodium bicarb, insulin with glucose administration, loop diuretics, kayexalate. iv. hypothermia: warm the child but avoid hyperthermia v. heart block: see above for atropine or tcp vi. toxic ingestion: treat per guid3203-m019 overdose, poisoning, & toxic exposure vii. head trauma: bradycardia with head trauma is an ominous sign of high intracranial pressure. provide oxygenation, ventilation, and consider a brief period of mild hyperventilation l. always consider the latest recommendations from the american heart association. iv. documentation: ems charts v. references: a. pediatric advanced life support manual, 2020. american heart association. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Pediatric Cardiogenic Shock
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric cardiogenic shock guideline number: guid-3203-ped006 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: restoration of inadequate cardiac output to improve perfusion by expanding intravascular volume, improving contractility, and treating the underlying cause. ii. pathophysiology: common causes of cardiogenic shock include congenital heart disease, myocarditis, cardiomyopathy, arrhythmias, sepsis, drug toxicity, or myocardial injury (trauma). marked tachycardia, high svr, and decreased cardiac output characterize cardiogenic shock. pulmonary venous congestion leads to pulmonary edema and increased work of breathing. typically, intravascular volume is normal or increased unless a concurrent illness causes hypovolemia. iii. definitions: iv. department guideline: a. assess and manage airway, breathing, and circulation. monitor etco2 if intubated. optimize oxygen content. b. assess for airway patency, anxiety/agitation, heart rate, pulse quality, skin perfusion, respiratory distress (retractions, grunting, and use of accessory muscles), signs of fluid overload, hypotension, and level of consciousness. c. obtain the largest iv access for the patient's size. 2 points of iv access are preferred. d. if unable to obtain iv, obtain io access per guid3203-pr013 - intraosseous infusions - ezio. e. manage cardiac arrythmias (supraventricular/ventricular tachycardia/bradycardia) prior to fluid resuscitation. consider betablocker or calcium channel blocker poisoning. f. if signs of pulmonary vascular congestion are present, withhold fluid bolus and consult with physician for order to administer furosemide 1-2mg/kg iv max single dose 20 mg. g. rapidly providing volume resuscitation of cardiogenic shock in the setting of poor myocardial function can aggravate pulmonary edema and further impair myocardial function. gradually provide volume resuscitation for cardiogenic shock. if no evidence of pulmonary vascular congestion or fluid overload: 1. resuscitate with 0.9% ns or lactated ringers 5-10 ml/kg warm ns or lr bolus over 10-20 minutes. a. repeat bolus if patient remains hypo-perfused unless signs of pulmonary vascular congestion or fluid overload develop. b. warm fluids per guid3203-pr026 - warming of blood product & intravenous fluids 2. for patients failing fluid boluses, consider initiating inotropic support early. a. dobutamine infusion at 2 - 20 mcg/kg/minute. -or- page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric cardiogenic shock guideline number: guid-3203-ped006 b. epinephrine infusion at 0.05 - 2 mcg/kg/min. c. milrinone infusion at 0.25 - 0.75mcg/kg/min. h. most children with cardiogenic shock benefit from a vasodilator (if blood pressure is adequate) to decrease systemic vascular resistance (svr) and increase cardiac output and tissue perfusion. obtain consultation from sending or receiving facility. i. if the blood glucose level is less than 60, treat per guid3203-m021 - hypoglycemia. j. monitor urine output by indwelling urinary catheter if available. titrate resuscitation to 1ml/kg/hr. k. always consider the latest recommendations from the american heart association. ii. documentation: a. ems charts iii. references: a. pediatric advanced life support manual, 2020. american heart association. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric cardiogenic shock guideline number: guid-3203-ped006 b. epinephrine infusion at 0.05 - 2 mcg/kg/min. c. milrinone infusion at 0.25 - 0.75mcg/kg/min. h. most children with cardiogenic shock benefit from a vasodilator (if blood pressure is adequate) to decrease systemic vascular resistance (svr) and increase cardiac output and tissue perfusion. obtain consultation from sending or receiving facility. i. if the blood glucose level is less than 60, treat per guid3203-m021 - hypoglycemia. j. monitor urine output by indwelling urinary catheter if available. titrate resuscitation to 1ml/kg/hr. k. always consider the latest recommendations from the american heart association. ii. documentation: a. ems charts iii. references: a. pediatric advanced life support manual, 2020. american heart association. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Pediatric Cyanotic Congenital Heart Disease
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric cyanotic congenital heart disease guideline number: guid-3203-ped007 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: restoration of cardiac output to improve tissue oxygenation and inadequate perfusion by maintaining an open ductus arteriosus and expanding intravascular volume as appropriate. ii. pathophysiology: certain congenital heart disease conditions exist resulting in circulatory shunts between the right heart and left heart bypassing the lungs. cyanosis ensues that is not responsive to application of supplemental oxygenation. after birth, in response to lung expansion and gas exchange, the ductus arteriosus gradually closes over the first one to two weeks of life. while the ductus arteriosus remains open, unoxygenated blood being shunted from right to left travels "backward" from the aorta into the pulmonary artery providing for lung perfusion and gas exchange. as the ductus closes, this circulatory pattern ceases, and the infant with congenital shunting cannot oxygenate blood. maintaining an open ductus arteriosus can be lifesaving until the congenital abnormality can be corrected surgically. these ductal-dependent cyanotic congenital heart disease conditions include: coarctation of the aorta, transposition of the great vessels, tricuspid atresia, tetralogy of fallot, and total anomalous pulmonary venous return. iii. definitions: iv. department guideline: a. assess airway, breathing, and circulation as per guid3203-ped001 - pediatric general management. b. apply cardiac monitor, monitor pulse, blood pressure, and oximetry. assess oxygenation with pre- ductal and post-ductal pulse oximetry readings on the upper and lower extremities, respectively. c. apply oxygen for hypoxia and determine response. if the response is worsening perfusion, remove the oxygen. avoid hyperventilation and hyperoxia. d. monitor etco2 if intubated. e. obtain the largest iv access for the patient's size, two if possible. if unable to obtain iv access, obtain io access per guid3203-pr013 - intraosseous infusions - ezio. f. if no improvement in hypoxia with oxygen, consider discussion for orders from the sending or receiving physician for prostaglandin e1, alprostadil, infusion. carefully monitor patient for hypotension and potential intubation/airway management due to side effects of prostaglandin administration. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric cyanotic congenital heart disease guideline number: guid-3203-ped007 i. prostaglandin e1 (alprostadil) infusion at 0.1 mcg/kg/min, and titrate to improved oxygenation and systemic perfusion, max dose 0.4mcg/kg/min. g. if signs of pulmonary vascular congestion and/or fluid overload are present, consult with physician to withhold fluid bolus and administer furosemide 1 mg/kg iv. maximum single dose 20mg iv. h. if no evidence of fluid overload: i. resuscitate with 20 ml/kg iv crystalloid bolus over 5-10 minutes. repeat up to two times for a total of 60 ml/kg if patient remains in shock unless signs of fluid overload are present. ii. for patients failing initial fluid bolus, initiate inotropic support: 1. dopamine infusion at 5 - 20 mcg/kg/min iv/io 2. dobutamine infusion at 2 - 20 mcg/kg/min iv/io 3. milrinone infusion at 0.25 - 0.75 mcg/kg/min iv/io 4. epinephrine infusion at 0.05 - 2 mcg/kg/min iv/io i. if the blood glucose level is less than 60, treat per guid3203-m021 - hypoglycemia. j. monitor urine output by indwelling urinary catheter if available. titrate resuscitation to 1 ml/kg/hr. v. documentation: a. ems charts vi. references: a. pediatric advanced life support manual, 2020. american heart association. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017
