title: Pediatric Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA)
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric diabetic ketoacidosis (dka) guideline number: guid-3203-ped008 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to recognize the presence and the precipitating factors of diabetic ketoacidosis (dka), treat metabolic and hormonal abnormalities, and manage systemic complications as they present. pathophysiology: diabetic ketoacidosis is characterized by hyperglycemia and ketonemia, resulting in an anion gap metabolic acidosis. ketonemia occurs because of increased lipolysis and ketogenesis used by the cell to produce energy when glucose is not available. these patients are significantly dehydrated secondary to osmotic diuresis from increased glucose in their blood and have frequently have significant electrolyte imbalances. rapid correction of hyperglycemia can produce cns dysfunction, brain edema, and brain herniation. hyperglycemic hyperosmolar syndrome (hhs) is an emergency that is distinguished from dka by marked hyperglycemia, absent to mild ketosis with minimal acidosis, and elevation in serum osmolality. definitions: ii. department guideline: a. assess airway, breathing, and circulation as per guid3203-ped001 - pediatric general management. b. apply cardiac monitor, monitor pulse, blood pressure, and oximetry. assess vital signs. c. apply oxygen for hypoxia. monitor etco2 if intubated. patients with acidosis must be adequately ventilated to aid in the correction of acidosis. d. obtain iv access for the patient's size. e. assess urine output. f. assess for severe dka: i. lab findings: hyperglycemia (blood glucose > 300-1,000 mg/dl.) metabolic acidosis (ph < 7.1, hco3 < 10 meq/l). elevated bun and creatinine, normal to hyperkalemia and hyponatremia. ii. physical exam: profound kussmaul's respirations, poor perfusion, severe dehydration with cool extremities, lethargy/obstruction, & poor urine output. g. hydration i. if signs of shock, follow guid3203-ped009 - hypotension. ii. if not in shock with signs of volume depletion, give 0.9% ns 20 ml/kg iv over 1 hour. iii. if no signs of volume depletion, start 0.9% ns at 1.5-2 times the maintenance rate. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric diabetic ketoacidosis (dka) guideline number: guid-3203-ped008 1. normal maintenance fluid rate: a. 4 ml/kg/hr iv for the first 10 kg. b. 2 ml/kg/hr iv for the second 10 kg c. 1 ml/kg/hr iv for each kg of body weight above 20 kg h. discuss with physician for orders for a regular insulin infusion at 0.05-0.1 units/kg/hour (no bolus). i. monitor blood glucose level every 30 minutes, adjust therapy to decrease serum glucose by no more than 100 mg/dl per hour. i. if serum glucose decreases by more than 100 mg/dl per hour, add 5% dextrose to iv fluids. ii. if serum glucose falls below 300 mg/dl, add 5% dextrose to the iv fluids. j. serum potassium may require supplementation via physician order kcl iv infusion at 10-20 meq/h. k. if ph is less than 7.0, contact medical control or speak to admitting physician to consider sodium bicarbonate. l. monitor for signs of cerebral edema (headache, decreased mental status) i. elevate head of bed 30 degrees ii. consider deceasing fluids and insulin infusion rates. iii. documentation: ems charts iv. references: a. glaser, barnett, mccaslin, et al. (2001). risk factors for cerebral edema in children with diabetic ketoacidosis. the pediatric emergency medicine collaborative research committee of the american academy of pediatrics. n engl j med; 344:264. b. wolfsdorf, glaser, agus, fritsch, hanas, rewers, sperling, & codner. (2018). ispad clinical practice consensus guidelines 2018: diabetic ketoacidosis and the hyperglycemic hyperosmolar state. pediatr diabetes, 19 suppl 27:155. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Pediatric Hypotension
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric hypotension guideline number: guid-3203-ped009 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to recognize the patient with hypotension. to provide and maintain adequate oxygenation, ventilation, and tissue perfusion. to provide adequate blood pressure support and expeditious transport to the nearest appropriate medical facility. ii. definitions: hypotension in children is determined by age & systolic blood pressure (bp) mmhg. a. term neonates (0 to 28 days): the systolic bp is < 60 mmhg b. infants (1 to 12 months): systolic bp is < 70 mmhg. c. children 1 to 10 years (5th bp percentile): systolic bp is < 70 mmhg + (age in years x 2). d. children > 10 years: systolic bp is < 90 mmhg. iii. department guideline: a. assess airway, breathing, and circulation as per guid3203-ped001 - pediatric general management. b. apply cardiac monitor, monitor pulse, blood pressure, and oximetry. assess vital signs. c. assess for clinical signs: tachypnea, tachycardia, altered mental status, and either bounding pulses in hyperemic extremities (warm shock) or peripheral vasoconstriction with weak peripheral pulses, mottling, and delayed capillary refill in cool extremities (cold shock). d. use a color-coded length-based tape to determine the child's weight (if not known) e. obtain iv/io access for the patient's size. f. treat underlying cause of hypotension: hypovolemia, decreased vascular resistance, heart failure g. fluid resuscitation i. administer 0.9% ns or lactated ringers 20 ml/kg iv, may be repeated per guid3203- pr015 - intravenous fluid therapy. ii. most hypovolemic children will require at least 40-60 ml/kg in the first hour and those with sepsis may require up to 80 ml/kg in the first hour. iii. to help deliver fluid rapidly: place an in-line 3-way stopcock in the iv tubing system. deliver fluid by using a 30- to 60-ml syringe to push fluids through the stopcock or use a pressure bag (beware of risk of air embolism) or a rapid infusion device. h. if actively hemorrhaging, control bleeding, and consider infusing blood products per guid3203- pr002 - blood product administration or guid3203-pr025 - emergency blood product administration at 10 ml/kg. i. utilize fluid warmer when possible per guid3203-pr026 warming of blood products & intravenous fluids. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric hypotension guideline number: guid-3203-ped009 j. if hypotension persists after adequate fluid resuscitation, initiate one of the following: i. epinephrine infusion at 0.05 -2 mcg/kg/min iv/io titrate q 5 min to appropriate sbp ii. norepinephrine infusion at 0.03 - 0.5 mcg/kg/min iv/io titrate q 5 min to appropriate sbp iii. dopamine infusion at 5 - 20 mcg/kg/min iv/io titrate q 5 min to appropriate sbp k. if hypotension attributed to heart failure, refer to guid3203-ped006 - cardiogenic shock. l. for patients with hypotension due to a dysrhythmia, follow appropriate guideline. m. a child in septic shock with fluid-refractory and epinephrine- or norepinephrine-resistant shock may have adrenal insufficiency. if you suspect adrenal insufficiency, or a patient is at known risk for adrenal insufficiency (i.e., history of steroid use), consider hydrocortisone 1-2 mg/kg iv early from a sending facility. if possible, obtain a baseline cortisol concentration before administration, and obtain expert consultation for any additional evaluation and management. n. maintain the optimal body temperature. iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: a. pediatric advanced life support manual, 2020. american heart association. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Pediatric Infectious Respiratory Distress
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric infectious respiratory distress guideline number: guid-3203-ped010 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to maintain a patent airway, optimize ventilation/oxygenation, decrease the work of breathing, treat appropriate respiratory diseases with antibiotics, antipyretics when indicated, and to maintain adequate hemodynamic parameters for the patient ii. pathophysiology: croup is a viral infection that causes erythema and edema of the vocal cords & subglottic region. it typically affects children 3 months to 3 years, and is characterized by a harsh, barking cough, frequently the child is not toxic appearing. epiglottitis is a bacterial infection of the epiglottis and larynx. it usually affects children 3 to 7 years of age. common presentation: toxic appearance, sudden onset with a high fever, muffled voice, and drooling. bronchiolitis causes mucous plugging and inflammation of the bronchi leading to hypoxia and atelectasis. respiratory syncytial virus (rsv) common cause. most often seen in <2 years of age. there is a history of gradual onset of cough, expiratory wheezing, & increased work of breathing with nasal flaring & retractions. pneumonia may be viral or bacterial. influenza virus most often causes viral pneumonia with an acute inflammatory response which develops in the lungs. bacterial pneumonia in a non-septic child is usually lobar and may cause a hypoxia. decreased pulmonary compliance can occur with resultant respiratory distress/failure. iii. definitions: iv. department guideline: a. assess and manage airway, breathing, and circulation. assess cns by consciousness and muscle tone b. position child to maximize airway opening. allow child to assume position of greatest comfort if possible and appropriate. c. supplemental oxygen should be given. monitor oxygen saturation and end tidal co2. d. airway should be secured with intubation, if necessary, per guid3203-g008 rapid sequence induction for endotracheal intubation and guid3203-pr020 endotracheal intubation. in the case of epiglottitis, if there is an available anesthesiologist or otorhinolaryngologist, it is recommended the patient be evaluated to determine whether the patient requires immediate airway management in the or prior to transport. always have an ett 1/2 mm and 1 mm smaller than usual ready in case of edematous anatomy. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric infectious respiratory distress guideline number: guid-3203-ped010 e. for total obstruction, a needle or surgical cricothyrotomy may need to be performed per guid3203-pr007 cricothyrotomy - surgical. f. most optimal therapy for epiglottitis includes maintaining a calm environment, avoiding invasive events, and airway evaluated by most experienced person available. intubate if epiglottitis is present. confirm ett with cxr and respiratory exam. g. administer antibiotics per order of referring or receiving physician. h. stridor should be treated with racemic epinephrine 2.25% 0.05 ml/kg to a maximum single dose of 0.5 ml diluted in 3ml of 0.9% ns and nebulized. may repeat in 20 minutes once. see the guid3203-pr012 - inhalation therapy. i. children with wheezing should be treated with bronchodilators per guid3203-ped014 - pediatric patient reactive airway disease. j. consider steroids for croup only, dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg iv/oral/im to maximum single dose of 10mg. k. use caution to reduce agitation (during attempts for intravenous access, etc.) as this can cause airway decompensation l. treat for shock as outlined in the guid3203-ped009 - hypotension. m. if any question of a bacterial process, antibiotic therapy should be initiated on interfacility transports prior to transport and after blood cultures obtained per guid3203-ped016 - pediatric sepsis/meningitis. n. antipyretics may be given for febrile children prior to transport on inter-facility transports. (acetaminophen 15 mg/kg or ibuprofen 10 mg/kg po). consider rectal administration of acetaminophen if child is having difficulty swallowing or severe stridor. o. for inter-facility transport, assess arterial blood gas and chest x-ray. p. follow orlando health best practices based for contact, droplet, or airborne precautions based on suspected organism. v. documentation: a. ems charts vi. references: a. pediatric advanced life support manual, 2020. american heart association. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

ett 1/2 mm and 1 mm smaller than usual ready in case of edematous anatomy. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017

1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric infectious respiratory distress guideline number: guid-3203-ped010 e. for total obstruction, a needle or surgical cricothyrotomy may need to be performed per guid3203-pr007 cricothyrotomy - surgical. f. most optimal therapy for epiglottitis includes maintaining a calm environment, avoiding invasive events, and airway evaluated by most experienced person available. intubate if epiglottitis is present. confirm ett with cxr and respiratory exam. g. administer antibiotics per order of referring or receiving physician. h. stridor should be treated with racemic epinephrine 2.25% 0.05 ml/kg to a maximum single dose of 0.5 ml diluted in 3ml of 0.9% ns and nebulized. may repeat in 20 minutes once. see the guid3203-pr012 - inhalation therapy. i. children with wheezing should be treated with bronchodilators per guid3203-ped014 - pediatric patient reactive airway disease. j. consider steroids for croup only, dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg iv/oral/im to maximum single dose of 10mg. k. use caution to reduce agitation (during attempts for intravenous access, etc.) as this can cause airway decompensation l. treat for shock as outlined in the guid3203-ped009 - hypotension. m. if any question of a bacterial process, antibiotic therapy should be initiated on interfacility transports prior to transport and after blood cultures obtained per guid3203-ped016 - pediatric sepsis/meningitis. n. antipyretics may be given for febrile children prior to transport on inter-facility transports. (acetaminophen 15 mg/kg or ibuprofen 10 mg/kg po). consider rectal administration of acetaminophen if child is having difficulty swallowing or severe stridor. o. for inter-facility transport, assess arterial blood gas and chest x-ray. p. follow orlando health best practices based for contact, droplet, or airborne precautions based on suspected organism. v. documentation: a. ems charts vi. references: a. pediatric advanced life support manual, 2020. american heart association. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Pediatric Obstructed Airway – Foreign Body Aspiration
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric obstructed airway - foreign body aspiration guideline number: guid-3203-ped011 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to remove the obstruction and maintain patent airway. to promote appropriate oxygenation and ventilation ii. pathophysiology: aspiration of a foreign body may cause airway obstruction. foreign bodies lodged in the esophagus will present differently from those lodged in the trachea or main stem bronchi. other history may include the sudden onset of coughing or wheezing, associated with an episode of choking.  if object lodged at level of the glottis: hoarseness, cough, aphonia, and decreased breath sounds.  if object lodged below cords and in the trachea: persistent cough, decreased breath sounds bilaterally, palpable vibration over the trachea (object acts as a ball valve with respirations).  if object lodged in bronchus: wheezing & coughing. the object may be lodged in the right main stem so potentially have asymmetrical chest movement & decreased breath sounds on right side.  if chest x-ray is obtained, assess distal hyperinflation secondary to air trapping. the films will show a hyperexpanded lung on the involved side on the inspiratory film and a shift of the mediastinal structures to the uninvolved side on the expiratory film.  if object lodged in esophagus: stridor may be present iii. definitions: iv. department guideline: a. assess and manage airway, breathing, and circulation per guid3203-ped001 - pediatric general management. b. identify partial versus complete airway obstruction. if a partial airway obstruction exists, assess the patient's ability to maintain the airway and adequacy of ventilation. c. if conscious: follow aha guidelines for obstructed airway i. if infant (< 1 year) 1. give 5 back slaps and 5 chest thrusts 2. repeat until effective or patient becomes unresponsive ii. if 1-8 years of age 1. give abdominal thrusts/heimlich maneuver 2. repeat until effective or patient becomes unresponsive d. if unresponsive page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric obstructed airway - foreign body aspiration guideline number: guid-3203-ped011 i. if infant (< 1 year) 1. perform a tongue-jaw lift and if you see object remove it. 2. using laryngoscope and magill forceps, attempt direct visualization for removal of object 3. open airway and try to ventilate if still obstructed. reposition the head and try to ventilate again 4. give chest compressions at a rate of at least 100 per minute 5. repeat the previous 4 steps ii. if above steps are ineffective, initiate the following 1. attempt to establish an airway with tracheal intubation per guid3203-g008 - rapid sequence intubation (rsi) for endotracheal (et) intubation. 2. if attempts at retrieval and/or positive pressure ventilation are unsuccessful, a cricothyrotomy should be performed per guid3203-pr007 - cricothyrotomy - surgical. an obstruction distal to the cricothyroid membrane may render a cricothyrotomy ineffective. 3. if the object is lodged below the cords and in the trachea, it may be able to be displaced distally by pushing it into the right main stem which allows for ventilation of one lung. the tracheal tube should then be pulled back into the trachea to ventilate the left lung. iii. follow advanced cardiac guidelines. v. documentation: a. ems charts vi. references: a. pediatric advanced life support manual, 2020. american heart association. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

pediatric general management. b. identify partial versus complete airway obstruction. if a partial airway obstruction exists, assess the patient's ability to maintain the airway and adequacy of ventilation. c. if conscious: follow aha guidelines for obstructed airway i. if infant (< 1 year) 1. give 5 back slaps and 5 chest thrusts 2. repeat until effective or patient becomes unresponsive ii. if 1-8 years of age 1. give abdominal thrusts/heimlich maneuver 2. repeat until effective or patient becomes unresponsive d. if unresponsive page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Pediatric Pulseless Electrical Activity (PEA)
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric pulseless electrical activity (pea) guideline number: guid-3203-ped012 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: ensure prompt identification and provide intervention to restore a stable cardiac rhythm with adequate cardiac output and. ii. pathophysiology: pulseless electrical activity (pea) is the presence of an organized cardiac rhythm without a measurable pulse. there is a recognized list of treatable causes for pea and the clinician should work promptly to assess for and correct, as appropriate, those causes. iii. definitions: a. pea - pulseless electrical activity b. aha - american heart association c. cpr - cardiopulmonary resuscitation iv. department guideline: a. initiate cpr per aha guideline with compression rate of 100-120/min. b. apply cardiac monitor. i. perform rhythm analysis every 2 minutes between cycles of cpr. c. obtain or confirm iv/io access. d. manage airway and ensure adequate oxygenation and ventilation with positive pressure ventilation. i. initially administer o2 via bvm ii. secure airway and ventilate with 100% oxygen. intubate, if necessary, per guid3203-pr020 endotracheal intubation. iii. ventilate with 1 breath every 2 to 3 seconds (a rate of about 20-30 breaths per minute). avoid hyperventilation. iv. apply etco2 monitor if advanced airway present. e. administer i. epinephrine (1:10,000) 0.01 mg/kg (0.1 ml/kg) iv/io, maximum dose 1 mg. ii. epinephrine (1:1,000) 0.1 mg/kg (0.1 ml/kg) via ett, max dose 1 mg iii. re-dose every 3-5 minutes f. administer 0.9% ns or lactated ringers 20 ml/kg iv g. consider underlying causes (5 h's and t's): hypovolemia, hypoxia, h+ ions - acidosis, hyper/hypokalemia, hypothermia, hypoglycemia, toxic ingestion, cardiac tamponade, tension page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric pulseless electrical activity (pea) guideline number: guid-3203-ped012 pneumothorax, thrombus - ami/pe, trauma. also: congenital heart disease and pulmonary hypertension. h. if cardiac arrest due to trauma i. anticipate airway obstruction by dental fragments, blood, or other debris ii. minimize cervical spine motion iii. control external bleeding with pressure or per guid3203-pr006 tourniquet therapy or guid- pr028 hemostatic dressing - quick clot gauze iv. perform unilateral or bilateral needle decompressions for suspected tension pneumothoraxes per guid3203-pr017 needle thoracostomy. pneumothorax is rare in the pediatric population. v. transport infants and children with multisystem trauma to the closest pediatric trauma center vi. initiate volume resuscitation with blood products per guid3203-pr025 - emergency blood product administration vii. non-accidental trauma should be considered for any infant with unanticipated, unexplained cardiac arrest i. for deterioration during transport after rosc, consider mechanical problems with the airway, ventilation, and/or blood volume. dysrhythmias are rare, except as an agonal event. consider: extubation, plugged endotracheal tube, right main stem bronchus intubation, distended stomach causing impaired lung expansion, pneumothorax, inadequate minute ventilation, ventilator malfunction, disconnected or depleted oxygen supply, hypovolemic shock, or agitation. j. report broselow color and/or estimated weight to receiving center prior to arrival k. out of hospital cessation of resuscitation efforts should not be considered in pediatric patients without contacting medical control. l. always consider the latest recommendations from the american heart association. v. documentation: ems charts vi. references: a. pediatric advanced life support manual, 2020. american heart association. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Pediatric Ventricular Fibrillation – Pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric ventricular fibrillation - pulseless ventricular tachycardia guideline number: guid-3203-ped013 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: ensure prompt identification and provide intervention to restore a stable cardiac rhythm with adequate cardiac output and. ii. pathophysiology: vf/vt is uncommon in the pediatric age group, and the patients typically have underlying structural heart disease. other causes include brugada (a genetic disease), and prolonged qt syndromes, hypoxia, acidosis, electrolyte imbalance, hypothermia, or poisoning. iii. definitions: a. vf - ventricular fibrillation b. vt - ventricular tachycardia iv. department guideline: a. initiate cpr per aha guideline with compression rate of 100-120/min. b. apply cardiac monitor and confirm vf/vt. place defibrillation pads on patient. c. defibrillation at 2 j/kg. immediately resume cpr for 5 cycles or approximately 2 minutes. i. repeat defibrillation at 4 j/kg for subsequent shock. for additional defibrillations, increase by 2j/kg up to a maximum of 10j/kg (or adult dose) noting the maximum allowable joule setting per manufacture's guidelines. ii. perform rhythm analysis every 2 minutes between cycles of cpr. d. obtain or confirm iv/io access. e. manage airway and ensure adequate oxygenation and ventilation with positive pressure ventilation. i. initially administer o2 via bvm ii. secure airway and ventilate with 100% oxygen. intubate, if necessary, per guid3203-pr020 endotracheal intubation. iii. ventilate with 1 breath every 2 to 3 seconds (a rate of about 20-30 breaths per minute). avoid hyperventilation. iv. apply etco2 monitor if advanced airway present. f. administer i. epinephrine (1:10,000) 0.01 mg/kg (0.1 ml/kg) iv/io, maximum dose 1 mg. ii. epinephrine (1:1,000) 0.1 mg/kg (0.1 ml/kg) via ett, max dose 1 mg iii. re-dose every 3-5 minutes g. administer an anti-arrhythmic page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric ventricular fibrillation - pulseless ventricular tachycardia guideline number: guid-3203-ped013 i. amiodarone 5 mg/kg iv/io bolus, maximum dose 300 mg. may repeat bolus up to 2 times for a maximum daily dose of 15mg/kg for refractory vf/pulseless vt. ii. lidocaine 1 mg/kg iv/io, maximum dose 100 mg repeated every 5 minutes as needed at 0.5 mg/kg iv/io, until the dysrhythmia is suppressed or 3 mg/kg have been given. if the dysrhythmia is suppressed, follow with a 20 - 50 mcg/kg/min infusion. iii. for polymorphic vt or torsade's de pointes administer magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg iv/io h. for deterioration during transport after rosc, consider mechanical problems with the airway, ventilation, and/or blood volume. dysrhythmias are rare, except as an agonal event. consider: extubation, plugged endotracheal tube, right main stem bronchus intubation, distended stomach causing impaired lung expansion, pneumothorax, inadequate minute ventilation, ventilator malfunction, disconnected or depleted oxygen supply, hypovolemic shock, or agitation. i. report broselow color and/or estimated weight to receiving center prior to arrival j. out of hospital cessation of resuscitation efforts should not be considered in pediatric patients without contacting medical control. k. always consider the latest recommendations from the american heart association v. documentation: a. ems charts vi. references: a. pediatric advanced life support manual, 2020. american heart association. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Pediatric Reactive Airway Disease
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric reactive airway disease guideline number: guid-3203-ped014 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to relieve bronchospasm and decrease airway edema and mucous production in pediatric patients with reactive airway disease. ii. pathophysiology: reactive airway disease represents the constellation of signs and symptoms including bronchospasm, airway inflammation, and mucous production. severe exacerbations are those that produce an fev1 or pefr < 50% of the patient's predicted best. the cause of the process may be intrinsic (infection, cold air, exercise) or extrinsic (seasonal/allergic component). iii. definitions: iv. department guideline: a. assess airway, breathing, and circulation as per guid3203-ped001 - pediatric general management. b. monitor etco2 if intubated. optimize oxygen content - keep o2 sat 95%. c. assess for airway patency, anxiety/agitation, heart rate, pulse quality, skin perfusion, respiratory distress (retractions, grunting, and use of accessory muscles), and level of consciousness. d. gather history of asthma, including prior hospital admission or intubations e. if endotracheal intubation is needed, consider using ketamine 1-4.5 mg/kg (start at 2mg/kg) iv/io as the induction agent per guid3203-g008 - rapid sequence intubation f. use caution to provide an adequate expiatory time with mechanical ventilation g. treat with inhalation therapy per guid3203-pr012 - inhalation therapy i. nebulizer: pediatric > 12 years old: albuterol 2.5 mg/3ml q 20 min may repeat x3. if needed, continuous nebulizer dose: 10-15 mg/h ii. nebulizer: pediatric < 12 years old: albuterol 0.15-0.3 mg/kg q 20 min may repeat x3. if needed, continuous nebulizer dose: 0.5 mg/kg/h iii. may add ipratropium bromide 0.02% 0.5 mg (2.5 ml) to the initial albuterol nebulized x 1 dose. h. consider methylprednisolone 1-2 mg/kg iv (max dose 60 mg) if the patient has not had oral or parenteral bolus steroids at the sending institution. treat patients given po steroids who vomit within 30 minutes of medication administration. i. for severe bronchospasm consider magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg iv over 15-30 minutes, max dose 2 grams. j. beta-agonists are not routinely utilized. if needed, consider giving either: page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric reactive airway disease guideline number: guid-3203-ped014 i. terbutaline 0.25 mg (> 12 yrs old) or 0.005-0.01 mg/kg (< 12 years old) sc. may repeat in 15 minutes. max dose 0.5mg in 4 hours ii. epinephrine (1:1,000) 0.01 mg/kg im to 0.5mg max single dose, repeated prn every 20 minutes to 3 doses total. preferred im site is anterior thigh. k. trial bi-pap if at sending facility: set pressure support at 10 and peep at 5 if patient is alert with intact airway reflexes, not vomiting, and a mask fits appropriately. i. documentation: a. ems charts ii. references: a. pediatric advanced life support manual, 2020. american heart association. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Pediatric Seizures/Status Epilepticus
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric seizures/status epilepticus guideline number: guid-3203-ped015 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: emergency management is imperative for status epilepticus. the goal is to stop the seizure, identify and correct the underlying cause, and to maintain a patent airway while providing oxygenation and protecting the patient from injury. ii. pathophysiology: status epilepticus is a single generalized tonic-clonic seizure or series of convulsions lasting 30 or more minutes during which time the patient does not regain consciousness. it may also be associated with permanent neuronal destruction. causes of acute seizures include toxic/metabolic disturbances (hypernatremia, hyponatremia, hypocalcemia, hypomagnesemia, hypoglycemia, exogenous toxins, and inherited metabolic disorders), intracranial pathology (hemorrhage, arteriovenous malformations, aneurysms, brain tumor), anoxia, hypertension, trauma, withdrawal from drugs/alcohol, or change/withdrawal of medications in children with known seizures. causes of chronic seizures include idiopathic, post-anoxia injury, post-infectious injury, post- traumatic injury, post-hemorrhagic injury, post-toxic injury, degenerative diseases, or congenital disorders. classification of seizures  partial (focal) seizures : simple partial seizures or complex partial seizures.  generalized seizures: convulsive or non-convulsive, tonic, clonic, atonic, myoclonic.  unclassified epileptic seizures: all seizures that cannot be classified. iii. definitions: iv. department guideline: a. prepare a safe environment for the patient. protect patient from injury. b. assess and manage airway, breathing, and circulation as per guid3203-ped001 - pediatric general management. optimize oxygen content. c. if a patient requires airway management per guid3203-g008 rapid sequence intubation (rsi) for endotracheal (et) intubation, avoid long-acting paralytics which may mask ongoing seizure activity. d. establish iv/io access. e. if the blood sugar level is less than 60 d/l, treat per guid3203-m021 - hypoglycemia. f. if the patient is actively seizing, administer one of the following drugs i. midazolam 0.05-0.2 mg/kg iv/im/io/in repeat as necessary q 2-5 minutes - max single dose 5mg for iv/in and 10mg of im). no max total dose. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric seizures/status epilepticus guideline number: guid-3203-ped015 ii. lorazepam at 0.05-0.1 mg/kg iv/io, max dose 4mg and may repeat dose in 5 minutes. repeat as needed - no max total dose. iii. diazepam 0.5 mg/kg pr. may repeat q 10-15 min. g. if seizure activity continues, administer levetiracetam 60 mg/kg iv/io maximum dose 4.5 grams over 5 minutes. for doses >1000 mg, dilute vials in 100 ml of 0.9% ns and administer over 15 minutes. this is the preferred second line agent after benzodiazepines. h. if the patient has required multiple doses of the above medications, consult picu md to obtain an order for administration of: i. phenobarbital 15-20 mg/kg iv (not faster than 30 mg/min). may repeat every 15 min as needed. max total dose 1000 mg. a. because the risk of respiratory failure is high with the completion of the phenobarbital load, the infusion should be interrupted if the seizure stops before its completion. ii. fosphenytoin bolus loading dose of 10-20 mg pe/kg iv infusion at 50 mg pe/minute (max dose 1500 mg). iii. valproic acid 20-40 mg/kg iv/io (max dose 3000mg) i. reassess airway patency. j. if at sending facility and febrile seizure is suspected, determine last dose of antipyretic, and consider dose of rectal acetaminophen 20 mg/kg pr every 4 to 8 hours. v. documentation: a. ems charts vi. references: a. pediatric advanced life support manual, 2020. american heart association. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Pediatric Sepsis or Meningitis
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric sepsis or meningitis guideline number: guid-3203-ped016 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to provide supportive care to the patient, to minimize additional oxygen demands secondary to increased metabolism, and to limit the spread of any infectious process. ii. pathophysiology: sepsis can be bacterial, viral, fungal, parasitic, and mycobacterial in nature. the infectious stimulus may spread into the circulation resulting in the release of a cascade of inflammatory mediators, which can damage the vascular endothelium, leading to tissue hypoperfusion and eventual organ failure. the net result is that the patient will exhibit signs and symptoms of septic shock. patients develop systemic responses to the infection including fever or hypothermia, shaking, chills, hyperventilation, tachycardia, petechial or ecchymotic rash, confusion, agitation, lethargy, or coma. the child that develops septic shock may have delayed capillary refill, cyanosis, oliguria, or anuria, altered mental status, and hypoxemia. several scoring systems exist (bombard, sirs, etc.) to identify and risk stratify sepsis. monitoring tissue hypoperfusion (low map or elevated serum lactate levels) is the gold standard. meningitis is an inflammation of the meningeal coverings of the brain. the inflammation can be viral or bacterial. nonbacterial (aseptic or viral) meningitis is usually associated with other diseases, such as measles, mumps, herpes, enteroviruses, & leukemia. viral meningitis is non-purulent and usually resolves spontaneously. bacterial meningitis is most commonly caused by one of the 3 following organisms: haemophilus influenzae, neisseria meningitis, and streptococcus pneumoniae. meningococcal meningitis is readily transmitted by droplet infection from nasopharyngeal secretions. appropriate precautions and ppe must be utilized. iii. definitions: iv. department guideline: a. assess airway, breathing, circulation, and cns by evaluation of the level of consciousness as per guid3203-ped001 - pediatric general management. b. assess the patient for rashes, petechial hemorrhages, ecchymosis, fever, nuchal rigidity, headache, and mental status changes. physical examination in young infants should include assessment of anterior fontanel. c. inhaled bronchodilators may be considered to optimize ventilation per guid3203-ped014 - reactive airway disease and guid3203-pr012 - inhalation therapy. d. fluid resuscitation page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric sepsis or meningitis guideline number: guid-3203-ped016 i. if signs of poor perfusion, administer 0.9% ns or lactated ringers 20 ml/kg iv, may be repeated per guid3203-pr015 - intravenous fluid therapy. ii. most hypovolemic children will require at least 40-60 ml/kg in the first hour and those with sepsis may require up to 80 ml/kg in the first hour. iii. to help deliver fluid rapidly: place an in-line 3-way stopcock in the iv tubing system. deliver fluid by using a 30- to 60-ml syringe to push fluids through the stopcock or use a pressure bag (beware of risk of air embolism) or a rapid infusion device. iv. assess iv patency and determine amount of volume previously received a. antibiotics should be initiated when appropriate on inter-facility transfers prior to transport at the sending or receiving physician orders. continue antiviral therapy if initiated by referring facility. b. meningitis can cause hypoglycemia and hyponatremia. v. check blood glucose level. if < 60 d/l treat per guid3203-m021 - hypoglycemia. vi. seizures should be treated per guid3203- ped014 - pediatric seizures/status epilepticus. c. antipyretics should be considered on inter-facility transfers in febrile patients (acetaminophen 15 mg/kg po or ibuprofen 10 mg/kg po for children > 6 months of age). v. documentation: a. ems charts vi. references: a. pediatric advanced life support manual, 2020. american heart association. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Pediatric Supraventricular Tachycardia
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric supraventricular tachycardia guideline number: guid-3203-ped017 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: ensure prompt identification and provide intervention to restore a stable cardiac rhythm with adequate cardiac output and. ii. pathophysiology: supraventricular tachycardia (svt) is a regular, rapid rhythm that arises from either electrical re-entry or an ectopic focus. reentrant tachycardia is the most common. svt can occur in a normal heart, or in association with rheumatic heart disease, pericarditis, physiologic disturbance, mitral valve prolapse, or a pre-excitation syndrome. ectopic svt may be seen in patients with chronic lung disease, pneumonia, and poisoning. svt is the most common significant dysrhythmia in pediatric patients. it is often well tolerated. the loss of atrial contribution to cardiac output, coupled with decreased ventricular filling time, contributes to lower cardiac output and the resultant symptoms of the svt. - infant heart rates are usually >220 beats/minute. - children heart rates are usually >180 beats/minute. - p waves absent/abnormal and rr interval not variable iii. definitions: a. svt - supraventricular tachycardia iv. department guideline: a. assess and manage airway, breathing, and circulation as per guid3203-ped001 - pediatric general management. b. establish iv/io access. c. if signs of poor perfusion, administer 0.9% ns or lactated ringers 20 ml/kg iv, may be repeated per guid3203-pr015 - intravenous fluid therapy. d. obtain a 12-lead ecg if available. e. identify and treat possible causes of tachycardia: hypoxemia, hypovolemia, hyperthermia, metabolic disorders, cardiac tamponade, toxins, and pain. f. stable svt: a. attempt vagal maneuvers. i. place a bag with ice water over the upper half of an infant's face (without obstructing the airway) for 15 to 20 seconds. ii. ask older children to bear down, blow through an obstructed straw, or try to push the plunger out of a 10 ml syringe by blowing into the tip. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric supraventricular tachycardia guideline number: guid-3203-ped017 iii. if the child is stable and the rhythm does not convert, you may repeat the attempt. if the second attempt fails, select another method or provide medication therapy. if the child is unstable, attempt vagal maneuvers only while preparing for pharmacologic or electrical cardioversion. b. consider treating pain and/or anxiety per guid3203-g005 - analgesia and sedation management. c. adenosine 0.1 mg/kg rapid iv/io push with flush (max dose 6 mg) i. if the dysrhythmias persist after 1-2 minutes- adenosine may be repeated at 0.2 mg/kg iv (max dose 12mg) g. unstable svt: a. a trial of adenosine is appropriate only if there is no delay to therapy b. consider pre-medication treat pain and/or anxiety per guid3203-g005 - analgesia and sedation management. a. synchronized cardiovert per guid3203-3203 - cardioversion. i. synchronized cardiovert at 0.5 j/kg. reassess rhythm. ii. synchronized cardiovert at 1 j/kg. reassess rhythm. iii. synchronized cardiovert at 2 j/kg. reassess rhythm, repeat as necessary. b. amiodarone may be considered when treating hemodynamically unstable svt refractory to vagal maneuvers, adenosine, and cardioversion and for whom expert consultation is not available. use with caution if hepatic failure is present. amiodarone 5mg/kg iv over 20-60 minutes (max single dose: 150 mg). monitor for hypotension. c. if no response to therapy, contact medical control or seek expert opinion regarding procainamide or verapamil for children < 1 year old. h. always consider the latest recommendations from the american heart association. v. documentation: a. ems charts vi. references: a. pediatric advanced life support manual, 2020. american heart association. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Pediatric Ventricular Tachycardia with Pulse
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric ventricular tachycardia with pulse guideline number: guid-3203-ped018 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: ensure prompt identification and provide intervention to restore a stable cardiac rhythm with adequate cardiac output and. pathophysiology: sustained ventricular tachycardia (vt) carries the risk of deteriorating into ventricular fibrillation (vf). electrolyte imbalances, toxins, or cardiac ischemia can cause vt. it is uncommon in the pediatric patient. rate may vary from near normal to > 200 bpm. most children who develop vt have underlying heart disease (or have had surgery for heart disease), long qt syndrome, or myocarditis/ cardiomyopathy. conditions and agents that predispose to torsades de pointes include long qt syndromes (often congenital and inherited), hypomagnesemia, hypokalemia, antiarrhythmic drug toxicity (procainamide, flecainide, sotalol, amiodarone), and other drug toxicities (tricyclic antidepressants, calcium channel blockers, phenothiazines). causes of vt in children include electrolyte disturbances (e.g., hyperkalemia, hypocalcemia, hypomagnesemia), drug toxicity, and acute systemic illnesses or coronary artery abnormalities causing myocardial ischemia. ii. definitions: a. vt - ventricular tachycardia b. aha - american heart association iii. department guideline: a. assess and manage airway, breathing, and circulation. monitor etco2 if intubated. optimize oxygen content. b. establish iv/io access c. obtain a 12-lead ecg if available. if wide complex (greater than 0.09 seconds), treat as possible vt. d. if signs of poor perfusion, administer 0.9% ns or lactated ringers 20 ml/kg iv, may be repeated per guid3203-pr015 - intravenous fluid therapy. e. if the dysrhythmia is polymorphic vt (torsade de pointes), administer magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg iv/io as an initial therapy, maximum dose 2 gm, then proceed with traditional vt therapies. f. stable patient (without signs or symptoms related to the vt): i. apply appropriately sized pads in preparation for cardioversion or defibrillation. ii. if the patient becomes unstable, proceed to cardioversion as in the unstable patient part of this guideline section. iii. administer an anti-dysrhythmic. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric ventricular tachycardia with pulse guideline number: guid-3203-ped018 i. amiodarone 5 mg/kg iv/io over 20-60 minutes (max single dose 150mg). -or- ii. procainamide 15 mg/kg iv/io over 30-60 minutes. -or- iii. lidocaine 1mg/kg iv/io repeated every 5 minutes as needed at 0.5 mg/kg iv, until the dysrhythmia is suppressed or 3 mg/kg has been administered. if the dysrhythmia is suppressed, follow with infusion 20 - 50 mcg/kg/min iv. iv. seek expert consultation when giving amiodarone or procainamide. do not routinely administer amiodarone and procainamide together or with other medications that prolong the qt interval. if these initial efforts do not terminate the rapid rhythm, reevaluate the rhythm. g. unstable patient (with signs or symptoms related to the vt): i. consider pre-medication treat pain and/or anxiety per guid3203-g005 - analgesia and sedation management. ii. synchronized cardiovert per guid3203-3203 - cardioversion. i. synchronized cardiovert at 0.5 j/kg. reassess rhythm. ii. synchronized cardiovert at 1 j/kg. reassess rhythm. iii. synchronized cardiovert at 2 j/kg. reassess rhythm, repeat as necessary. iii. if the dysrhythmia persists after 4 cardioversions, attempt control with an anti-dysrhythmic as described in the stable patient part of this guideline. iv. after conversion, institute anti-arrhythmic therapy as described in the stable patient part of this guideline. v. if no response to therapy, contact medical control. h. always consider the latest recommendations from the american heart association. iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: a. pediatric advanced life support manual, 2020. american heart association. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

pages.text: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric ventricular tachycardia with pulse guideline number: guid-3203-ped018 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: ensure prompt identification and provide intervention to restore a stable cardiac rhythm with adequate cardiac output and. pathophysiology: sustained ventricular tachycardia (vt) carries the risk of deteriorating into ventricular fibrillation (vf). electrolyte imbalances, toxins, or cardiac ischemia can cause vt. it is uncommon in the pediatric patient. rate may vary from near normal to > 200 bpm. most children who develop vt have underlying heart disease (or have had surgery for heart disease), long qt syndrome, or myocarditis/ cardiomyopathy. conditions and agents that predispose to torsades de pointes include long qt syndromes (often congenital and inherited), hypomagnesemia, hypokalemia, antiarrhythmic drug toxicity (procainamide, flecainide, sotalol, amiodarone), and other drug toxicities (tricyclic antidepressants, calcium channel blockers, phenothiazines). causes of vt in children include electrolyte disturbances (e.g., hyperkalemia, hypocalcemia, hypomagnesemia), drug toxicity, and acute systemic illnesses or coronary artery abnormalities causing myocardial ischemia. ii. definitions: a. vt - ventricular tachycardia b. aha - american heart association iii. department guideline: a. assess and manage airway, breathing, and circulation. monitor etco2 if intubated. optimize oxygen content. b. establish iv/io access c. obtain a 12-lead ecg if available. if wide complex (greater than 0.09 seconds), treat as possible vt. d. if signs of poor perfusion, administer 0.9% ns or lactated ringers 20 ml/kg iv, may be repeated per guid3203-pr015 - intravenous fluid therapy. e. if the dysrhythmia is polymorphic vt (torsade de pointes), administer magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg iv/io as an initial therapy, maximum dose 2 gm, then proceed with traditional vt therapies. f. stable patient (without signs or symptoms related to the vt): i. apply appropriately sized pads in preparation for cardioversion or defibrillation. ii. if the patient becomes unstable, proceed to cardioversion as in the unstable patient part of this guideline section. iii. administer an anti-dysrhythmic. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017

1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: pediatric ventricular tachycardia with pulse guideline number: guid-3203-ped018 i. amiodarone 5 mg/kg iv/io over 20-60 minutes (max single dose 150mg). -or- ii. procainamide 15 mg/kg iv/io over 30-60 minutes. -or- iii. lidocaine 1mg/kg iv/io repeated every 5 minutes as needed at 0.5 mg/kg iv, until the dysrhythmia is suppressed or 3 mg/kg has been administered. if the dysrhythmia is suppressed, follow with infusion 20 - 50 mcg/kg/min iv. iv. seek expert consultation when giving amiodarone or procainamide. do not routinely administer amiodarone and procainamide together or with other medications that prolong the qt interval. if these initial efforts do not terminate the rapid rhythm, reevaluate the rhythm. g. unstable patient (with signs or symptoms related to the vt): i. consider pre-medication treat pain and/or anxiety per guid3203-g005 - analgesia and sedation management. ii. synchronized cardiovert per guid3203-3203 - cardioversion. i. synchronized cardiovert at 0.5 j/kg. reassess rhythm. ii. synchronized cardiovert at 1 j/kg. reassess rhythm. iii. synchronized cardiovert at 2 j/kg. reassess rhythm, repeat as necessary. iii. if the dysrhythmia persists after 4 cardioversions, attempt control with an anti-dysrhythmic as described in the stable patient part of this guideline. iv. after conversion, institute anti-arrhythmic therapy as described in the stable patient part of this guideline. v. if no response to therapy, contact medical control. h. always consider the latest recommendations from the american heart association. iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: a. pediatric advanced life support manual, 2020. american heart association. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Neonatal Stabilization & Resuscitation
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 standing orders guideline title: neonatal stabilization and resuscitation guideline number: guid-3203-ped019 issue date: 7/1/2024 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to ensure neonatal stabilization and resuscitation while undergoing transition from intrauterine to extrauterine life. these recommendations are also applicable to neonates who have completed perinatal transition and require resuscitation during the first few weeks following birth. ii. considerations for transport iii. definitions: iv. department guideline: a. stabilization i. determine the estimated gestational age (ega) based on last menstrual period or recent ultrasound and estimated date of confinement (edc). ask expected due date (edd) or it can be estimated from the lmp ii. provide warmth to the infant. iii. assess and manage airway, breathing and circulation. iv. if baby is a term gestation with good tone and normal breathing/crying, allow mother to hold while performing routine care v. perform 1 and 5 minute apgar assessment 1. an apgar score is given for each sign at 1 minute and 5 minutes after the birth. if there are problems with the baby an additional score is given at 10 minutes. 2. a score of 7 - 10 is considered normal, while 4 - 7 might require some resuscitative measures. apgars of 3 and below requires immediate resuscitation. if neonatal resuscitation is required, follow neonatal resuscitation program (nrp) guidelines. a. activity (muscle tone): i. 0 - limp; no movement. ii. 1 - some flexion of arms and legs. iii. 2 - active motion. b. pulse (heart rate): i. 0 - no heart rate. ii. 1 - fewer than 100 beats per minute. iii. 2 - at least 100 beats per minute. c. grimace (reflex response): page 1 of 4 © orlando health 2022 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 standing orders guideline title: neonatal stabilization and resuscitation guideline number: guid-3203-ped019 i. 0 - no response to airways being suctioned. ii. 1 - grimace during suctioning. iii. 2 - grimace and pull away, cough, or sneeze during suctioning. d. appearance (color): i. 0 - the baby's whole body is completely bluish-gray or pale. ii. 1 - good color in body with bluish hands or feet. iii. 2 - good color all over. e. respiration (breathing): i. 0 - not breathing. ii. 1 - weak cry; may sound like whimpering, slow or irregular breathing. iii. 2 - good, strong cry; normal rate and effort of breathing. b. initiate cardiac monitoring, pulse oximetry, and serial vital signs. 1. oxygen saturations rise gradually over the first ten minutes of birth, relative normal o2 sats are listed below: a. 1 min 60%-65% b. 2 min 65%-70% c. 3 min 70%-75% d. 4 min 75%-80% e. 5 min 80%-85% f. 10 min 85%-95% ii. reassess for neonatal distress; gasping respirations, hr < 100, central cyanosis. 1. if none, encourage bonding with mother/nursing prior to, but not during, transport. 2. if any distress noted proceed to resuscitation per nrp guidelines c. resuscitation i. provide warmth to the infant. 1. utilize external porta-warm mattress while avoiding direct contact with the infant's skin. ii. dry the baby and stimulate breathing. iii. simultaneously evaluate respirations, heart rate, and color. perform initial apgar score. 1. if apneic or hr < 100 bpm, begin positive-pressure ventilations. 2. if breathing and hr > 100 bpm but with central cyanosis, administer supplemental oxygen. 3. if breathing, hr > 100 bpm and pink, observation and continuous evaluation is indicated. iv. after 30 seconds, reassess for apnea or hr < 100/bpm: 1. if positive-pressure ventilations are effective and hr remains > 100/bpm, proceed stabilization 2. if labored breathing/cyanosis persists but hr > 60 bpm consider cpap page 2 of 4 © orlando health 2022 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 standing orders guideline title: neonatal stabilization and resuscitation guideline number: guid-3203-ped019 3. if despite adequate ventilation with supplemental oxygen the hr < 60 bpm, continue positive-pressure ventilations & begin chest compressions at rate >100 bpm. v. most neonates should *not* require endotracheal intubation. for prolonged resuscitation consult with receiving physician or medical control to consider tracheal intubation per guid 3203-pr020 endotracheal intubation with the following caveats: 1. the provider with the most experience intubating neonates performs the task 2. use of rsi medications are generally not indicated in the neonatal population. 3. choose cuffed tracheal tubes of appropriate size for patient: a. 2.5 tt for < 1,000 g and below 28 week gestation. b. 3.0 tt for 1,000 - 2,000 g (1 - 2 kg) and 28 - 34 weeks. c. 3.5 tt for 2,000 - 3,000 g (2 - 3 kg) and 34 - 48 weeks. d. 3.5 - 4.0 tt for > 3,000 g (3 kg). 4. for infants over 34 weeks old an appropriately sized lma or igel may be used 5. examine the patient for indications of congenital airway or maxillofacial anomalies that may make intubation difficult (e.g., micro or retrognathia, macroglossia, etc.). if airway anatomy is suspected or confirmed to be abnormal discuss with medical control. 6. place pulse oximetry preferably on right hand and cardiac monitor. 7. after intubation, maintain the tube's position by holding the ring finger firmly over the tube on the roof of the infant's mouth. 8. confirm correct depth of tube. a. 6 cm mark at teeth: < 1,000 g b. 7 cm mark at teeth: 1,000 - 2,000 g c. 8 cm mark at teeth: 2,000 - 3,000 g d. 9 cm mark at teeth: 3,000 - 4,000 g e. 10 cm mark at teeth: 4,000 g 9. secure the endotracheal tube with commercial device or tape. 10. reconfirm appropriate position of tracheal tube. 11. after tt is secure, or if prolonged bls manual ventilation is required, place an og tube to decompress the stomach. vi. establish iv/io access. vii. consider naloxone 0.1 mg/kg iv/io per nrp guidelines only if patient cannot be adequately ventilated. viii. after 30 seconds, reassess hr: 1. if hr remains < 60 bpm despite adequate ventilation and chest compressions: a. administer epinephrine 0.01 mg/kg iv/ io (1 mg/10 ml (0.1 mg/ml)) b. if no iv/io then administer epinephrine 0.1 mg/kg (1 mg/10 ml (0.1 mg/ml)) via the tracheal tube, maximum volume 3 ml. c. repeat epinephrine dose every 3 - 5 minutes as needed. d. consider ns bolus of 10 ml/kg iv/io. page 3 of 4 © orlando health 2022 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 standing orders guideline title: neonatal stabilization and resuscitation guideline number: guid-3203-ped019 ix. if at any time resuscitation is effective and hr remains > 100 bpm, proceed to stabilization v. documentation: ems charts vi. references: a. apgar scoring page 4 of 4 © orlando health 2022

title: 12-Lead Electrocardiogram (ECG/EKG)
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: 12-lead electrocardiogram (ecg/ekg) guideline number: guid-3203-pr001 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to obtain 12 lead ecg with the following: a. patients who have recently experienced chest discomfort, related complaints, or active chest pain. b. any patient with suspected dysrhythmias c. any patient in whom the transport team suspects myocardial injury, insult or disruption. ii. definitions: none iii. department guideline: a. place leads on each rue, rle, lue, lle for the limb leads. b. place precordial leads as noted. c. acquire and print 12 lead ecg report. d. serial ecg should be performed if indicated and time permits iv. documentation: ems charts v. references: none lead location v1 4th intercostals space to the right of the sternum v2 4th intercostals space to the left of the sternum v3 directly between leads v2 and v4 v4 5th intercostal space at midclavicular line v5 level with v4 at the left anterior axillary line v6 level with v5 at left mid axillary line page 1 of 1 © orlando health 2017

title: Blood Product Administration
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: blood product administration guideline number: guid-3203-pr002 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to provide safe and effective blood and blood product administration from a sending facility and to recognize and effectively treat transfusion reactions. ii. definitions: none iii. department guideline: a. equipment: i. personal protective equipment. ii. appropriate blood product. iii. filtered blood tubing or leukocyte reduction filter. iv. a one-liter bag of 0.9 % normal saline (ns). v. pressure bag b. indications: i. hypovolemia with acute blood loss. ii. restoration of clotting factors with ffp c. contraindications: i. patient's refusal of blood or blood products. ii. the unavailability of type specific blood or type o negative d. procedure: i. utilize appropriate protective equipment. ii. initially assess the following: 1. vital signs. 2. skin condition; i.e. rashes, redness, etc. 3. size and integrity of iv site. a. adults: prefer 18 g for prbcs and 20 g for other products. b. pediatrics: 22-24 g. c. never transfuse blood in piggyback fashion into any iv with medications infusing other than ns. 4. breath sounds. page 1 of 3 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: blood product administration guideline number: guid-3203-pr002 5. fluid volume status. 6. history of previous transfusion reactions. iii. if available, obtain copy of patient's blood type & screen from transferring facility. iv. verify order for blood product transfusion. ensure consent form has been signed. v. ideally, the blood should be warm. vi. check the unit of blood for: (have two providers check simultaneously) 1. patient's name. 2. patient's blood type. 3. blood product type. 4. blood product abo type. 5. unit number. 6. expiration date. vii. confirm that the above are correct and consistent with the patient. viii. once the unit of blood and the patient have been correctly correlated, document the person hanging the blood. sign with date and time. ix. flush iv with 0.9 % ns until clear. x. may place blood in pressure bag and inflate the bag not to exceed 300 mmhg. xi. start transfusion slowly; assess vital signs every 10 minutes. watch for a transfusion reaction or complications. xii. if a transfusion reaction occurs: 1. stop transfusion immediately. 2. assess vital signs. 3. remove blood bag and tubing and set aside. 4. assure bag and tubing are given to receiving rn/appropriate medical staff. 5. provide systemic support as required. 6. transfusion reactions can be defined as shown in the chart below. xiii. when transfusion is complete: 1. flush iv with 0.9 % ns until clear 2. record time of completion on tag xiv. additional points: 1. pediatric transfusion is 10 ml/kg. 2. all transfusions should be completed within 4 hours. 3. review potassium and calcium levels if multiple transfusions received. iv. documentation: a. ems charts - unit #, blood product type (ffp, prbc, factor etc.), time transfusion was initiated and completed, volume and rate infused, reaction yes/no, vital signs before transfusion and when completed v. references: none page 2 of 3 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: blood product administration guideline number: guid-3203-pr002 transfusion reactions type onset cause s/s treatment febrile first 15 mins presence of bacterial pathogens sensitivity to leukocytes / platelets hemolytic episode fever and shaking chills within 30 min. shock soon after ns infusion tylenol systemic support vasopressor infusion per guid- 3203-m011 allergic 15-30 mins sensitivity to plasma protein hives generalized itching wheezing anaphylaxis benadryl 25-50mg ivp - if patient has history of reactions pre-medicate with benadryl septic 15-30 mins contaminated blood with bacteria chills high fever vomiting & diarrhea hypotension ns infusion vasopressor infusion per guid- 3203-m011 solumedrol 125mg ivp hemolytic 30 mins blood incompatible with patient leading to antibodies in patient's plasma rapidly combining with donor rbcs and cells are hemolyzed chills low back pain ha nausea cp fever hypotension vascular collapse ns infusion vasopressor infusion per guid- 3203-m011 page 3 of 3 © orlando health 2017

title: Capillary Blood Glucose Monitoring
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: capillary blood glucose monitoring guideline number: guid-3203-pr003 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to obtain capillary or venous blood sample and measure a blood glucose level. ii. definitions: iii. department guideline: a. equipment: i. appropriately calibrated blood glucose testing device. onboard is currently the poct nova statstrip xpress2 glucose hospital meter. see orlando health policy #3888 for testing guidelines using this device. ii. test strip, lancet, alcohol prep pad b. indications: i. known patient with history of diabetes experiencing an acute medical emergency ii. altered mental status, suspected stroke, or confirmed interfacility stroke transfer iii. suspected overdose, poisoning, or toxic exposure iv. unresponsiveness v. any neonate or pediatric patient prior to transport vi. any trauma patient vii. acute seizure viii. patients receiving a continuous insulin drip c. contraindications: testing results may not be accurate if hematocrits are < 20 g/dl or > 70 g/dl d. procedure: i. select and prepare appropriate site with alcohol prep pad 1. pad of the finger in the adult or pediatric patient 2. heel of the neonate 3. obtain blood from venous access site if patient has decreased peripheral blood flow such as in acute shock, hypotension, or on multiple vasopressors. neonate whole blood is not allowed. ii. insert test strip in blood glucose testing device, while allowing prepared site to dry. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: capillary blood glucose monitoring guideline number: guid-3203-pr003 iii. puncture prepared site with lancet and allow a large drop of blood to form. iv. apply test strip to blood droplet and allow device to calculate the reading. v. control bleeding and bandage puncture site. vi. if glucose level < 60 mg/dl, & symptomatic, treat per guid3203-m021 hypoglycemia. iv. documentation: a. record blood glucose in ems charts v. references: page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Cardioversion
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: cardioversion guideline number: guid-3203-pr004 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to restore hemodynamic stability in a patient who exhibits a tachydysrhythmia that compromises effective perfusion ii. department guideline: a. indications: i. ventricular tachycardia with a pulse (unstable) ii. atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (unstable) iii. atrial flutter with rapid ventricular response (unstable) iv. supraventricular tachycardia (unstable) b. contraindications: patient is wet or for any other reason cannot be electrically isolated from the medical team members. c. equiptment: i. cardiac monitor with ability to cardiovert ii. conductive hands-free defibrillation pads 1. adult 2. pediatric pads for infants/children under 15 kg. d. procedure: i. if the procedure is to be performed in flight, notify the pilot of the intent to cardiovert. do not proceed further until authorized to do so by the pilot. ii. place conductive hands-free pads on the patient's bare chest. 1. anterior / posterior: place negative electrode on left anterior chest, halfway between the xiphoid process and the left nipple. this corresponds with the v2-v3 ecg position. place the positive electrode on left posterior chest beneath the scapula and lateral to the spine. 2. anterior / anterior: place the negative electrode on the left chest, mid-axilla over the fourth intercostal space. place positive electrode on anterior right chest, sub- clavicular area. 3. avoid placing the pads over any implantable electrical device. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: cardioversion guideline number: guid-3203-pr004 iii. confirm the need to cardiovert per guid3203-c003 atrial fibrillation & atrial flutter or guid3203-c006 narrow and wide complex tachycardia iv. consider sedation or analgesia per guid3203-g005 analgesia and sedation management if map > 65 mmhg. v. turn the monitor/defibrillator on. locate defib mode if using a non-department monitor. vi. initiate the "synchronize" mode by pressing sync on the zoll x series and confirm that indicator markers appear on the oscilloscope on the r waves of each complex (the size of the complex may need to be adjusted or the lead may need to be changed for adequate synch confirmation). vii. select the joule dose by adjusting the energy level arrow buttons based on current recommended dosing. 1. for adult: a. for narrow regular rhythm: 50-100 j b. for narrow irregular rhythm: 120-200 j c. for wide regular complex: 100 j d. for wide irregular: defibrillation dose, not synchronized e. if no response after initial cardioversion, give 2nd dose of 200 j. f. subsequent doses should be higher than initial dose up to max joule dose of monitor. (other facility/agency monitors maximum may be up to 360 j). 2. for pediatric: a. initial: synchronized cardiovert at 0.5 to 1 j/kg. b. subsequent doses: synchronized cardiovert at 2 j/kg. c. if no response to therapy, contact medical control. viii. charge the monitor/defibrillator to the appropriate energy level. ix. when defibrillator is fully charged, notify team members of the intent to cardiovert by stating loudly and clearly "clear for shock" and assuring that nobody is touching the patient or stretcher. x. push the shock button to discharge the shock, realizing that it may take a few seconds to synchronize to the rhythm and cardiovert the patient. xi. if the synchronization is not coordinating and the patient is markedly unstable, unsynchronized cardioversion should be performed by inactivating the "synchronize" mode and performing defibrillation at the appropriate energy level. xii. observe for any change in rhythm or vital signs. xiii. repeat as appropriate for the patient's condition ensuring the sync mode is selected. iii. documentation: a. ems charts iv. references: a. american heart association, 2015. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

pages.text: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: cardioversion guideline number: guid-3203-pr004 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to restore hemodynamic stability in a patient who exhibits a tachydysrhythmia that compromises effective perfusion ii. department guideline: a. indications: i. ventricular tachycardia with a pulse (unstable) ii. atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (unstable) iii. atrial flutter with rapid ventricular response (unstable) iv. supraventricular tachycardia (unstable) b. contraindications: patient is wet or for any other reason cannot be electrically isolated from the medical team members. c. equiptment: i. cardiac monitor with ability to cardiovert ii. conductive hands-free defibrillation pads 1. adult 2. pediatric pads for infants/children under 15 kg. d. procedure: i. if the procedure is to be performed in flight, notify the pilot of the intent to cardiovert. do not proceed further until authorized to do so by the pilot. ii. place conductive hands-free pads on the patient's bare chest. 1. anterior / posterior: place negative electrode on left anterior chest, halfway between the xiphoid process and the left nipple. this corresponds with the v2-v3 ecg position. place the positive electrode on left posterior chest beneath the scapula and lateral to the spine. 2. anterior / anterior: place the negative electrode on the left chest, mid-axilla over the fourth intercostal space. place positive electrode on anterior right chest, sub- clavicular area. 3. avoid placing the pads over any implantable electrical device. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017

1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: cardioversion guideline number: guid-3203-pr004 iii. confirm the need to cardiovert per guid3203-c003 atrial fibrillation & atrial flutter or guid3203-c006 narrow and wide complex tachycardia iv. consider sedation or analgesia per guid3203-g005 analgesia and sedation management if map > 65 mmhg. v. turn the monitor/defibrillator on. locate defib mode if using a non-department monitor. vi. initiate the "synchronize" mode by pressing sync on the zoll x series and confirm that indicator markers appear on the oscilloscope on the r waves of each complex (the size of the complex may need to be adjusted or the lead may need to be changed for adequate synch confirmation). vii. select the joule dose by adjusting the energy level arrow buttons based on current recommended dosing. 1. for adult: a. for narrow regular rhythm: 50-100 j b. for narrow irregular rhythm: 120-200 j c. for wide regular complex: 100 j d. for wide irregular: defibrillation dose, not synchronized e. if no response after initial cardioversion, give 2nd dose of 200 j. f. subsequent doses should be higher than initial dose up to max joule dose of monitor. (other facility/agency monitors maximum may be up to 360 j). 2. for pediatric: a. initial: synchronized cardiovert at 0.5 to 1 j/kg. b. subsequent doses: synchronized cardiovert at 2 j/kg. c. if no response to therapy, contact medical control. viii. charge the monitor/defibrillator to the appropriate energy level. ix. when defibrillator is fully charged, notify team members of the intent to cardiovert by stating loudly and clearly "clear for shock" and assuring that nobody is touching the patient or stretcher. x. push the shock button to discharge the shock, realizing that it may take a few seconds to synchronize to the rhythm and cardiovert the patient. xi. if the synchronization is not coordinating and the patient is markedly unstable, unsynchronized cardioversion should be performed by inactivating the "synchronize" mode and performing defibrillation at the appropriate energy level. xii. observe for any change in rhythm or vital signs. xiii. repeat as appropriate for the patient's condition ensuring the sync mode is selected. iii. documentation: a. ems charts iv. references: a. american heart association, 2015. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Chest Tube Management
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: chest tube management guideline number: guid-3203-pr005 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to maintain the chest tube and device used to evacuate air and fluid from the pleural cavity and/or to restore normal dynamics of pulmonary function. ii. definitions: iii. department guideline: a. indications: a chest tube in place as a result of the following- hemothorax, pneumothorax, chylothorax (lymphatic fluid leak), or pleural effusion b. contraindications: none c. procedure: assess the patient, tube, and chamber to assure proper functioning. i. patient: assess for respiratory distress, lung sounds, subcutaneous emphysema, vital signs, chest, x-ray ii. tube: 1. location of the tube: right/left pleural or mediastinal 2. drainage holes should not be visible external to the chest wall. if they are visible: a. cover with occlusive dressing or consider replacement of tube. b. never advance a chest tube once the sterile field has been removed. 3. make sure connections are tight. 4. tape the tube to the patient very well 5. look for clots in tube. if clots are present, milk but do not strip tube. iii. unit: water filled unit (i.e. atrium): 1. it should be about 12 inches below the patient and below the level of patient's chest when transferring patient to aircraft. 2. confirm ordered amount of pressure. ensure the suction source vacuum is at -80 mmhg or higher. continue suction en route to receiving facility. 3. assess water seal. water seal chamber should be at 2 cm. 4. bubbling: a. gentle bubbling is the goal. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: chest tube management guideline number: guid-3203-pr005 b. absent is appropriate if the bubbling unit is not hooked to suction and the remainder of patient assessment is stable. c. vigorous is indicative of excessive air leak or suction is set too high. d. recheck all connections 5. tidaling: a. with respirations is good b. decreased could mean an obstruction c. excessive could mean distress. 6. fluid level should be per manufacturer's specs. iv. waterless unit: 1. dial in the pressure to 20 cm of water 2. attach to suction as necessary. v. output: 1. note output since insertion (past 8 hours if in place > 24 hours) + output in transport. 2. consider blood product replacement. vi. use a y-connector to connect to chambers to one suction unit if necessary. vii. avoid dependent loops in the tubing. viii. do not clamp the drainage tubing at any time unless you are about to change the drainage system or assessing for the location of an air leak. ix. if the drainage system is knocked over, gently tip the drain to the right to return the fluid to the correct column. x. heimlich valves are for pneumothorax only. if heimlich valve present, ensure valve is connected to tube securely xi. frequently reassess patient to ensure airway/ventilation remains intact. monitor for signs of tension or respiratory distress. iv. documentation: ems charts v. references: page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017
