title: Tourniquet Therapy
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: tourniquet therapy guideline number: guid-3203-pr006 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to stop blood loss and provide hemorrhage control by applying pressure to an artery using combat application tourniquet (cat) or a swat-t (stretch, wrap, and tuck tourniquet). ii. definitions: iii. department guideline: a. indications: to control potentially fatal hemorrhagic wounds only after other means of stopping blood loss have failed (direct pressure, a pressure dressing, wound packing) such as a mangled extremity or traumatic amputation. b. contraindications: do not place tourniquet over joint. c. apply appropriate personal protective equipment (ppe). d. expose the extremity by removing clothing in proximity to the injury. e. procedure for cat: i. place directly over exposed skin at least 5 cm proximal to the injury. ii. route the self-adhering band around the extremity. iii. pass the band through the outside slit of the buckle. iv. pull the self-adhering band tight. v. twist the rod until bright red bleeding stops and the distal pulse is eliminated. vi. lock the rod in place with the clip. f. procedure for swat-t: i. consider placing bandage material in or around wound. ii. wrap the swat-t above the wound area. wrap once around the extremity to ensure the tourniquet is secure against the skin. iii. pull the tourniquet tight against the extremity until the diamonds become squares. iv. wrap around the extremity. on the last wrap, create a loop, then tuck the end under the loop. the swat-t will grip itself to remain in place. g. reassessment, continuation and removal i. recheck for bleeding and distal pulse. if bleeding is not controlled, consider additional tightening or apply a second tourniquet. ii. clearly record the date/time of application of the tourniquet and relay this information to the receiving facility. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: tourniquet therapy guideline number: guid-3203-pr006 iii. keep tourniquet on throughout the transport - a correctly applied tourniquet should only be removed by the receiving hospital. iv. documentation: ems chart v. references: a. http://swat-t.com/#intro b. www.stopthebleed.org page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Cricothyrotomy – Surgical
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: cricothyrotomy - surgical guideline number: guid-3203-pr007 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to allow rapid entrance into the airway for ventilation and oxygenation when other means of airway control (bag valve mask, intubation, etc.) have proven unsuccessful. ii. definitions: a. etco2 - end tidal carbon dioxide iii. department guideline: a. equipment: i. oxygen ii. suction iii. bag valve mask iv. using the h&h emergency cricothyrotomy kit 1. contents of kit includes: #10 scalpel, 10 ml syringe, 35 f bougie, tracheal hook, kelly forceps/clamp, short 6.0 airway catheter (trach) with flange, tracheal ties 2. additional supplies needed: 4 x 4's, chlorohexidine or betadine cleaning solution v. for standard surgical technique 1. #10 scalpel 2. 6.0 - 7.0 ett 3. chlorohexidine or betadine cleaning solution 4. gauze 5. kelly forceps/clamp iv. indications: a. inability to oxygenate or ventilate the patient by any other means including subglottic airway b. total airway obstruction c. severe trauma compromising airway v. contraindications: a. the ability to obtain airway control and effective ventilation by less invasive means. b. pediatric patients (< 8 years old) c. inability to identify proper landmarks. page 1 of 3 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: cricothyrotomy - surgical guideline number: guid-3203-pr007 vi. procedures: a. using the h&h emergency cricothyroid kit i. open the airway and position the head so the neck is clearly visible. if the patient has sustained any type of spinal trauma, always maintain cervical spine precautions. ii. after locating and palpating the cricothyroid membrane, consider marking with a pen. iii. clean the area with betadine or chlorohexidine. iv. attach the 10 ml syringe to the balloon port. ensure bougie is through the tracheal tube. v. stabilize the cricothyroid membrane and make a 2-3 cm vertical incision in the midline using the #10 scalpel blade. the incision should be long enough to accommodate the dilator and trach. dissect bluntly through the subcutaneous tissues until the membrane is visible. vi. make a second 1.5-2 cm horizontal incision through the cricoid membrane using the scalpel blade. vii. if bleeding occurs, control with forceps and gauze. suction area as needed. viii. place the hook into the hole and apply mild traction towards the head of the patient. ix. introduce the bougie until you feel the resistance of the carina. x. remove the hook and introduce the tracheal tube. xi. remove the bougie. xii. inflate the cuff. xiii. manually secure the tracheostomy tube while beginning to ventilate the patient using a bag valve device with etco2 waveform capnography attached. xiv. confirm placement by auscultating for equal, bilateral breath sounds and observing for equal, bilateral chest expansion. xv. secure the tracheostomy tube in place with tracheostomy ties. b. using standard surgical technique i. open the airway and position the head so the neck is clearly visible. if the patient has sustained any type of spinal trauma, always maintain cervical spine precautions ii. after locating and palpating the cricothyroid membrane, consider marking with a pen. iii. clean the area thoroughly with chlorohexidine or betadine. iv. stabilize the cricothyroid membrane and make a 2cm vertical incision in the midline using the #10 scalpel blade. dissect bluntly through the subcutaneous tissues until the membrane is visible. v. make a second 1.5-2 cm horizontal incision through the cricoid membrane using the scalpel blade. vi. use a curved hemostat to apply traction to the cricothyroid membrane allowing for tube insertion. vii. introduce a bougie until you feel the resistance of the carina. viii. insert an appropriately sized, cuffed endotracheal tube into the cricothyroid membrane incision, directing the tube distally into the trachea until the cuff is securely in the trachea ix. inflate the cuff. page 2 of 3 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: cricothyrotomy - surgical guideline number: guid-3203-pr007 x. manually secure the tube while beginning to ventilate the patient using a bag valve device with etco2 waveform capnography attached. xi. confirm placement by auscultating for equal, bilateral breath sounds and observing for equal, bilateral chest expansion. xii. secure tube with cloth tape if available c. illustration of tracheal anatomy vii. documentation: ems charts viii. references: page 3 of 3 © orlando health 2017

title: Defibrillation
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: defibrillation guideline number: guid-3203-pr008 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: restoration of a perfusing cardiac rhythm in a patient exhibiting pulseless ventricular tachycardia or ventricular fibrillation ii. department guideline: a. equipment: i. cardiac monitor/defibrillator. current department monitor used is the zoll x series. ii. conductive hands-free defibrillation pads 1. adult 2. pediatric pads for infants/children under 15 kg. b. indications: i. ventricular fibrillation ii. pulseless ventricular tachycardia. c. contraindications: patient is wet or for any other reason cannot be electrically isolated from the medical team members. d. procedure: i. if the procedure is to be performed in flight, notify the pilot of the intent to cardiovert. do not proceed further until authorized to do so by the pilot. ii. place conductive hands-free pads on the patient's bare chest per manufacturers guidelines. 1. anterior / posterior: place negative electrode on left anterior chest, halfway between the xiphoid process and the left nipple. this corresponds with the v2-v3 ecg position. place the positive electrode on left posterior chest beneath the scapula and lateral to the spine. 2. anterior / anterior: place the negative electrode on the left chest, mid-axilla over the fourth intercostal space. place positive electrode on anterior right chest, sub- clavicular area. 3. avoid placing the pads over any implantable electrical device. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: defibrillation guideline number: guid-3203-pr008 iii. confirm the need to defibrillate/deliver unsynchronized shock per guid3203-c007 ventricular fibrillation and pulseless ventricular tachycardia. iv. turn the monitor/defibrillator on. locate defib mode if using a non-department monitor. v. select the joule dose by adjusting the energy level arrow buttons based on current recommended dosing. 1. adults: 120-200 j for biphasic 2. pediatric: defibrillation at 2 j/kg vi. charge the monitor/defibrillator to the appropriate energy level. vii. when defibrillator is fully charged, notify team members of the intent to defibrillate by stating loudly and clearly "clear for shock" and assuring that nobody is touching the patient or stretcher. viii. push the shock button to discharge the shock, defibrillating the patient. ix. immediately after shock delivery, resume cpr beginning with compressions for 5 cycles (about 2 minutes), and then recheck rhythm. interruption of compressions should be minimized. x. observe for any change in end-tidal co2. xi. repeat as appropriate for the patient's condition. second and subsequent doses should be equivalent or higher. 1. adult: up to the highest setting of 200 j for the zoll x monitor. a. max setting may be higher for other facility/agency monitors e.g., 360 j. 2. pediatric: second shock at 4 j/kg. subsequent shocks ≥ 4 j/kg to a maximum of 10 j/kg or adult dose. xii. after three unsuccessful defibrillation attempts in an adult patient, if a second monitor is available, apply a second set of pads in the anterior-posterior (or anterior-anterior if a/p placement was used first) connected to the second monitor and perform dual sequential defibrillation (dsed) by pushing the shock button on the second monitor 1 second after pressing shock on the initial monitor (use maximum setting, up to 360 j, on both monitors) iii. documentation: a. ems charts iv. references: a. american heart association, 2015. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

cycles (about 2 minutes), and then recheck rhythm. interruption of compressions should be minimized. x. observe for any change in end-tidal co2. xi. repeat as appropriate for the patient's condition. second and subsequent doses should be equivalent or higher. 1. adult: up to the highest setting of 200 j for the zoll x monitor. a. max setting may be higher for other facility/agency monitors e.g., 360 j. 2. pediatric: second shock at 4 j/kg. subsequent shocks ≥ 4 j/kg to a maximum of 10 j/kg or adult dose. xii. after three unsuccessful defibrillation attempts in an adult patient, if a second monitor is available, apply a second set of pads in the anterior-posterior (or anterior-anterior if a/p placement was used first) connected to the second monitor and perform dual sequential defibrillation (dsed) by pushing the shock button on the second monitor 1 second after pressing shock on the initial monitor (use maximum setting, up to 360 j, on both monitors) iii. documentation: a. ems charts iv. references: a. american heart association, 2015. page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: End-Tidal CO2 Monitoring
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: end-tidal co2 monitoring guideline number: guid-3203-pr009 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: end-tidal co2 detector revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to assist in the confirmation of proper endotracheal tube placement ii. definitions: a. ett- endotracheal tube b. etco2 - end tidal carbon dioxide c. gcs - glasgow coma scale iii. department guideline: a. equipment: i. in-line etco2 detector ii. nasal cannula etco2 detector iii. monitor with digital capnometry and capnography cable/sensor/adaptor. b. indications: i. in line: all patients who are intubated, have a supraglottic airway device [king tube, laryngeal mask airway (lma), i gel] or advanced alternative airway in place. ii. nasal cannula capnometry 1. use in non-intubated patients with a gcs < 14 who receive sedation or analgesia. 2. patients who are a gcs > 8 but show signs of decreased work of breathing. c. contraindications: none. d. procedure: i. digital capnometry or waveform capnography are the referenced standards for confirmation and maintenance of ett placement. ii. digital capnometry and capnography: 1. choose age/size appropriate adapter for specific monitor. 2. prepare monitor for use. 3. disconnect the tube from the ventilation circuit and connect the etco2 adapter to the tube or if high efficiency particulate air (hepa) filter present, connect between the filter and the ventilator. 4. reconnect the ventilation circuit and resume ventilation of the patient. 5. observe the unit for a numeric value and a waveform. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: end-tidal co2 monitoring guideline number: guid-3203-pr009 a. an appropriate, reproducible, 4-phase waveform with a numeric value confirms that the tube is placed in the trachea. b. an inappropriate, flatline, or non-reproducible waveform suggests the tube is incorrectly placed. remove the ett and replace with a secure airway. c. a reproducible, 4-phase waveform with an extremely low (≤ 10 mmhg) etco2 suggests very poor perfusion or extreme acidosis. 6. begin continuous monitoring of capnometric and capnographic etco2 to give a breath-by-breath status of tube placement and ventilatory adequacy throughout transport. 7. etco2 goal a. 35-45 mmhg for most patients b. 35-40 mmhg if head injury present c. 30-35 mmhg if there is evidence of herniation with head injury per guid3203-t014 - traumatic brain injury. 8. assess waveform for bronchospasm iii. if monitoring device were to fail and colorimetric etco2 detector available: 1. inspect ez cap device to ensure purple color prior to application. 2. apply the ez cap to the tube after placement in a patient's mouth or nose. 3. ventilate with a bag valve device attached to the ez cap a minimum of six ventilations with 100% oxygen. 4. observe the color on the ez cap. a. purple should change to gold if co2 is present in the exhaled air. this is indicative of correct placement of the ett in the trachea. b. absent or poor color change is suggestive of incorrect placement of the ett or absence of circulation. confirm tube placement by direct laryngoscopy, if possible, or remove the tube and re-attempt intubation. ("purple with a pulse is poor…pull it out. gold is good.") iv. nasal cannula with capnometry 1. use in non-intubated patients with a gcs < 14 who receive sedation or analgesia. 2. patients who are a gcs > 8 but show signs of decreased work of breathing. 3. persistently low etco2 (sustained < 25 mmhg) is a sign of poor perfusion or metabolic acidosis associated with poor outcomes. iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: none page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Gastric Tube Insertion
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: gastric tube insertion guideline number: guid-3203-pr010 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: gastric decompression pr010 revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to effectively remove the gaseous and liquid contents of a patient's stomach via a nasally (ng) or orally (og) placed tube. also provides a means of administering enteral medications to a patient otherwise unable to ingest them. ii. definitions: none iii. department guideline: a. equipment: 1. personal protective equipment (ppe) 2. ng/og tube, appropriate size 3. water soluble lubricant 4. 60 ml catheter tip syringe 5. tape or other securing device 6. stethoscope b. indications: 1. gastric emptying or gastric decompression a. comatose, gastrointestinal bleeding, overdose, gastric distention/obstruction, cardiac arrest, intubated patient/advanced alternative airway with a port c. contraindications: 1. absolute contraindications a. esophageal rupture, caustic ingestion (alkaline), nasogastric placement with nasal or midface trauma (orogastric tube may be placed), recent esophageal or throat surgery, recent nasal surgery (orogastric tube may be placed). 2. relative contraindications/considerations a. recent banding for esophageal varices, esophageal varices, esophageal stricture, coagulation abnormality (if known). d. be aware of complications: nasal bleeding, gagging, airway obstruction, placement in trachea e. procedure: orogastric (og) 1. preferred route in intubated patients. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: gastric tube insertion guideline number: guid-3203-pr010 2. measure the og tube alongside the patient. the end of the tube should be placed at the corner of the mouth, to the tip of the ear, and extended to the xyphoid process. this is the depth the tube should be inserted. 3. insert tube into the oral cavity and advance into the stomach. 4. after the tube has been inserted, assess the tube for correct placement. a 60 ml syringe should be attached to the tube and gastric contents should be aspirated. 20 ml of air should be instilled into the abdomen while listening or feeling for an "air rush" over the patient's abdomen. 5. once gastric placement is confirmed, secure the tube in place with tape. 6. the tube should not be clamped if patient is to be transported by aircraft. f. procedure: nasogastric (ng) 1. explain the procedure to the patient. 2. measure the ng tube alongside the patient. the end of the tube should be placed at the tip of the nose, extended to the corner of the ear, and then to the xyphoid process. this is the depth the tube should be inserted. 3. lubricate the end of the tube and begin to insert it into one of the patient's nares. pass the tube gently along the floor of the nasal passage and advance it as the patient swallows. remove the tube if patient coughs or experiences dyspnea. 4. after the ng tube has been inserted, assess the tube for correct placement. a 60 ml syringe should be attached to the tube and gastric contents should be aspirated. 20ml of air should be instilled into the abdomen while listening or feeling over the abdomen for an "air rush". 5. once gastric placement is confirmed, secure the ng tube in place with tape. 6. the ng tube should not be clamped if patient is to be transported by aircraft. g. post placement 1. consider hooking og/ng to low intermittent suction or review orders for suction. 2. measure and record amount of output, color, and consistency. iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: a. none page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Gum Elastic Bougie
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: gum elastic bougie guideline number: guid-3203-pr011 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: end-tidal co2 detector revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to assist with placing an oral endotracheal tube during difficult intubations or cricothyrotomy ii. definitions: a. ett - endotracheal tube iii. department guideline: a. indications: i. should be considered/available in all intubations. ii. most useful when orotracheal intubation is considered difficult due to unfavorable anatomy or known or suspected neck trauma/limiting neck mobility iii. adult bougie: ett size ≥ 6.0 iv. pediatric bougie: ett size < 6.0 b. contraindications: i. should not be used for nasal intubations c. procedure: i. assess airway and need for a bougie. ii. prepare equipment needed for endotracheal intubation as indicated per procedure guid3203-pr020 - endotracheal intubation. iii. begin intubation procedure as indicated in guid3203-pr020 - endotracheal intubation. iv. perform laryngoscopy and when anatomy is identified, pass the bougie into the trachea or anteriorly toward the presumed opening of the trachea. tracheal placement of the bougie is noted by a clicking feel/sound as the flexed tip passes over the tracheal rings. v. advance bougie until the device is well inside the tracheal lumen (there should be 'hold up' at 30-40 cm depth. if this does not occur the bougie is likely to be in the esophagus. a typical bougie is 60 cm so 1/3 to 1/2 of bougie should remain out of the patients mouth when "hold up" is achieved adults.) vi. thread the proximal end of the bougie through the distal end of a lubricated ett. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: gum elastic bougie guideline number: guid-3203-pr011 vii. advance the tracheal tube over the bougie until the bougie exits the proximal opening in the tracheal tube. if possible, continue laryngoscopy when advancing ett over bougie, to assist ready passage of the endotracheal tube. viii. if resistance is encountered at the vocal cords, withdraw the tracheal tube 1-2 cm, rotate it 90 degrees counterclockwise and then re-advance the tracheal tube. ix. when the tracheal tube is clearly in the trachea, remove the bougie and inflate the cuff. x. confirm proper ett placement by calculating 3 x the ett size. ventilate the patient with 100% oxygen. secure the ett. iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Inhalation Therapy
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: inhalation therapy guideline number: guid-3203-pr012 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to optimize oxygenation, prevent or reverse hypoxia in acute bronchospasm, and decrease airway swelling and or stridor in cases of reversible non-foreign body upper airway obstruction. ii. definitions: none iii. department guideline: a. equipment: i. small volume nebulizer ii. oxygen source iii. albuterol in 0.083% pre-mixed solution iv. ipratropium (atrovent) 0.5mg solution v. vaponephrine (racemic epinephrine) in 2.25% solution b. indications: i. bronchospasm ii. partial airway obstruction caused by croup, epiglottis or laryngospasm iii. hyperkalemia c. contraindications: i. use caution in patients with tachycardia-related cardiac dysrhythmias ii. use with caution in patients with known coronary disease iii. consider termination of treatment if heart rate increases more than 20% iv. do not use atrovent with patients who have a known history of a peanut allergy d. general assessment: i. use appropriate ppe for the patient's condition ii. assess the adequacy of the airway, breathing and circulation, intervene as appropriate iii. provide ventilatory support as needed iv. monitor and record vital signs including rr, spo2, hr, ecg and bp v. administer high flow, high concentration oxygen e. procedure: adult/pediatric general - nebulizer i. prepare the nebulizer with medication per condition below ii. connect the nebulizer to an oxygen source at 8 lpm page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: inhalation therapy guideline number: guid-3203-pr012 iii. instruct the patient to seal their lips around the mouthpiece of the nebulizer and inhale through their mouth deeply iv. if the patient is unable to seal their lips around the mouthpiece the treatment may be given as blow-by or connected to a mask f. condition: adult/pediatric bronchospasm i. prepare the nebulizer with 1. adult: albuterol 2.5 mg/3 ml 2. pediatric: > 12 years old: albuterol 2.5 mg/3 ml q 20 min may repeat x3. if needed, continuous nebulizer dose: 10-15 mg/h 3. pediatric: < 12 years old: albuterol 0.15-0.3 mg/kg q 20 min may repeat x3. if needed, continuous nebulizer dose: 0.5 mg/kg/h 4. ipratropium bromide 0.02% 0.5 mg (2.5 ml) to the initial albuterol nebulized x 1 dose. g. condition: partial airway obstruction due to croup, epiglottitis, or laryngospasm. i. after obtaining an order medication from the sending physician, prepare the nebulizer with racemic epinephrine 0.05 ml/kg of a 2.25% solution to a max single dose of 0.5 ml and may repeat every 20 minutes prn. a. condition: severe ards i. if patient is being administered aerosolized epoprosterol (flolan) and the appropriate equipment is available, continue at current dosing in consultation with sending/admitting physician or medical control b. condition: hyperkalemia i. prepare the nebulizer with 1. consider albuterol 2.5 mg/3 ml of ns via nebulizer, may repeat. iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: a. none page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Intraosseous (IO) Insertion & Infusions
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: intraosseous (io) insertion & infusions guideline number: guid-3203-pr013 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: intraosseous infusions (adult and pediatric) using ez-io revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to provide an alternative route for administration of intravenous fluids and medications in the adult and pediatric patient when standard peripheral intravenous access has been unsuccessful. ii. definitions: a. io - intraosseous iii. department guideline: a. equipment: i. iv tubing and fluid ii. proper needle size 1. 15 mm = pink = 3-39 kg 2. 25 mm = blue = >39 kg 3. 45 mm = yellow = >39 kg with excessive tissue over the site iii. betadine, chlorohexidine, or alcohol prep pads iv. 10 ml aspiration syringe with 5 ml of nacl v. lidocaine 2% preservative free injection solution vi. ez-io™ from vidacare is approved for patients 3 kg and above as follows: 1. proximal tibia: adults, children, and infants 2. distal tibia: adults, children, and infants 3. proximal humerus: adults, children, and infants 4. distal femur: children and infants b. indications: i. unsuccessful establishment of peripheral intravenous access or inability to perform venous cannulation as a result of anatomical abnormalities or inadequate positioning. c. contraindications: i. ability to obtain iv access by another route (peripheral or external jugular) ii. fracture or burns to the target bone iii. previous orthopedic procedures at or proximal to insertion site iv. infection at the insertion site page 1 of 3 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: intraosseous (io) insertion & infusions guideline number: guid-3203-pr013 v. inability to locate landmarks vi. excessive tissue over the insertion site vii. known congenital defect of the involved bone viii. known bone pathology (cancer or osteoporosis) d. procedure: i. always observe body substance isolation procedures and aseptic technique ii. assure that there are no contraindications present. iii. locate the desired insertion site: 1. for the proximal tibia: locate the patella and the tibial tuberosity. the needle will be inserted 1 cm width medial to the tibial tuberosity. (see figure 1.) 2. for the proximal humerus: orient the arm at the patient's side, firmly adducted, with the forearm flexed across the umbilicus. locate the greater tubercle of the humerus. the needle will be inserted laterally, perpendicular to the bone at 90 degrees iv. clean the insertion site with alcohol, chlorohexidine or betadine. (see figure 2.) v. prepare the ez-io driver and needle set. the needle is sterile. the top of the needle is magnetized and will attach to the end of the driver. once the needle is attached to the driver, remove the needle from its plastic container. vi. twist the sterile needle cap (cover) and gently remove it from the end of the needle. do not touch the end of the needle otherwise. vii. place the driver in your dominant hand. locate your insertion site with the opposite hand. viii. position the needle over the insertion site with the needle at a 90 degree angle to the surface of the bone. power the needle with firm pressure thru the skin until it reaches the surface of the bone (see figure 3). there is a 5 mm mark on the needle that should still be visible. if it is not, the procedure should be abandoned as the needle may not be long enough to penetrate the io space. ix. if appropriate, continue the insertion, powering the needle with firm and steady pressure until the flange touches the skin or a sudden lack of resistance is felt (indicating entrance into the bone marrow cavity) x. remove the driver xi. while grasping the hub with one hand, rotate the stylet counterclockwise (unscrew it) and remove it. (see figure 4) xii. attempt to aspirate blood. the needle should be well seated in tibial bone at 90 degrees. xiii. administer lidocaine 2% preservative free prior to the flush for conscious patients. 1. adult: lidocaine 40 mg io 2. pediatrics: lidocaine 0.5mg/kg io xiv. flush the needle with 10 ml of sterile saline. there should be a free flow of fluid with no leakage of fluid under the skin. xv. attach the catheter and cover with sterile dressing. (see figures 5 and 6.) xvi. apply yellow instructional wrist band to patient. page 2 of 3 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: intraosseous (io) insertion & infusions guideline number: guid-3203-pr013 xvii. the ez-io should not be left in place for more than 24 hours. it can be removed by pulling the hub while rotating gently in a clockwise fashion. iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: a. vidacare reference materials figure 1 figure 2 figure 3 figure 4 figure 5 figure 6 figure 7 figure 8 page 3 of 3 © orlando health 2017

pages.text: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: intraosseous (io) insertion & infusions guideline number: guid-3203-pr013 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: intraosseous infusions (adult and pediatric) using ez-io revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to provide an alternative route for administration of intravenous fluids and medications in the adult and pediatric patient when standard peripheral intravenous access has been unsuccessful. ii. definitions: a. io - intraosseous iii. department guideline: a. equipment: i. iv tubing and fluid ii. proper needle size 1. 15 mm = pink = 3-39 kg 2. 25 mm = blue = >39 kg 3. 45 mm = yellow = >39 kg with excessive tissue over the site iii. betadine, chlorohexidine, or alcohol prep pads iv. 10 ml aspiration syringe with 5 ml of nacl v. lidocaine 2% preservative free injection solution vi. ez-io™ from vidacare is approved for patients 3 kg and above as follows: 1. proximal tibia: adults, children, and infants 2. distal tibia: adults, children, and infants 3. proximal humerus: adults, children, and infants 4. distal femur: children and infants b. indications: i. unsuccessful establishment of peripheral intravenous access or inability to perform venous cannulation as a result of anatomical abnormalities or inadequate positioning. c. contraindications: i. ability to obtain iv access by another route (peripheral or external jugular) ii. fracture or burns to the target bone iii. previous orthopedic procedures at or proximal to insertion site iv. infection at the insertion site page 1 of 3 © orlando health 2017

1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: intraosseous (io) insertion & infusions guideline number: guid-3203-pr013 v. inability to locate landmarks vi. excessive tissue over the insertion site vii. known congenital defect of the involved bone viii. known bone pathology (cancer or osteoporosis) d. procedure: i. always observe body substance isolation procedures and aseptic technique ii. assure that there are no contraindications present. iii. locate the desired insertion site: 1. for the proximal tibia: locate the patella and the tibial tuberosity. the needle will be inserted 1 cm width medial to the tibial tuberosity. (see figure 1.) 2. for the proximal humerus: orient the arm at the patient's side, firmly adducted, with the forearm flexed across the umbilicus. locate the greater tubercle of the humerus. the needle will be inserted laterally, perpendicular to the bone at 90 degrees iv. clean the insertion site with alcohol, chlorohexidine or betadine. (see figure 2.) v. prepare the ez-io driver and needle set. the needle is sterile. the top of the needle is magnetized and will attach to the end of the driver. once the needle is attached to the driver, remove the needle from its plastic container. vi. twist the sterile needle cap (cover) and gently remove it from the end of the needle. do not touch the end of the needle otherwise. vii. place the driver in your dominant hand. locate your insertion site with the opposite hand. viii. position the needle over the insertion site with the needle at a 90 degree angle to the surface of the bone. power the needle with firm pressure thru the skin until it reaches the surface of the bone (see figure 3). there is a 5 mm mark on the needle that should still be visible. if it is not, the procedure should be abandoned as the needle may not be long enough to penetrate the io space. ix. if appropriate, continue the insertion, powering the needle with firm and steady pressure until the flange touches the skin or a sudden lack of resistance is felt (indicating entrance into the bone marrow cavity) x. remove the driver xi. while grasping the hub with one hand, rotate the stylet counterclockwise (unscrew it) and remove it. (see figure 4) xii. attempt to aspirate blood. the needle should be well seated in tibial bone at 90 degrees. xiii. administer lidocaine 2% preservative free prior to the flush for conscious patients. 1. adult: lidocaine 40 mg io 2. pediatrics: lidocaine 0.5mg/kg io xiv. flush the needle with 10 ml of sterile saline. there should be a free flow of fluid with no leakage of fluid under the skin. xv. attach the catheter and cover with sterile dressing. (see figures 5 and 6.) xvi. apply yellow instructional wrist band to patient. page 2 of 3 © orlando health 2017

1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: intraosseous (io) insertion & infusions guideline number: guid-3203-pr013 xvii. the ez-io should not be left in place for more than 24 hours. it can be removed by pulling the hub while rotating gently in a clockwise fashion. iv. documentation: a. ems charts v. references: a. vidacare reference materials figure 1 figure 2 figure 3 figure 4 figure 5 figure 6 figure 7 figure 8 page 3 of 3 © orlando health 2017

title: Intracranial Pressure Invasive Monitoring
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: intracranial pressure invasive monitoring guideline number: guid-3203-pr014 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: purpose: continual monitoring of the intra-cerebral pressure in the cranial vault. ii. definitions: icp - intracranial pressure iii. department guideline: a. assess patient: keep neck in neutral position. keep head of bed at 30 degrees. assess that dressing is clean, dry, and intact. b. assess device (always use personal protection equipment/gloves during the procedure). i. assess all stopcocks to make sure nothing is clamped. ii. assess filter at the top of the collection device. it must not get wet. do not lay device flat. iii. assess waveform - p1, p2, p3. assess for kinks, air bubbles, blood clots, or debris. iv. evaluate the invasive monitor system from referring hospital to ensure that ventricular catheter is functioning correctly and accurately. verify that a patent transducer system is in place. use preservative free normal saline only, do not add heparin to the system. c. zero the device. i. place and secure with tape the transducer level with the outer aspect of the ear canal and the lateral canthus of the eye on the same side as the ventriculostomy catheter is inserted on. connect the transducer to the invasive monitor using the invasive monitoring cable. ii. zero the ventriculostomy line. turn the stopcock closest to the insertion site off to the patient and open to air. observe the waveform on the monitor flatten. press the zero button. the monitor will calibrate and "zeroed" will appear on the monitor screen. iii. replace the cap and open the stopcock to begin transducing the icp. once the ventriculostomy has been "zeroed" observe the monitor for a normal waveform. d. monitor the patient i. do not flush pressure lines while connected to patient. ii. close the drain for all patient movements and altitude changes. iii. if the patient sustains an icp > 15 cm/h2o for more than 5 minutes consult the receiving physician. iv. if the device is inadvertently pulled out, place a sterile dressing over the insertion site. iv. documentation: ems charts page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: intracranial pressure invasive monitoring guideline number: guid-3203-pr014 v. references: none page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Intravenous (IV) Fluid Therapy
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: intravenous (iv) fluid therapy guideline number: guid-3203-pr015 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: iv fluid therapy revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to provide guidelines for fluid resuscitation and maintenance of fluid administration ii. definitions: iv - intravenous iii. department guideline: a. equipment: iv fluids: 0.9% normal saline (ns), lactated ringers, dextrose containing solutions b. indications: hemodynamic instability requiring fluid replacement or medications c. procedure: general i. all patients will have an iv line/saline lock in place unless contraindicated. ii. pt's with hypovolemia, potential for hypovolemia, or hemodynamic instability present should preferably have two large bore ivs. iii. if unable to establish a peripheral iv line, proceed to intraosseous (io) infusion. iv. iv fluid replacement is based on patient condition and appropriate standard of care. a. if hypotension present, treat per guid3203-m011 - hypotension or per guid3203-ped010 - pediatric hypotension. b. limit iv fluids in trauma patients. if active hemorrhage, treat with blood products per guid3203-pr025 - emergency blood product administration. c. for burn patients treat per guid3203-t002 - burns. v. for adults, utilize pressure bag for rapid infusion if indicated. d. procedure: pediatric i. if delivering an iv bolus, consider 'push-pull' method using a syringe attached to a fluid bag with a 3-way stopcock to draw iv fluid into syringe then deliver to patient using repetitive strokes of the syringe. ii. following acute resuscitation, consider a dextrose containing maintenance fluid infusion in addition to other volume replacement (e.g. septic patients, neonates, burn patients). 1. maintenance rate: a. using the 4-2-1 rule i. 4ml/kg/hr for the 1st 10kg of body weight *plus* ii. 2ml/kg/hr for the 2nd 10kg of body weight *plus* iii. 1ml/kg/hr for each additional kg of body weight page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: intravenous (iv) fluid therapy guideline number: guid-3203-pr015 b. alternate formula: i. 0-10 kg --- 100 ml/kg 24 hrs = hourly rate ii. 0-20 kg --- 1000 ml + 50 ml/kg for each kg > 10 kg  24 hrs = hourly rate iii. 20 kg or > --- 1500 ml + 20 ml/kg for each kg > 20 kg  24 hrs = hourly rate iv. documentation: ems charts v. references: none page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: KING LTS-D Laryngeal Tube
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: i-gel supraglottic airway device guideline number: guid-3203-pr016 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to maintain a patent airway with the i-gel supraglottic airway device when direct laryngoscopy and passage of an endotracheal tube in not possible. ii. definitions: none iii. department guideline: a. equipment: i-gel pediatri c adult tube size 1 1.5 2 2.5 3 4 5 color pink blue grey white yellow green orange weight < 5 kg 5-12 kg 10-25 kg 25-35 kg 30-50 kg 50-90 kg > 90 kg b. indications: i. the i-gel is intended for difficult and emergent airway management in the patient who is apneic without a gag reflex. ii. if device was previously placed by prehospital personnel and is providing adequate oxygenation and ventilation, do not selectively remove it. c. contraindications: i. responsive patients with an intact gag reflex ii. known esophageal disease iii. caustic substance ingestion iv. patients with laryngectomy / tracheostomy v. complete airway obstruction vi. foreign body aspiration vii. pharyngeal hemorrhage d. procedure: i. apply a water-based lubricant to the beveled distal tip and posterior aspect of the tube. avoid introduction of lubricant in or near the ventilatory openings. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: i-gel supraglottic airway device guideline number: guid-3203-pr016 ii. pre-oxygenate patient. iii. position the head (neutral of sniffing position) iv. hold i-gel with dominant hand. with non-dominant hand, gently press chin downward v. insert the igel into the mouth towards the hard palate. vi. advance gently downwards and backwards along the hard palate without exerting excessive force until definitive resistance is felt. vii. attach an end tidal co2 detector then begin ventilation with a resuscitator bag viii. verify by capnography, visualize rise of chest, auscultate breath sounds and confirm negative epigastric sounds ix. if unable to verify placement by capnography, chest rise or breath sounds, remove i-gel and attempt reinsertion. x. secure i-gel with a commercial securing device or tape. do not cover the proximal opening of the gastric access lumen. xi. once ventilation and oxygenation are confirmed place a gastric tube through the proximal opening of the gastric access chanel (the size 1 neonatal does not have a gastric channel) per guid3203-pr009 - gastric decompression. xii. continue to ventilate patient with a bag valve device throughout transport. iv. documentation: ems charts v. references: none page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Needle Thoracostomy
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: needle/simple thoracostomy guideline number: guid-3203-pr017 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 12/10/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to optimize oxygenation, prevent/reverse hypoxia in cases of tension pneumothorax, and to relieve intra-thoracic pressure being placed on the mediastinum causing hemodynamic compromise in cases of tension pneumothorax. ii. definitions: none iii. department guideline: a. equipment: i. cleaning solution: alcohol, betadine, or chlorohexidine. ii. needle 1. 10-14 gauge, ≥ 3-inch catheter over the needle device for adult patients. 2. 14-18-gauge 2-inch catheter over the needle device for pediatric patients. iii. simple 1. sterile gloves, 11-blade scalpel, curved forceps, tegaderm dressing b. indications: signs of tension pneumothorax include: increased hr, rr, work of breathing, pip and map in intubated patients with positive pressure ventilation. decreased spo2, lung sounds to the affected side(s), chest rise on affected side(s). jvd in euvolemic patient, subcutaneous emphysema, hypotension, tracheal deviation (late sign), hyper-resonance upon percussion, eventual cardiac arrest. c. contraindications: **simple thoracostomy may only be performed in the setting of traumatic cardiac arrest with suspicion of tension pneumothorax, in all other scenarios needle thoracostomy should be performed** d. procedure needle thoracostomy: i. assess airway/breathing/circulation, & clinical signs/symptoms of tension pneumothorax. ii. administer high concentration oxygen. iii. monitor the patient's vital signs hr, ecg, rr, spo2, bp, and etco2 in intubated patients. iv. identify the site: locate the 2nd intercostal space in the mid-clavicular line or the 4th or 5th intercostal space in the mid-anterior-axillary line of the affected side. v. prep this site with cleaning solution. vi. insert the decompression needle into the site and burry the needle to the hub. page 1 of 2 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: needle/simple thoracostomy guideline number: guid-3203-pr017 vii. remove the needle leaving the catheter in place and listen for release of air. viii. assess for desired effect and complications. assess lung sounds. continue to monitor vital signs. ix. continually reassess the patient for signs and symptoms of a redeveloping tension pneumo- thorax. if signs and symptoms re-develop, additional needle thoracostomies may be indicated. e. procedure simple thoracostomy: i. utilize only the setting of adult (>16 yo) traumatic cardiac arrest with suspicion for tension pneumothorax ii. identify the site: locate the 4th or 5th intercostal space at the midaxillary line (usually above the nipple line, lift breast tissue out of the way if necessary). when in doubt, use a more cephalad (toward the head) approach to avoid inadvertently entering the peritoneal space. iii. prep this site with cleaning solution. iv. using scalpel, make a 1-2 inch incision over the rib. the scalpel is used for skin incision only v. use the curved forceps for blunt dissection, and then insert them through the intercoastal muscles into the pleural space. vi. open the forceps to allow expulsion of air and blood, then remove them. a finger may be gently inserted to confirm entrance into the pleural space vii. use occlusive dressing to cover wound viii. this procedure may be performed bilaterally if rosc is not achieved after initial procedure iv. documentation: ems charts v. references: none page 2 of 2 © orlando health 2017

title: Non-Invasive Positive Pressure Ventilation
normalizedText: 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: non-invasive positive pressure ventilation guideline number: guid-3203-pr018 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 4/30/24 developed by: air care team approved by: dr. christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: to provide adequate support and oxygenation to patients not yet requiring intubation and invasive mechanical ventilation. ii. definitions: a. bipap - bilevel positive airway pressure d. cpap - continuous positive airway pressure b. epap - expiratory positive airway pressure e. ipap - inspiratory positive airway pressure c. nppv - non-invasive positive pressure ventilation f. peep - positive end-expiratory pressure iii. department guideline: a. types of nppv i. cpap - continuous positive airway pressure mode - provides continuous peep 1. indications: congestive heart failure (chf), pulmonary edema, obstructive sleep apnea ii. bipap - bi-level positive airway pressure mode - applies a continuous peep throughout the breath cycle, but also pressurizes to a set pressure during inhalation 1. indications: asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (copd), resp failure from exhaustion, chf, or pulmonary edema b. equipment: i. nppv mask from sending facility ii. oxygen source iii. revel transport ventilator iv. disposable ventilator tubing c. indications: i. any patient requiring ventilatory or oxygenation support who does not yet require intubation or refuses intubation on an interfacility transport. ii. hypoxemia secondary to pulmonary edema d. contraindications: penetrating chest trauma, persistent nausea/vomiting, obtundation, respiratory arrest, or inability to protect patient's airway e. procedure: i. assess/manage airway, breathing and circulation. assess ecg, spo2 and blood pressure. ii. explain the procedure to the patient page 1 of 3 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: non-invasive positive pressure ventilation guideline number: guid-3203-pr018 iii. fit and size mask to the patient. prepare the headpiece and circuit according to manufacturer's specifications. ensure mask vents are closed. iv. attach gas outlet, pressure transducer, & exhalation valve tubes to corresponding connectors. v. turn the ventilator on allow self-check to perform. vi. select appropriate patient size, scroll to nppv, and hit select. vii. initiate settings appropriate for condition or begin with referring hospital settings. 1. bipap (target initial is ipap/epap of 10/5 cm/h2o). pressure support is the difference between ipap and epap. 2. cpap (target initial is ipap/epap 0/ 5 cm/h2o) 3. if transferring from an open to closed system, drop ipap by 4 5 cm/h2o (but not less than 10 5 cm/h2o total ipap), dial up as necessary. viii. use appropriate peep setting, ensuring correlation between tubing and desired peep level. ix. attach ventilator to 50psi oxygen source utilizing high pressure tubing. ensure that the portable oxygen tank has a minimum of 1000 psi of pressure or connect oxygen tubing to oxygen inlet 15 lpm flow and dial the fio2 to read the low-pressure source. x. set breath rate to 0 or consider a rate to augment the breath support. xi. set fio2. titrate to use lowest concentration. xii. turn off low pressure and minute ventilation alarms, set appropriate for patient. xiii. attach ventilator circuit to the mask and administer treatment. xiv. troubleshooting. if patient does not tolerate ventilator settings… 1. enter the ventilator's extended features by pushing and holding the monitor "select" key for three (3) seconds and scroll to 'vent control' menu a. adjust rise profile to 1-2. a faster rise time will deliver o2 quickly. b. adjust flow termination to 40% to prevent prolonged inhalation. c. adjust time termination to 0.7 2. if spo2 remains low, increase the epap or fio2. 3. if etco2 low, reduce the pressure support, thus lowering ipap 4. if etco2 high, increase the pressure support (ps), thus elevating ipap 5. exit the extended features menus by turning the set value knob until exit is displayed and push select until monitored data is displayed in the window. xv. if patient is agitated, coach patient to breathe calmly and consider light sedation xvi. consider a 30 second trial of the patient lying flat prior to heading aircraft to assess if patient will tolerate lying flat while being loaded into the aircraft. xvii. continue treatment during the entire transport. xviii. if patient is receiving a nebulized bronchodilator, it may be continued by placing the nebulizer device in-line with the nppv circuit. xix. reassess vital signs every five minutes while the patient is on nppv and document. xx. if respiratory status deteriorates, consider intubation or other airway management. f. complications: hypotension or pneumothorax page 2 of 3 © orlando health 2017 1414 kuhl ave. orlando, fl 32806 department guideline guideline title: non-invasive positive pressure ventilation guideline number: guid-3203-pr018 iv. documentation: in ems charts, document mode of ventilation & all ventilator settings (the rise profile, flow term, and time term). document measurements of ventilation (vte, ve, and frequency). v. references: page 3 of 3 © orlando health 2017

title: Intravenous Insertion – Peripheral
normalizedText: intravenous insertion - peripheral guideline title: intravenous insertion - peripheral guideline number: guid-3203- pr019 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 6/8/2021 developed by: air care team approved by: christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 cccvi. purpose: to provide vascular access via peripheral vein(s) for the purpose of providing support to the circulatory system and to maintain adequate circulation through the administration of iv fluids, blood products, and medications. cccvii. definitions: a. iv - intravenous cccviii. department guideline: a. equipment: i. iv flush solution: 0.9% normal saline ii. over the needle catheters (14g - 24g) iii. site prep and securing equipment: saline lock, gauze pads, alcohol pads, occlusive dressing, and tape. b. indications: the need or potential need to administer iv fluids, blood products, or medications. c. contraindications: i. absolute: none. ii. relative: vessels distal to large lacerations, or fractures should be avoided, vessels in extremities with impaired sensation, shunt or fistula in involved extremity, mastectomy on involved side, or fracture on involved extremity. d. procedure: i. explain the procedure to the patient and assemble all needed equipment. ii. "flush" the saline lock. iii. choose appropriate size iv catheter. remember to consider the intended therapy (i.e. trauma patients should have large bore iv access) e. peripheral extremity and scalp vein iv access: i. apply a venous constricting band proximal to the desired puncture site. ii. clean the desired puncture site and surrounding area with alcohol. iii. perform vein puncture with the bevel of the needle up and observe blood return. iv. if blood return is noted advance the catheter and remove the needle if applicable v. dispose of the needle in an appropriate sharps container vi. if indicated, draw blood samples. vii. remove the venous constricting band if used viii. flush the iv line with solution. ix. assess the site for signs of infiltration or dislodgement x. secure the catheter with an occlusive dressing and tape. xi. secure the administration set and iv tubing as needed with tape or with coban. f. external jugular vein cannulation i. do not perform this procedure on both external jugular veins ii. find the landmarks - midway between the angle of the jaw and the mid-point of the clavicle iii. cleanse the desired puncture site and surrounding area with alcohol iv. if appropriate turn patient's head away from the intended site of insertion v. if appropriate place patient into a trendelenburg position or hold your thumb over the vein to facilitate venous filling and insertion vi. insert the catheter pointing towards the ipsilateral acromioclavicular joint until a flash fills the chamber of the catheter, then advance catheter over the needle, and remove needle. vii. dispose of the needle in the appropriate sharps container. viii. connect the administration set tubing to the cannula ix. assess the site for signs of infiltration or dislodgement x. secure the catheter with an occlusive dressing and tape xi. secure the administration set and iv tubing as needed with tape cccix. documentation: ems charts cccx. references: none

title: Endotracheal Intubation
normalizedText: endotracheal intubation guideline title: endotracheal intubation guideline number: guid-3203- pr020 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 12/2018, 6/8/2021 developed by: air care team approved by: christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 cccxi. purpose: to secure a patient airway due to actual or potential airway compromise due to depressed sensorium, who require ventilatory assistance, or whose combativeness threatens airway compromise. cccxii. definitions: a. ett - endotracheal tube b. nrb - nonrebreather mask c. bvm - bag valve mask d. etco2 - end-tidal carbon dioxide cccxiii. department guideline: a. equipment: i. appropriate personal protective equipment following current oh infection prevention guidelines. ii. oxygen iii. 2 endotracheal tubes of appropriate size (cuffs checked; stylets available). iv. video laryngoscopy equipment (e.g., c-mac) v. standard laryngoscope handle and appropriate size blade (lights checked) as backup. vi. bvm unit with reservoir and appropriately sized mask and peep valve vii. suction equipment with yankaur tip attached. viii. 10ml syringe ix. cardiac monitor for monitoring of capnography (etco2), ekg, and vital signs. x. tape or commercial device to secure tube xi. 100% nrb mask and/or nasal cannula capable of delivering oxygen at 10-25 l/min xii. broselow tape for pediatric patients xiii. rescue equipment: supraglottic airway devices (king lt-d) xiv. gum elastic bougie xv. high efficiency particulate air (hepa) filter for suspected infectious patients. xvi. magill forceps xvii. medications see guid-g008 - rapid sequence intubation (rsi) for endotracheal (et) intubation xviii. surgical airway equipment see guid-pr007 - cricothyrotomy - surgical xix. stethoscope b. indications: i. patient unable to protect his/her own airway. 1. loss of gag/cough reflex or inability to handle secretions ii. patient is incapable of sustaining adequate intrinsic ventilatory effort. 1. apnea or signs of hypoxia 2. glasgow coma scale <8 3. airway obstruction due to edema, burns, epiglottis iii. patient will need airway management to facilitate safe transport 1. anticipated loss of control of airway due to edema, trauma, or stridor iv. patient will need prolonged ventilatory support 1. need for controlled mechanical ventilation due to ventilator or oxygenation failure from sedation, narcosis, stroke, brain injury, spinal cord injury, nerve injury, motor neuron disease, muscular problems, chest wall injuries, pleural effusions, pneumothorax, hemothorax, bronchospasm, inhaled foreign object, acute lung injury, pulmonary edema, or anaphylaxis, hemodynamic instability, multisystem failure, metabolic issues. v. persistent hypoxemia despite all efforts to improve oxygenation. c. contraindications: i. patients who are maintaining a patent airway ii. anticipated difficulty ventilating patients after paralysis d. procedure: i. perform an assessment identifying the need for airway intervention. ii. obtain a baseline neurological assessment. iii. apply personal protective equipment iv. prepare the patient 1. apply cardiac monitor, pulse ox, and noninvasive bp and obtain initial readings. a. if patient is hypotensive, consider volume resuscitation with iv fluids or blood products per guid-m011 - hypotension and/or guid-pr025 - emergency blood product administration prior to rsi attempt. 2. if patient is conscious, get a history and explain procedure, and reassure patient. 3. gather equipment above. ensure all equipment is set up. check and have back up devices ready including supraglottic devices and emergency cricothyrotomy kit. 4. establish iv access if patient requires medications to facilitate intubation. 5. assess anterior neck for possible surgical airway. 6. select appropriate tube size using broselow tape for pediatric patients. v. pre-oxygenate patient with high flow o2 by nrb mask. if available, simultaneously use a standard nasal cannula and leave in place during procedure for passive oxygenation. 1. do not manually ventilate the patient using a bvm unless respiratory effort is ineffective, as this may cause gastric distention with vomiting and aspiration. 2. if unable to maintain oxygen saturation >93%, suction the mouth and oropharynx to decrease risk of view obstruction and ventilator-associated pneumonia. consider 2-person thumbs up bagging technique with peep valve. squeeze bvm in synchrony with patient's intrinsic respiratory effort. vi. consider elevating the head of bed 30 degrees. position the head in sniffing position if possible. if trauma is suspected, consider removing the cervical collar (if present) while providing in-line manual immobilization of the head and neck. vii. video laryngoscopy should be utilized for initial attempt unless the equipment is unavailable/malfunctioning or a documented clinical reason (i.e. copious blood in the airway) for direct laryngoscopy is given. viii. ensure proper functioning of all equipment including suction, oxygen, medications, and airways (appropriate sizes, cuff integrity, bougie setup). ix. once intubating conditions are optimized, administer medications if needed per guid-3203-g008 rapid sequence induction for endotracheal intubation x. introduce the laryngoscope into the mouth. 1. if using video device, insert blade into mouth and walk down airway. when uvula identified lift to expose epiglottis and slowly advance to expose airway. 2. if using standard laryngoscope, sweep tongue to the left and advance blade to the base of the epiglottis (vallecula). lift laryngoscope upwards and outwards to expose the glottis. 3. if using a straight blade, directly elevate the epiglottis. xi. visualize the vocal cords. consider utilizing external laryngeal manipulation by having your teammate place their hand on the tracheal cartilage and gently shifting it until the trachea is in better view xii. consider gentle cricoid pressure is suspicious for a high risk of aspiration xiii. if you encounter a partial view utilize a gum elastic bougie per guid-pr011 - gum elastic bougie. xiv. advance the tip of the endotracheal tube through the vocal cords. once the cuff has passed through, stop advancing (approximately 2-3cm). xv. secure the tube and inflate the cuff until there is no air leak. xvi. confirm placement by placing an etco2 detector on et tube. begin ventilations with bvm at 100% oxygen. auscultate for air movement in stomach and bilateral breath sounds. observe chest rise and condensation in the ett. 1. if tube placement is unable to be confirmed or there exists any doubt as to correct tube placement, immediately remove tube and oxygenate patient with a bvm to maintain spo2 >93%. 2. if right main stem intubation is suspected (decreased or absent left sided breath wounds, asymmetrical chest wall movement with ventilation, decreasing spo2 despite good capnographic waveform), deflate the cuff, slightly pull back on the tube (1-2cm) and evaluate tube placement again, then re-inflate the cuff. xvii. secure the tube with tape or commercial device. xviii. note the depth in cm at the teeth or gums. xix. if unable to visualize vocal cords or pass et tube after 20 seconds, stop the attempt, ventilate patient with 100% oxygen via bvm for 30-60 seconds, and re-attempt intubation. xx. if intubation fails after 2 attempts and you are unable to adequately ventilate the patient with a bvm, consider placing a king tube per guid-pr016 - king lts-d or other supraglottic device if available. xxi. if the king or other supraglottic device is unsuccessful and the patient is still unable to be ventilated with a bvm, consider cricothyrotomy per guid-pr007 - cricothyrotomy - surgical. xxii. after intubation: 1. monitor vital signs, etco2, and recheck placement frequently per guid-pr009 - end-tidal co2 monitoring 2. consider placing a gastric tube for gastric decompression per guid-pr010 - gastric decompression. 3. administer post-intubation sedation per guid-g008 - rapid sequence intubation (rsi) for endotracheal (et) intubation, guid-g005 - analgesia and sedation management, or guid-g007 - propofol diprivan (if ordered by sending facility). 4. move patients to the transport ventilator per guid-pr024 - ventilation with a mechanical ventilator. 5. verify tracheal tube position with any movement of the patient and upon turnover of care at receiving facility. e. complications: vomiting, cardiac dysrhythmias (bradycardia, pvc's, and v-fib), failed intubation, aspiration of stomach contents, pneumothorax, cervical vertebral injury, tear or trauma to bronchus, esophagus, mandible, teeth, laryngospasm, increased intracranial pressure. cccxiv. documentation: a. ems charts: etco2 must be numerically documented with vital signs and a printed strip must be attached to the chart cccxv. references: none

title: Restraints
normalizedText: restraints guideline title: restraints guideline number: guid-3203- pr021 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 6/8/2021 developed by: air care team approved by: christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 cccxvi. purpose: to ensure the safety of the transport team and enhance the rendering of appropriate care when there is the potential for patient combativeness. since a combative patient in flight can pose a serious safety threat to themselves and to the transport team, it is important to perform a thorough assessment prior to utilization of restraints. this end goal may be met utilizing either chemical or mechanical methods, as well as both concurrently. it is also realized that this document is a guideline for incremental restraint, which the provider's clinical judgment may alter to meet the goals of restraint quickly, for the safety of the patient and provider. cccxvii. definitions: cccxviii. department guideline: a. equipment: i. soft restraints for wrists and ankles ii. sedative medications iii. monitoring devices: end-tidal carbon dioxide (etco2), ecg, pulse oximetry, and bp b. indications: i. combative behavior in which the patient poses a safety threat to themselves or to the medical transport team. predictors of violent or combative behavior are: history of violence, behavior indicating potential violence through posture, speech, or motor activity, alcohol or drug intoxication, alteration of consciousness, psychiatric disorder with acute process or behaviors occurring, exposure to chemicals or smoke, or altered level of consciousness associated with altering environmental factors, or central nervous system trauma with altered level of consciousness with actual combative behavior. ii. non-violent restraints to limit patient mobility, avoid treatment interruptions, or to enable active staff interventions safely. c. contraindications: ability to ensure the patient's safety with less restrictive measures d. procedure: i. assess patient for a physiologic etiology for uncooperative behavior such as hypoglycemia, hypoxia, and hypoperfusion. manage as appropriate. ii. attempt de-escalation techniques and use therapeutic communication. iii. complete a neurologic examination and measure vital signs. vital signs and neurologic status must be continually monitored while a patient remains restrained in any fashion. iv. soft wrist and ankle restraints can be utilized as a non-violent restraint. quick release methods are to be used to secure according to manufactures instructions. v. restraint devices from sending facility may be continued such as anchored mitts. vi. consideration of sedation vs. physical restraint with the following options: 1. chemical sedation per guid-g006 - anxiety/combative patient management 2. physical restraint: a. soft restraints when used should be applied to the wrist and ankles at a comfortable angle b. restraints should be anchored to either the stretcher or the backboard to allow the patient to be placed on their side in the event of emesis. c. when applying soft restraints, allow for enough room underneath the device so that one finger maybe inserted between the restraint and the extremity. d. circulation, sensory and motor function of all restrained patients should be assessed every 10 minutes to coincide with assessment and documentation of vital signs. vii. the patient in custody shall be placed in 4-point restraints or handcuffed (feet and hands) by law enforcement agents. this is considered a forensic restraint. the team shall be responsible for assessing distal circulation. the police/correction officer should accompany the patient during the transport, with approval of the pilot in command. viii. there are times when ground transportation is most appropriate for patients. these situations may include situations such as combative patients. the general principle is that the team should go with the patient even if this requires going by ground. if the medical transport team determines that transporting the patient is considered unsafe due to combativeness, the team may refuse to transport the patient by air and accompany the patient via ground ambulance. ix. if sedation and physical restraints have been ineffective or in the provider's sound medical judgment the clinical situation requires chemical restraint with paralyzation, this may be utilized per guid- g008 - rapid sequence induction for endotracheal intubation and guid-pr020 endotracheal intubation. x. carefully document the medical decision-making that led the medical transport team to choose such a method of restraint. cccxix. documentation: ems charts cccxx. references: a. orlando health policy #2580 restraint use in the acute care setting

pages.text: restraints guideline title: restraints guideline number: guid-3203- pr021 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 6/8/2021 developed by: air care team approved by: christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 cccxvi. purpose: to ensure the safety of the transport team and enhance the rendering of appropriate care when there is the potential for patient combativeness. since a combative patient in flight can pose a serious safety threat to themselves and to the transport team, it is important to perform a thorough assessment prior to utilization of restraints. this end goal may be met utilizing either chemical or mechanical methods, as well as both concurrently. it is also realized that this document is a guideline for incremental restraint, which the provider's clinical judgment may alter to meet the goals of restraint quickly, for the safety of the patient and provider. cccxvii. definitions: cccxviii. department guideline: a. equipment: i. soft restraints for wrists and ankles ii. sedative medications iii. monitoring devices: end-tidal carbon dioxide (etco2), ecg, pulse oximetry, and bp b. indications: i. combative behavior in which the patient poses a safety threat to themselves or to the medical transport team. predictors of violent or combative behavior are: history of violence, behavior indicating potential violence through posture, speech, or motor activity, alcohol or drug intoxication, alteration of consciousness, psychiatric disorder with acute process or behaviors occurring, exposure to chemicals or smoke, or altered level of consciousness associated with altering environmental factors, or central nervous system trauma with altered level of consciousness with actual combative behavior. ii. non-violent restraints to limit patient mobility, avoid treatment interruptions, or to enable active staff interventions safely.

c. contraindications: ability to ensure the patient's safety with less restrictive measures d. procedure: i. assess patient for a physiologic etiology for uncooperative behavior such as hypoglycemia, hypoxia, and hypoperfusion. manage as appropriate. ii. attempt de-escalation techniques and use therapeutic communication. iii. complete a neurologic examination and measure vital signs. vital signs and neurologic status must be continually monitored while a patient remains restrained in any fashion. iv. soft wrist and ankle restraints can be utilized as a non-violent restraint. quick release methods are to be used to secure according to manufactures instructions. v. restraint devices from sending facility may be continued such as anchored mitts. vi. consideration of sedation vs. physical restraint with the following options: 1. chemical sedation per guid-g006 - anxiety/combative patient management 2. physical restraint: a. soft restraints when used should be applied to the wrist and ankles at a comfortable angle b. restraints should be anchored to either the stretcher or the backboard to allow the patient to be placed on their side in the event of emesis. c. when applying soft restraints, allow for enough room underneath the device so that one finger maybe inserted between the restraint and the extremity. d. circulation, sensory and motor function of all restrained patients should be assessed every 10 minutes to coincide with assessment and documentation of vital signs. vii. the patient in custody shall be placed in 4-point restraints or handcuffed (feet and hands) by law enforcement agents. this is considered a forensic restraint. the team shall be responsible for assessing distal circulation. the police/correction officer should accompany the patient during the transport, with approval of the pilot in command. viii. there are times when ground transportation is most appropriate for patients. these situations may include situations such as combative patients. the general principle is that the team should go with the patient even if this requires going by ground. if the medical transport team determines that transporting the patient is considered unsafe due to combativeness, the team may refuse to transport the patient by air and accompany the patient via ground ambulance.

ix. if sedation and physical restraints have been ineffective or in the provider's sound medical judgment the clinical situation requires chemical restraint with paralyzation, this may be utilized per guid- g008 - rapid sequence induction for endotracheal intubation and guid-pr020 endotracheal intubation. x. carefully document the medical decision-making that led the medical transport team to choose such a method of restraint. cccxix. documentation: ems charts cccxx. references: a. orlando health policy #2580 restraint use in the acute care setting

title: Transcutaneous External Pacing
normalizedText: transcutaneous external pacing guideline title: transcutaneous external pacing guideline number: guid-3203- pr022 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 6/8/2021 developed by: air care team approved by: christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 cccxxi. purpose: to improve perfusion and oxygenation due to symptomatic bradycardia cccxxii. definitions: a. tcp - transcutaneous pacing b. sbp - systolic blood pressure cccxxiii. department guideline: a. equipment: i. cardiac monitor with pacing capabilities ii. external conductive hands-free pad electrodes and cable b. indications: i. symptomatic bradycardia unresponsive to medication administration. 1. pulse<50 with hypotension (sbp < 90) 2. symptoms: altered mental status, chest pain, and/or dyspnea ii. second or third-degree heart block c. contraindications: there are no contraindications for the truly symptomatic patient d. procedure: i. assess the need for tcp. confirm brady-dysrhythmia. ii. consider sedation or analgesia per guid-3203-g005 analgesia and sedation management. iii. turn external pacemaker on. iv. attach standard 4 lead ecg cable. v. place pacing pads on patient's bare chest: 1. anterior / posterior (preferred): place negative electrode on left anterior chest, halfway between the xiphoid process and the left nipple. this corresponds with the v2-v3 ecg position. place the positive electrode on left posterior chest beneath the scapula and lateral to the spine. 2. anterior / anterior: place the negative electrode on the left chest, mid-axilla over the fourth intercostal space. place positive electrode on anterior right chest, sub-clavicular area. vi. select lead i, ii, iii to provide the largest amplitude qrs complex. vii. push the "pacer" or locate pacer function. viii. select demand mode. ix. select desired 'rate' (typically 10-20 beats above the patient's intrinsic rate, or 60 if no rate exists) x. observe for a pacing stimulus marker for each "r" wave. if pacing marker does not exist with each "r" wave, increase the ecg size gain until the sensing marker appears or change the ecg lead. xi. when sensing is confirmed, increase ma output by 10 ma increments, until electrical capture is determined (evidenced by a wide qrs and tall, broad t wave). xii. determine optimum threshold. maintain stimulation at or 10% above lowest ma value that maintains capture. xiii. mechanical or ventricular capture is evidenced by signs of improving cardiac output. palpate for a femoral, right brachial, or radial artery pulse. xiv. assess every 5 minutes: blood pressure, pulse, respirations, spo2, level of consciousness, and ecg rhythm. xv. the non-invasive pacing may produce skeletal muscle contractions. secure all tubing, cables, and other equipment to prevent their displacement cccxxiv. documentation: ems charts cccxxv. references: a. american heart association, 2015. b. zoll - x series for ems www.zoll.com
