position. place the positive electrode on left posterior chest beneath the scapula and lateral to the spine. 2. anterior / anterior: place the negative electrode on the left chest, mid-axilla over the fourth intercostal space. place positive electrode on anterior right chest, sub-clavicular area. vi. select lead i, ii, iii to provide the largest amplitude qrs complex. vii. push the "pacer" or locate pacer function. viii. select demand mode. ix. select desired 'rate' (typically 10-20 beats above the patient's intrinsic rate, or 60 if no rate exists) x. observe for a pacing stimulus marker for each "r" wave. if pacing marker does not exist with each "r" wave, increase the ecg size gain until the sensing marker appears or change the ecg lead. xi. when sensing is confirmed, increase ma output by 10 ma increments, until electrical capture is determined (evidenced by a wide qrs and tall, broad t wave). xii. determine optimum threshold. maintain stimulation at or 10% above lowest ma value that maintains capture. xiii. mechanical or ventricular capture is evidenced by signs of improving cardiac output. palpate for a femoral, right brachial, or radial artery pulse. xiv. assess every 5 minutes: blood pressure, pulse, respirations, spo2, level of consciousness, and ecg rhythm. xv. the non-invasive pacing may produce skeletal muscle contractions. secure all tubing, cables, and other equipment to prevent their displacement cccxxiv. documentation: ems charts cccxxv. references: a. american heart association, 2015. b. zoll - x series for ems www.zoll.com

title: Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump
normalizedText: intra-aortic balloon pump/impella device guideline title: intra-aortic balloon pump/impella device guideline number: guid- 3203-pr023 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 6/8/2021 developed by: air care team approved by: christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 cccxxvi. purpose: intra-aortic balloon pump (iabp) therapy is designed to increase coronary artery perfusion, increases systemic perfusion, decrease myocardial workload, and decease afterload. it is an acute, short-term therapy for patients with reversible left ventricular failure. cccxxvii. definitions: a. iabp - intra-aortic balloon pump b. cxr - chest x-ray cccxxviii. department guideline: a. equipment: i. transport intra-aortic balloon pump 1. our pump currently is the cardiosave iabp rescue produced by maquet, which is the smallest and lightest model. weight: 24.1kg/53lb, size in cart: 30.7inches high x 16 inches deep x 13 inches wide. ii. spare battery and electrical cord iii. 250 ml or > of ns iv. pressure bag v. invasive monitoring tubing vi. 3 way stop cock vii. 60ml catheter tip syringe b. indications: i. decompensated systolic heart failure ii. acute myocardial infarction where cardiogenic shock cannot be reversed iii. acute mitral regurgitation c. contraindications: i. moderate to severe aortic insufficiency ii. thoracic and abdominal aortic aneurysms d. procedure: i. establish care defined by guid-g002 - general patient care. ii. obtain the following information if available from the staff or from the patient's record: iabp insertion site, method of visualization of correct placement and full inflation of the balloon via fluoroscopy or viewing of the cxr, catheter type and brand, size, balloon volume, technique of insertion (sheath or sheath-less), size and location of sheath, and indication for placement. 1. if the patient is connected to an iabp from a non-orlando health facility, ensure that all connectors are present. contact sending facility cath lab for adapters. iii. complete an assessment noting specifically 1. the patient: a. loc: thrombi may develop and dislodge b. left arm: skin color, temperature. cap refill, quality of radial pulse and compare to right side. place pulse ox on left hand if possible. (if balloon migrates upwards, occluding he left subclavian, you will have decreased perfusion to the arm) c. abdomen: swelling, pain, nausea, decreased/absent bowel sounds. (may indicate a balloon migration and an occlusion of the renal, celiac, or mesenteric arteries) d. leg or arm with insertion site: skin color, temperature, cap refill, compare skin and pulses to opposite extremity. immobilize the leg with a knee immobilizer or soft restraint if needed to prevent patient from bending of the knee and kinking of the iabp catheter e. insertion site: location, condition, dressing- intact, presence of bleeding, or hematoma. mark the position of the catheter at the insertion site before moving the patient f. urine output: foley draining should be >0.5ml/kg/hr. 2. review labs: complete blood count (cbc), platelet, and coagulation tests. 3. review pulmonary artery pressures (pa), cardiac output (co) or index measurements (ci) if available. 4. the waveform a. place the pump in 1:2 mode if patient can tolerate. print a strip of the waveform. (pt should augment at equal to or greater than the unassisted systole.) b. balloon augmentation should occur after the aortic valve closes. a sharp v wave should be noted, and the diacritic notch disappears. c. balloon assisted aortic end diastolic pressure should be lower than patient's unassisted diastolic pressure. d. the assisted systolic pressure will be less than the unassisted systolic pressure. e. balloon pressure waveform should have the following: filling pressure above zero, sharp up stroke, pressure plateau, rapid deflation 5. review all iv fluid and medication infusions and transfer medications to transport pumps iv. set up the transport iabp 1. check arterial line and other transducers for compatibility and change if needed. 2. check for fiber-optic sensor connector. 3. plug iabp model to ac power. turn on pump and allow system to perform a self-check. check helium level and battery levels. 4. place ecg skin leads on patient and monitor ecg on iabp screen. 5. pressure transducer: open transducer vent to atmosphere. press zero pressure key. close pressure transducer vent to atmosphere and assess pressure wave on display screen. 6. transfer the balloon lumen tubing to the iabp console. 7. check settings on iabp to match previous settings. timing should be "auto". place pump initially in 1:2 mode and print waveform strip to ensure accurate timing and augmentation. 8. start pumping by removing it from standby mode. v. prepare patient for transport and move patient over to stretcher. reassess patient and insertion site. keep patient in supine position with hob elevated no more than 30 degrees. vi. load patient and iabp. secure pump with two approved straps. use caution when loading. if iabp disconnects, immediately reattach the patient's balloon to the chamber. vii. reassess patient and monitor iabp tubing for connectivity, blood, possible dislodgment. complete a cardiovascular and peripheral reassessment every 15 minutes. monitor for signs of inappropriate iabp placement or balloon perforation. cccxxix. trouble shooting a. if cardiac rhythm changes to asystole, change trigger to atrial pressure. decrease augmentation to 50%. b. when cardioverting/defibrillating, stand clear from patient and equipment. c. reasons pump is not augmenting i. tachycardia or bradycardia: affects cardiac output ii. hypovolemia: map<50 at the time of insertion is not enough to augment iii. svr>1200: post coronary artery bypass grafting (cabg) or on high dose vasopressors, pump is unable to compete with increased afterload. iv. balloon volume is too low. d. timing errors i. early inflation: iabp prior to aortic valve closure (dicrotic notch) 1. will increase left ventricular wall stress, afterload, and mvo2 ii. late inflation: inflation markedly after closure of aortic valve 1. produces suboptimal coronary perfusion iii. early deflation: sharp drop following diastolic augmentation 1. causes increased assisted systolic pressure, suboptimal coronary flow, and suboptimal afterload reduction iv. late deflation: afterload reduction is absent 1. left ventricle is ejecting against a greater resistance e. loss of vacuum of iabp failure i. leak in the gas: check and tighten connections on the pneumatic tubing ii. check the power source iii. hand inflate and deflate the balloon every 5 minutes with half the total balloon volume f. suspected balloon perforation i. observe for a loss of >10mm in diastolic augmentation ii. check for blood in the catheter iii. assess for changes in the balloon pressure waveform iv. put iabp in standby v. clamp the iabp catheter to prevent arterial blood back up vi. disconnect iabp catheter form the iabp console vii. call control to notify the physician - the catheter should not lie dormant for longer than 30 minutes g. gas loss: pressure waveform falls below baseline h. kink: rounded balloon waveform i. poor cardiac function or low vascular resistance: refer to medical management per guid-3203-m011 hypotension. cccxxx. documentation: a. ems charts cccxxxi. references: a. www.getinge.com (previously maquet)

(if balloon migrates upwards, occluding he left subclavian, you will have decreased perfusion to the arm) c. abdomen: swelling, pain, nausea, decreased/absent bowel sounds. (may indicate a balloon migration and an occlusion of the renal, celiac, or mesenteric arteries) d. leg or arm with insertion site: skin color, temperature, cap refill, compare skin and pulses to opposite extremity. immobilize the leg with a knee immobilizer or soft restraint if needed to prevent patient from bending of the knee and kinking of the iabp catheter e. insertion site: location, condition, dressing- intact, presence of bleeding, or hematoma. mark the position of the catheter at the insertion site before moving the patient f. urine output: foley draining should be >0.5ml/kg/hr. 2. review labs: complete blood count (cbc), platelet, and coagulation tests. 3. review pulmonary artery pressures (pa), cardiac output (co) or index measurements (ci) if available. 4. the waveform a. place the pump in 1:2 mode if patient can tolerate. print a strip of the waveform. (pt should augment at equal to or greater than the unassisted systole.)

b. balloon augmentation should occur after the aortic valve closes. a sharp v wave should be noted, and the diacritic notch disappears. c. balloon assisted aortic end diastolic pressure should be lower than patient's unassisted diastolic pressure. d. the assisted systolic pressure will be less than the unassisted systolic pressure. e. balloon pressure waveform should have the following: filling pressure above zero, sharp up stroke, pressure plateau, rapid deflation 5. review all iv fluid and medication infusions and transfer medications to transport pumps iv. set up the transport iabp 1. check arterial line and other transducers for compatibility and change if needed. 2. check for fiber-optic sensor connector. 3. plug iabp model to ac power. turn on pump and allow system to perform a self-check. check helium level and battery levels. 4. place ecg skin leads on patient and monitor ecg on iabp screen. 5. pressure transducer: open transducer vent to atmosphere. press zero pressure key. close pressure transducer vent to atmosphere and assess pressure wave on display screen. 6. transfer the balloon lumen tubing to the iabp console. 7. check settings on iabp to match previous settings. timing should be "auto". place pump initially in 1:2 mode and print waveform strip to ensure accurate timing and augmentation. 8. start pumping by removing it from standby mode. v. prepare patient for transport and move patient over to stretcher. reassess patient and insertion site. keep patient in supine position with hob elevated no more than 30 degrees. vi. load patient and iabp. secure pump with two approved straps. use caution when loading. if iabp disconnects, immediately reattach the patient's balloon to the chamber. vii. reassess patient and monitor iabp tubing for connectivity, blood, possible dislodgment. complete a cardiovascular and peripheral reassessment every 15 minutes. monitor for signs of inappropriate iabp placement or balloon perforation. cccxxix. trouble shooting

a. if cardiac rhythm changes to asystole, change trigger to atrial pressure. decrease augmentation to 50%. b. when cardioverting/defibrillating, stand clear from patient and equipment. c. reasons pump is not augmenting i. tachycardia or bradycardia: affects cardiac output ii. hypovolemia: map<50 at the time of insertion is not enough to augment iii. svr>1200: post coronary artery bypass grafting (cabg) or on high dose vasopressors, pump is unable to compete with increased afterload. iv. balloon volume is too low. d. timing errors i. early inflation: iabp prior to aortic valve closure (dicrotic notch) 1. will increase left ventricular wall stress, afterload, and mvo2 ii. late inflation: inflation markedly after closure of aortic valve 1. produces suboptimal coronary perfusion iii. early deflation: sharp drop following diastolic augmentation 1. causes increased assisted systolic pressure, suboptimal coronary flow, and suboptimal afterload reduction iv. late deflation: afterload reduction is absent 1. left ventricle is ejecting against a greater resistance e. loss of vacuum of iabp failure i. leak in the gas: check and tighten connections on the pneumatic tubing ii. check the power source iii. hand inflate and deflate the balloon every 5 minutes with half the total balloon volume f. suspected balloon perforation i. observe for a loss of >10mm in diastolic augmentation ii. check for blood in the catheter iii. assess for changes in the balloon pressure waveform iv. put iabp in standby v. clamp the iabp catheter to prevent arterial blood back up vi. disconnect iabp catheter form the iabp console vii. call control to notify the physician - the catheter should not lie dormant for longer than 30 minutes g. gas loss: pressure waveform falls below baseline h. kink: rounded balloon waveform i. poor cardiac function or low vascular resistance: refer to medical management per guid-3203-m011 hypotension.
cccxxx. documentation: a. ems charts cccxxxi. references: a. www.getinge.com (previously maquet)

title: Ventilation with a Mechanical Ventilator
normalizedText: ventilation with a mechanical ventilator guideline title: ventilation with a mechanical ventilator guideline number: guid-3203- pr024 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 2/20/2020, 6/8/2021 developed by: air care team approved by: christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 cccxxxii. purpose: to provide adequate support of ventilation and oxygenation to patients with an advanced airway during transport cccxxxiii. definitions: a. bvm - bag valve mask b. rr - respiratory rate c. ards - acute respiratory distress syndrome d. copd - chronic obstructive pulmonary disease e. i:e - inspiratory : expiratory ratio f. abg - arterial blood gas g. pip - peak inspiratory pressure h. peep - positive end expiratory pressure i. etco2 - end-tidal co2 cccxxxiv. department guideline: a. equipment: i. revel ventilator ii. disposable ventilator circuit iii. compressed oxygen source yielding 50 psi output iv. charged internal battery for ventilator, power supply for charging and power source v. bag-valve-mask with oxygen reservoir vi. etco2 detector vii. pulse oximeter viii. miscellaneous ventilator circuit adjuncts. (e.g., disposable peep valve, hepa filter, heat-moisture exchanger, flex connector, aerosol medication chamber) b. indications: i. any patient requiring placement of an artificial airway to support ventilation and/or oxygenation. c. contraindications: i. mechanical ventilation in patients with suspected tension pneumothorax is contraindicated until the tension pneumothorax has been resolved. d. general expectations i. perform a ventilator check at the beginning of each shift to ensure there is not any mechanical or electronic failure. ii. for interfacility transports, the ventilator should be removed from the aircraft and taken to the patient's side in any patient with a known advanced airway or potential need for advanced airway. iii. for scene flights, the ventilator should be attached to the patient after loading in the aircraft as soon as practice and safe with regard to aircraft lifting operations. iv. for long distance flights, calculate the anticipated oxygen need. e. procedures: i. verify artificial airway patency and position. apply etco2 detection and pulse oximetry equipment to verify artificial airway placement and to evaluate effectiveness of current ventilation technique per guid-pr009 -end-tidal co2 monitoring. ii. prepare the bvm device for emergent use in case of a ventilator failure iii. obtain calculated and non-calculated ventilator settings and deploy mechanical ventilation therapy to the patient 1. identify the minute ventilation needs of the patient by multiplying the respiratory rate and the tidal volume. iv. ensure the desired settings are reaching the patient v. drive changes based on pulse oximetry and etco2 data. f. revel ventilator: i. push the on/off button on the lower interface panel. verify that the ventilator powers up, displays the ventilator model name (revel) or part number (xxxxx-xxx) followed by same patient in the display window. ii. once the ventilator is turned on, the window displays same patient. turn the scroll knob to the right until new patient displays on the window. push the select button to confirm. iii. to set for patient size (the window displays patient id) scroll the knob to the right until patient size displays on the window. push the select button to confirm. then scroll the knob until the desired patient size displays (infant, pediatric or adult). push the select button to confirm. iv. once the patient size is selected, an option for intubated or nppv will show, scroll the knob until the desired option displays for intubate or noninvasive positive-pressure ventilation (nppv). push the select button to confirm. v. select appropriate mode vi. set trigger to volume-cycled if required. vii. select desired tidal volume (vt). patients without shock should typically be ventilated with a vt of 6-8ml/kg based upon ideal body weight. tidal volume equals lung protection. 1. ibw formula is based on patient's height. a. males = 50 + 2.3 [height (inches) - 60] b. females = 45.5 + 2.3 [height (inches) -60] viii. select appropriate respiratory rate (rr). 1. 16-18 breaths per minute should be starting point unless clinical presentation dictates otherwise. (metabolic acidosis, dka, etc.) 2. respiratory rate equals ventilation. adjust rr to achieve ph goals. ix. select desired fio2 if applicable. 1. an fio2 of 0.21-1.00 may be selected. consider current abg, spo2 and anticipated altitude changes when selecting an fio2 for transport. 2. consider that pao2's greater than 300 are associated with increased mortality in traumatic brain injury. an fio2 of 0.5 or less is always safe, as pao2's will not exceed 300 at this setting. in general, use the lowest fio2 setting that maintains spo2 at 93-99%. x. if positive end expiratory pressure (peep) is required, select the desired amount and set via the ventilator's peep function. 1. peep is typically set within the range of 1-20 cm h2o. (see clinical pearls section, below, for more information.) 2. fio2 and peep together equal oxygenation. see fio2/peep scale below for recommendations for fio2 and peep. xi. plateau pressure (pplat) - in volume mode, perform an inspiratory hold. 1. pplat should be less than 30cm h20. 2. if higher than 30mm h20 a. decrease tidal volume to prevent potential barotrauma. b. consider lung compliance problems and possibly switch to pressure control. xii. select desired inspiratory time (it). 1. adult patients should receive an it of 0.8-1.5 seconds. 2. pediatric patients should receive an it of 0.5-1.0 seconds. 3. clinical goal should be to maintain an i:e ratio of no less than 1:2. xiii. the ventilator may be equipped with a default 1:2 i:e ratio setting. this may be selected instead of setting an it. caution should be used in patients with an obstructive lung component. xiv. set high pressure alarm at 50cm h2o and low-pressure alarm at 10cm h2o. occlude patient end of ventilator circuit and test for leaks, correct function, and observe delivery of several breaths. xv. attach patient to the ventilator and observe peak inspiratory pressure (pip). 1. reset high pressure alarm to 10-15cm h2o above the pip. 2. reset low inspiratory pressure alarm to 10-15cm h2o below the pip. xvi. make note of mean airway pressure (paw) xvii. set low minute volume alarm if so equipped xviii. observe delivery of several breaths. evaluate patient for adequate chest rise, etco2 and spo2. adjust ventilator settings as necessary to improve clinical parameters. 1. monitor patient for autopeep, cuff leaks, deadspace problems, and adequate sedation xix. observe for alarms and immediately view alarm message to assist in identifying possible cause. (see alarm troubleshooting section, below, for more information.) xx. if at any time the ventilator should fail or an alarm is received that cannot be corrected, the patient should be immediately ventilated with a bag-valve device attached to a 100% oxygen source until ventilator issue can be resolved. xxi. at the completion of the transport, dispose of ventilator circuit and clean ventilator as necessary g. alarm troubleshooting: i. crashing ventilator patient pneumonic to help troubleshoot problem. 1. d - dislodgement - check etco2, tube depth and listen for lung sounds 2. o - obstruction - secretions, foreign object 3. p - pneumothorax - high pip, decreased exhaled tidal volume, tachycardia, hypotension. jvd, trachea deviation late signs. 4. e - equipment - this can cause high and low pressure alarms. start at patient and work back to ventilator. 5. s - stack breaths - immediately disconnect vent circuit from et tube. often seen in copd and asthma patient who have difficulty exhaling. ii. possible causes of low inspiratory pressure alarm: 1. disconnect of one of the ventilator circuit tubes 2. decrease in source gas pressure (e.g., oxygen tank has dropped below 500psi). 3. increase in lung compliance causing lowered peak inspiratory pressure (pip) 4. cuff leak 5. exhalation valve failure causing gas escape during inspiration 6. patient is spontaneously inspiring and requiring a greater flow than the ventilator is delivering. iii. possible causes of high-pressure limit alarm: 1. occlusion of artificial airway a. kinked tube, mucous plug, or patient is biting on airway tube 2. decrease in lung compliance a. worsening pulmonary injury, bronchospasm, development of a pneumothorax, etc. 3. patient is coughing 4. exhalation valve stuck closed, not allowing patient to exhale adequately. cccxxxv. documentation a. ems charts i. record all set parameters, pip and paw trends in the patient transport record. note in patient transport record that all alarms were set. cccxxxvi. references: a. ardsnet. (n.d.). mechanical ventilation protocol summary. retrieved from http://www.ardsnet.org/files/ventilator_protocol_2008-07.pdf b. ardsnet fio2/peep scale table 7.3 prompted changes based on ongoing physiological data parameter level blood pressure low spo2 low etco2 low high frequency high exhaled tidal volume low high peak inspiratory pressure high ongoing ventilator monitor corrective action: 1. fluid or pressor therapy, consider tension pneumothorax 2. check for overbreathing 3. consider "resuscitation mode" ↑ peep or fio2 rr, check perfusion status (see low blood pressure above) ↑ rr consider sedation; patient is over-breathing the set ventilator rate 1. r/o air trapping (autopeep of 0 confirms no air-trapping) 2. r/o small leaks - check for full tracheal tube pilot bulb 3. assume a dead space problem: ↑ vt (while maintaining a pip < 35 cm h2o) n/a 1. assess for plateau pressure 2. rule out dopes pneumonic . ¡e dislodgement: tracheal tube misplaced • ¡e obstruction: kink in tracheal tube, stepping on circuit, secretions • ¡e pneumothorax ¡ e equipment: ensure tight and proper connections • ¡e sedation: be sure to adequately sedate your patients high pip/normal plateau pressure = airway resistance problem; consider bronchodilators 2. increase vt or pc (possibly too low) low 1. reconnect all connections (most likely the circuit is disconnected) plateau pressure high inspiratory hold maneuver: high pip/ high plateau pressure = lung compliance problem; change to pс. autopeер high expiratory hold maneuver: if autopeep is > 0, the patient is air trapping; provide a longer e time by adjusting the i:e ratio to 1:4, 1:5, or 1:6. also consider a bronchodilator to allow for more complete exhalation

pages.text: ventilation with a mechanical ventilator guideline title: ventilation with a mechanical ventilator guideline number: guid-3203- pr024 issue date: 09/09/2014 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 2/20/2020, 6/8/2021 developed by: air care team approved by: christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 cccxxxii. purpose: to provide adequate support of ventilation and oxygenation to patients with an advanced airway during transport cccxxxiii. definitions: a. bvm - bag valve mask b. rr - respiratory rate c. ards - acute respiratory distress syndrome d. copd - chronic obstructive pulmonary disease e. i:e - inspiratory : expiratory ratio f. abg - arterial blood gas g. pip - peak inspiratory pressure h. peep - positive end expiratory pressure i. etco2 - end-tidal co2 cccxxxiv. department guideline: a. equipment: i. revel ventilator ii. disposable ventilator circuit iii. compressed oxygen source yielding 50 psi output iv. charged internal battery for ventilator, power supply for charging and power source v. bag-valve-mask with oxygen reservoir vi. etco2 detector vii. pulse oximeter viii. miscellaneous ventilator circuit adjuncts. (e.g., disposable peep valve, hepa filter, heat-moisture exchanger, flex connector, aerosol medication chamber) b. indications: i. any patient requiring placement of an artificial airway to support ventilation and/or oxygenation. c. contraindications:

i. mechanical ventilation in patients with suspected tension pneumothorax is contraindicated until the tension pneumothorax has been resolved. d. general expectations i. perform a ventilator check at the beginning of each shift to ensure there is not any mechanical or electronic failure. ii. for interfacility transports, the ventilator should be removed from the aircraft and taken to the patient's side in any patient with a known advanced airway or potential need for advanced airway. iii. for scene flights, the ventilator should be attached to the patient after loading in the aircraft as soon as practice and safe with regard to aircraft lifting operations. iv. for long distance flights, calculate the anticipated oxygen need. e. procedures: i. verify artificial airway patency and position. apply etco2 detection and pulse oximetry equipment to verify artificial airway placement and to evaluate effectiveness of current ventilation technique per guid-pr009 -end-tidal co2 monitoring. ii. prepare the bvm device for emergent use in case of a ventilator failure iii. obtain calculated and non-calculated ventilator settings and deploy mechanical ventilation therapy to the patient 1. identify the minute ventilation needs of the patient by multiplying the respiratory rate and the tidal volume. iv. ensure the desired settings are reaching the patient v. drive changes based on pulse oximetry and etco2 data. f. revel ventilator: i. push the on/off button on the lower interface panel. verify that the ventilator powers up, displays the ventilator model name (revel) or part number (xxxxx-xxx) followed by same patient in the display window. ii. once the ventilator is turned on, the window displays same patient. turn the scroll knob to the right until new patient displays on the window. push the select button to confirm. iii. to set for patient size (the window displays patient id) scroll the knob to the right until patient size displays on the window. push the select button to confirm. then scroll the knob until the desired patient size displays (infant, pediatric or adult). push the select button to confirm. iv. once the patient size is selected, an option for intubated or nppv will show, scroll the knob until the desired option displays for intubate or noninvasive positive-pressure ventilation (nppv). push the select button to confirm. v. select appropriate mode vi. set trigger to volume-cycled if required.

vii. select desired tidal volume (vt). patients without shock should typically be ventilated with a vt of 6-8ml/kg based upon ideal body weight. tidal volume equals lung protection. 1. ibw formula is based on patient's height. a. males = 50 + 2.3 [height (inches) - 60] b. females = 45.5 + 2.3 [height (inches) -60] viii. select appropriate respiratory rate (rr). 1. 16-18 breaths per minute should be starting point unless clinical presentation dictates otherwise. (metabolic acidosis, dka, etc.) 2. respiratory rate equals ventilation. adjust rr to achieve ph goals. ix. select desired fio2 if applicable. 1. an fio2 of 0.21-1.00 may be selected. consider current abg, spo2 and anticipated altitude changes when selecting an fio2 for transport. 2. consider that pao2's greater than 300 are associated with increased mortality in traumatic brain injury. an fio2 of 0.5 or less is always safe, as pao2's will not exceed 300 at this setting. in general, use the lowest fio2 setting that maintains spo2 at 93-99%. x. if positive end expiratory pressure (peep) is required, select the desired amount and set via the ventilator's peep function. 1. peep is typically set within the range of 1-20 cm h2o. (see clinical pearls section, below, for more information.) 2. fio2 and peep together equal oxygenation. see fio2/peep scale below for recommendations for fio2 and peep. xi. plateau pressure (pplat) - in volume mode, perform an inspiratory hold. 1. pplat should be less than 30cm h20. 2. if higher than 30mm h20 a. decrease tidal volume to prevent potential barotrauma. b. consider lung compliance problems and possibly switch to pressure control. xii. select desired inspiratory time (it). 1. adult patients should receive an it of 0.8-1.5 seconds. 2. pediatric patients should receive an it of 0.5-1.0 seconds. 3. clinical goal should be to maintain an i:e ratio of no less than 1:2. xiii. the ventilator may be equipped with a default 1:2 i:e ratio setting. this may be selected instead of setting an it. caution should be used in patients with an obstructive lung

component. xiv. set high pressure alarm at 50cm h2o and low-pressure alarm at 10cm h2o. occlude patient end of ventilator circuit and test for leaks, correct function, and observe delivery of several breaths. xv. attach patient to the ventilator and observe peak inspiratory pressure (pip). 1. reset high pressure alarm to 10-15cm h2o above the pip. 2. reset low inspiratory pressure alarm to 10-15cm h2o below the pip. xvi. make note of mean airway pressure (paw) xvii. set low minute volume alarm if so equipped xviii. observe delivery of several breaths. evaluate patient for adequate chest rise, etco2 and spo2. adjust ventilator settings as necessary to improve clinical parameters. 1. monitor patient for autopeep, cuff leaks, deadspace problems, and adequate sedation xix. observe for alarms and immediately view alarm message to assist in identifying possible cause. (see alarm troubleshooting section, below, for more information.) xx. if at any time the ventilator should fail or an alarm is received that cannot be corrected, the patient should be immediately ventilated with a bag-valve device attached to a 100% oxygen source until ventilator issue can be resolved. xxi. at the completion of the transport, dispose of ventilator circuit and clean ventilator as necessary g. alarm troubleshooting: i. crashing ventilator patient pneumonic to help troubleshoot problem. 1. d - dislodgement - check etco2, tube depth and listen for lung sounds 2. o - obstruction - secretions, foreign object 3. p - pneumothorax - high pip, decreased exhaled tidal volume, tachycardia, hypotension. jvd, trachea deviation late signs. 4. e - equipment - this can cause high and low pressure alarms. start at patient and work back to ventilator. 5. s - stack breaths - immediately disconnect vent circuit from et tube. often seen in copd and asthma patient who have difficulty exhaling. ii. possible causes of low inspiratory pressure alarm: 1. disconnect of one of the ventilator circuit tubes 2. decrease in source gas pressure (e.g., oxygen tank has dropped below 500psi).

3. increase in lung compliance causing lowered peak inspiratory pressure (pip) 4. cuff leak 5. exhalation valve failure causing gas escape during inspiration 6. patient is spontaneously inspiring and requiring a greater flow than the ventilator is delivering. iii. possible causes of high-pressure limit alarm: 1. occlusion of artificial airway a. kinked tube, mucous plug, or patient is biting on airway tube 2. decrease in lung compliance a. worsening pulmonary injury, bronchospasm, development of a pneumothorax, etc. 3. patient is coughing 4. exhalation valve stuck closed, not allowing patient to exhale adequately. cccxxxv. documentation a. ems charts i. record all set parameters, pip and paw trends in the patient transport record. note in patient transport record that all alarms were set. cccxxxvi. references: a. ardsnet. (n.d.). mechanical ventilation protocol summary. retrieved from http://www.ardsnet.org/files/ventilator_protocol_2008-07.pdf b. ardsnet fio2/peep scale

table 7.3 prompted changes based on ongoing physiological data parameter level blood pressure low spo2 low etco2 low high frequency high exhaled tidal volume low high peak inspiratory pressure high ongoing ventilator monitor corrective action: 1. fluid or pressor therapy, consider tension pneumothorax 2. check for overbreathing 3. consider "resuscitation mode" ↑ peep or fio2 rr, check perfusion status (see low blood pressure above) ↑ rr consider sedation; patient is over-breathing the set ventilator rate 1. r/o air trapping (autopeep of 0 confirms no air-trapping) 2. r/o small leaks - check for full tracheal tube pilot bulb 3. assume a dead space problem: ↑ vt (while maintaining a pip < 35 cm h2o) n/a 1. assess for plateau pressure 2. rule out dopes pneumonic . ¡e dislodgement: tracheal tube misplaced • ¡e obstruction: kink in tracheal tube, stepping on circuit, secretions • ¡e pneumothorax ¡ e equipment: ensure tight and proper connections • ¡e sedation: be sure to adequately sedate your patients high pip/normal plateau pressure = airway resistance problem; consider bronchodilators 2. increase vt or pc (possibly too low) low 1. reconnect all connections (most likely the circuit is disconnected) plateau pressure high inspiratory hold maneuver: high pip/ high plateau pressure = lung compliance problem; change to pс. autopeер high expiratory hold maneuver: if autopeep is > 0, the patient is air trapping; provide a longer e time by adjusting the i:e ratio to 1:4, 1:5, or 1:6. also consider a bronchodilator to allow for more complete exhalation

title: Emergency Blood Product Administration
normalizedText: emergency blood product administration guideline title: emergency blood product administration guideline number: guid-3203- pr025 issue date: 7/10/2019 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 6/8/2021 developed by: air care team approved by: christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 i. purpose: this department guideline is used to provide safe and effective administration of uncrossmatched blood and blood products to air care team (act) patients with hemorrhagic shock from trauma or other mechanisms. this guideline is also used to recognize and effectively treat transfusion reactions. ii. definitions: when used in this department guideline, these terms have the following meanings. a. blood products: whole blood, o positive and o negative b. iv - intravenous c. sbp - systolic blood pressure d. ns - normal saline iii. department guideline: a. equipment: i. personal protective equipment (ppe) ii. appropriate blood product iii. filtered blood tubing or leukocyte reduction filter iv. a patent iv with normal saline infusing v. pressure bags may be utilized with caution for transfusing blood as long as the pressure inside the bag does not exceed 300 mmhg b. indications: i. adult indications 1. blunt or penetrating trauma, hypovolemia with acute blood loss not limited to gastrointestinal hemorrhage, post-partum hemorrhage, or severe coagulopathy, signs and symptoms of hemorrhagic shock with: a. sbp < 90 mmhg or b. pulse rate >110 bpm or c. evidence of peripheral vasoconstriction including cool, pale skin and delayed capillary refill > 2 seconds. 2. hemorrhage with documented hgb < 7 g/dl ii. pediatric indications 1. blunt or penetrating trauma, hypovolemia with acute blood loss, signs and symptoms of hemorrhagic shock with: a. evidence of hypoperfusion (pallor, peripheral vasoconstriction including cool, pale skin and delayed capillary refill > 2 seconds) or b. sbp < (70 mmhg + 2 x age) or c. pulse rate - clinical context should be taken into account when assessing pulse rate number (e.g., presence of fever, dehydration, anxiety, pain, etc.) i. neonates (less than 28 days) < 100 bpm or > 180 bpm ii. infant (1 month - 1 year) < 80 bpm or > 160 bpm iii. child (1 - 10 years) < 60 bpm or > 130 bpm iv. older child (>10 years) < 60 bpm or > 110 bpm 2. hemorrhage with documented hgb < 7 g/dl c. contraindications: i. patients or patient's legal representatives' refusal of blood or blood products due to religious or personal reasons. ii. previous history of significant blood transfusion reaction. d. dosage: i. adult 1. bolus entire unit of whole blood iv/io, may give second unit of whole blood if indicated ii. pediatric 1. bolus 20 ml/kg of whole blood iv/io, may repeat if indicated e. procedure: i. consider the administration of tranexamic acid (txa), see air care team guid-3203-t016 tranexamic acid for severe hemorrhage in trauma patients. ii. utilize appropriate ppe. iii. initially assess for the following: 1. vital signs. 2. skin condition: rashes, redness, etc. 3. size and integrity of iv site. a. adults: prefer at least an 18 g for whole blood b. pediatrics: 22-24 g. c. breath sounds. d. fluid volume status. e. history of previous transfusion reactions. iv. blood products must be maintained at a controlled temperature of 1 - 6 degrees celcius during transport and must be infused within 4 hours of removal from the thermal control environment. v. select the appropriate blood product 1. type a whole blood for both males and females of all ages. 2. females: a. childbearing age or below: o negative (o-) whole blood if available. b. o+ can be given to women of childbearing years in life-threatening situations if o- is not available: notify the receiving facility that patient received o+ and will require rho(d) immune globulin (rhogam). c. beyond childbearing age: o+ whole blood vi. ideally blood should be warmed during administration using approved blood/fluid warmer device following manufacturer's instructions, see guid3202-pr026 warming of blood products and intravenous fluids. vii. never transfuse blood in piggyback fashion into any iv with medications infusing. only piggyback into ns line. viii. once the patient has been identified as meeting the criteria for emergent transfusion of unmatched blood: 1. bedside verification process for emergency release blood a. at the bedside the transfusionist (act rn) or independent verifier (credentialed act paramedic) verifies the following information: patient name/alias, mr#, blood product label type (o negative or o positive), the blood tag unit number and expiration date. b. enter date and time on the designated line on the blood tag if all information matches c. after verification by both the transfusionist (act rn) and the independent verifier (credentialed act paramedic); the blood is transfused. 2. may place blood in pressure bag and inflate the bag not to exceed 300 mmhg. 3. bolus entire unit whole blood for adult. 4. see part d. ii, 1 - above - pediatric dosage for pediatric dosing and rate of transfusion. 5. between each transfusion: a. flush iv with ns flush. b. record time of completion on uncrossmatched emergency blood release form. f. transfusion reaction - reassess airway patency, hemodynamic stability and treat any transfusion reaction or complications per act guid-3203-m004 anaphylaxis for adults or act guid-3203-ped002 pediatric allergic reaction/anaphylaxis for pediatrics. i. immediate signs and symptoms 1. allergic reaction - hives, facial swelling, laryngeal edema, bronchospasm 2. febrile - fever (> 1 c change from baseline), chills, headache, flushing, tachycardia 3. hemolytic reaction - sudden onset of chills, fever, flushing, lower back or chest pain. also, may present as shock, excess bleeding, hematuria 4. circulatory overload - dyspnea, tachypnea, hypertension with headache 5. contaminated blood - shaking fever with dry flushed skin, severe hypotension, abdominal pain, vomiting, diarrhea. ii. if a transfusion reaction occurs: 1. stop transfusion immediately. 2. assess vital signs. 3. remove blood bag and tubing and set aside. 4. provide systemic support as required. 5. assure bag and tubing are given to receiving blood bank or rn/appropriate medical staff, upon arrival at receiving facility, along with a copy of the uncrossmatched emergency blood release form with transfusion reaction section completed. iii. once transfusion of any products is complete: 1. complete the uncrossmatched emergency blood release form in its entirety (leave blood bank slip attached to units but do not complete). 2. notify receiving physician of any transfusion reactions and treatment provided. 3. notify receiving physician if o+ blood was given to a female of child bearing age. 4. obtain all necessary signatures. 5. make 2 copies of completed uncrossmatched emergency blood release form: original must stay with patients chart regardless of receiving facility. g. orlando regional medical center (ormc), arnold palmer hospital or winnie palmer hospital as receiving facility: 1. bring completed blood product bags, copy of the uncrossmatched emergency blood release form, and a patient label to the blood bank for testing and replacement of products. 2. complete the air medical blood component disposition log immediately upon returning to aircraft base of operations. 3. scan and email component disposition log to r-actbloodbankqc@orlandohealth.com and retain copy at base in blood product tracking binder. 4. upload a copy of the uncrossmatched emergency blood release form to emscharts patient record. h. other hospital as receiving facility: 1. leave original form with patient chart. leave a copy of the uncrossmatched emergency blood release form with the completed blood products bags and notify facility that the form and units must get to the blood bank for testing. 2. proceed to ormc, if weather and operational considerations allow, to replace used blood products. 3. complete the air medical blood component disposition log immediately upon returning to aircraft base of operations. 4. scan and email component disposition log to r-actbloodbankqc@orlandohealth.com and retain copy at base in blood product tracking binder. 5. upload a copy of the uncrossmatched emergency blood release form to emscharts patient record. i. if unable to restock blood products at ormc, contact blood supplier immediately upon return to base and request replacement units be delivered. complete air medical blood component disposition log immediately upon receiving units. c. realted guidelines and processes: i. guid3203-g002 - general patient care ii. guid3203-t016 - tranexamic acid iii. guid3203-m004 - anaphylaxis iv. guid3203-ped002 - pediatric allergic reaction/anaphylaxis v. guid3203-ped009 - pediatric hypotension d. documentation: i. emscharts blood product administration on page 7 ii. presence or absence of transfusion reaction iii. vital signs before transfusion and when completed e. uncrossmatched emergency blood release form (no number) f. air medical blood component disposition log - form 842.3 g. references: i. air methods blood products administration for hemorrhagic shock ii. maher, p., et al (2017). logistical concerns for prehospital blood product use by air medical services. air medical journal 36:263-267

fever (> 1 c change from baseline), chills, headache, flushing, tachycardia 3. hemolytic reaction - sudden onset of chills, fever, flushing, lower back or chest pain. also, may present as shock, excess bleeding, hematuria 4. circulatory overload - r-actbloodbankqc@orlandohealth.com and retain copy at base in blood product tracking binder. 4. upload a copy of the uncrossmatched emergency blood release form to emscharts patient record. h. other hospital as receiving facility: 1. leave original form with patient chart. leave a copy of the uncrossmatched emergency blood release form with the completed blood products bags and notify facility that the form and units must get to the blood bank for testing. 2. proceed to ormc, if weather and operational considerations allow, to replace used blood products. 3. complete the air medical blood component disposition log immediately upon returning to aircraft base of operations. 4. scan and email component disposition log to r-actbloodbankqc@orlandohealth.com and retain copy at base in blood product tracking binder. 5. upload a copy of the uncrossmatched emergency blood release form to emscharts patient record. i. if unable to restock blood products at ormc, contact blood supplier immediately upon return to base and request replacement units be delivered. complete air medical blood component disposition log immediately upon receiving units. c. realted guidelines and processes: i. guid3203-g002 - general patient care ii. guid3203-t016 - tranexamic acid iii. guid3203-m004 - anaphylaxis iv. guid3203-ped002 - pediatric allergic reaction/anaphylaxis v. guid3203-ped009 - pediatric hypotension d. documentation: i. emscharts blood product administration on page 7 ii. presence or absence of transfusion reaction iii. vital signs before transfusion and when completed e. uncrossmatched emergency blood release form (no number) f. air medical blood component disposition log - form 842.3 g. references: i. air methods blood products administration for hemorrhagic shock ii. maher, p., et al (2017). logistical concerns for prehospital blood product use by air medical services. air medical journal 36:263-267

title: Emergency Whole Blood Product Administration
normalizedText: emergency whole blood product administration issue date: 05/16/2022 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 6/8/2021, 07/01/2022 developed by: air care team approved by: christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 iv. purpose: this department guideline is used to provide safe and effective administration of uncrossmatched blood and blood products to air care team (act) patients with hemorrhagic shock from trauma or other mechanisms. this guideline is also used to recognize and effectively treat transfusion reactions. v. definitions: when used in this department guideline, these terms have the following meanings. a. blood products: whole blood, o positive and o negative b. iv - intravenous c. sbp - systolic blood pressure d. ns - normal saline vi. department guideline: a. equipment: i. personal protective equipment (ppe) ii. appropriate blood product iii. filtered blood tubing or leukocyte reduction filter iv. a patent iv with normal saline infusing v. pressure bags may be utilized with caution for transfusing blood as long as the pressure inside the bag does not exceed 300 mmhg b. indications: i. adult indications 1. blunt or penetrating trauma, hypovolemia with acute blood loss not limited to gastrointestinal hemorrhage, post-partum hemorrhage, or severe coagulopathy, signs and symptoms of hemorrhagic shock with: a. sbp < 90 mmhg or b. pulse rate >110 bpm or guideline title: emergency whole blood product administration guideline number: guid-3203- pr025b c. evidence of peripheral vasoconstriction including cool, pale skin and delayed capillary refill > 2 seconds. 2. hemorrhage with documented hgb < 7 g/dl ii. pediatric indications 1. blunt or penetrating trauma, hypovolemia with acute blood loss, signs and symptoms of hemorrhagic shock with: a. evidence of hypoperfusion (pallor, peripheral vasoconstriction including cool, pale skin and delayed capillary refill > 2 seconds) or b. sbp < (70 mmhg + 2 x age) or c. pulse rate - clinical context should be taken into account when assessing pulse rate number (e.g., presence of fever, dehydration, anxiety, pain, etc.) i. neonates (less than 28 days) < 100 bpm or > 180 bpm ii. infant (1 month - 1 year) < 80 bpm or > 160 bpm iii. child (1 - 10 years) < 60 bpm or > 130 bpm iv. older child (>10 years) < 60 bpm or > 110 bpm 2. hemorrhage with documented hgb < 7 g/dl c. contraindications: i. patients or patient's legal representatives' refusal of blood or blood products due to religious or personal reasons. ii. previous history of significant blood transfusion reaction. d. dosage: i. adult 1. bolus entire unit of whole blood iv/io, may give second unit of whole blood if indicated ii. pediatric 1. bolus 20 ml/kg of whole blood iv/io, may repeat if indicated e. procedure: i. consider the administration of tranexamic acid (txa), see air care team guid-3203-t016 tranexamic acid for severe hemorrhage in trauma patients. ii. utilize appropriate ppe. iii. initially assess for the following: 1. vital signs. 2. skin condition: rashes, redness, etc. 3. size and integrity of iv site. a. adults: prefer at least an 18 g for whole blood b. pediatrics: 22-24 g. c. breath sounds. d. fluid volume status. e. history of previous transfusion reactions. iv. blood products must be maintained at a controlled temperature of 1 - 6 degrees celcius during transport and must be infused within 4 hours of removal from the thermal control environment. v. select the appropriate blood product 1. type a whole blood for both males and females of all ages. 2. females: a. childbearing age or below: o negative (o-) whole blood if available. b. o+ can be given to women of childbearing years in life-threatening situations if o- is not available: notify the receiving facility that patient received o+ and will require rho(d) immune globulin (rhogam). c. beyond childbearing age: o+ whole blood vi. ideally blood should be warmed during administration using approved blood/fluid warmer device following manufacturer's instructions, see guid3202-pr026 warming of blood products and intravenous fluids. vii. never transfuse blood in piggyback fashion into any iv with medications infusing. only piggyback into ns line. viii. once the patient has been identified as meeting the criteria for emergent transfusion of unmatched blood: 1. bedside verification process for emergency release blood a. at the bedside the transfusionist (act rn) or independent verifier (credentialed act paramedic) verifies the following information: patient name/alias, mr#, blood product label type (o negative or o positive), the blood tag unit number and expiration date. b. enter date and time on the designated line on the blood tag if all information matches c. after verification by both the transfusionist (act rn) and the independent verifier (credentialed act paramedic); the blood is transfused. 2. may place blood in pressure bag and inflate the bag not to exceed 300 mmhg. 3. bolus entire unit whole blood for adult. 4. see part d. ii, 1 - above - pediatric dosage for pediatric dosing and rate of transfusion. 5. between each transfusion: a. flush iv with ns flush. b. record time of completion on uncrossmatched emergency blood release form. f. transfusion reaction - reassess airway patency, hemodynamic stability and treat any transfusion reaction or complications per act guid-3203-m004 anaphylaxis for adults or act guid-3203-ped002 pediatric allergic reaction/anaphylaxis for pediatrics. i. immediate signs and symptoms 1. allergic reaction - hives, facial swelling, laryngeal edema, bronchospasm 2. febrile - fever (> 1 c change from baseline), chills, headache, flushing, tachycardia 3. hemolytic reaction - sudden onset of chills, fever, flushing, lower back or chest pain. also, may present as shock, excess bleeding, hematuria 4. circulatory overload - dyspnea, tachypnea, hypertension with headache 5. contaminated blood - shaking fever with dry flushed skin, severe hypotension, abdominal pain, vomiting, diarrhea. ii. if a transfusion reaction occurs: 1. stop transfusion immediately. 2. assess vital signs. 3. remove blood bag and tubing and set aside. 4. provide systemic support as required. 5. assure bag and tubing are given to receiving blood bank or rn/appropriate medical staff, upon arrival at receiving facility, along with a copy of the uncrossmatched emergency blood release form with transfusion reaction section completed. iii. once transfusion of any products is complete: 1. complete the uncrossmatched emergency blood release form in its entirety (leave blood bank slip attached to units but do not complete). 2. notify receiving physician of any transfusion reactions and treatment provided. 3. notify receiving physician if o+ blood was given to a female of childbearing age. 4. obtain all necessary signatures. 5. make 2 copies of completed uncrossmatched emergency blood release form: original must stay with patients chart regardless of receiving facility. g. orlando regional medical center (ormc), arnold palmer hospital or winnie palmer hospital as receiving facility: 1. bring completed blood product bags, copy of the uncrossmatched emergency blood release form, and a patient label to the blood bank for testing and replacement of products. 2. complete the air medical blood component disposition log immediately upon returning to aircraft base of operations. 3. scan and email component disposition log to r-actbloodbankqc@orlandohealth.com and retain copy at base in blood product tracking binder. 4. upload a copy of the uncrossmatched emergency blood release form to emscharts patient record. h. other hospital as receiving facility: 1. leave original form with patient chart. leave a copy of the uncrossmatched emergency blood release form with the completed blood products bags and notify facility that the form and units must get to the blood bank for testing. 2. proceed to ormc, if weather and operational considerations allow, to replace used blood products. 3. complete the air medical blood component disposition log immediately upon returning to aircraft base of operations. 4. scan and email component disposition log to r-actbloodbankqc@orlandohealth.com and retain copy at base in blood product tracking binder. 5. upload a copy of the uncrossmatched emergency blood release form to emscharts patient record. i. if unable to restock blood products at ormc, contact blood supplier immediately upon return to base and request replacement units be delivered. complete air medical blood component disposition log immediately upon receiving units. h. realted guidelines and processes: i. guid3203-g002 - general patient care ii. guid3203-t016 - tranexamic acid iii. guid3203-m004 - anaphylaxis iv. guid3203-ped002 - pediatric allergic reaction/anaphylaxis v. guid3203-ped009 - pediatric hypotension i. documentation: i. emscharts blood product administration on page 7 ii. presence or absence of transfusion reaction iii. vital signs before transfusion and when completed j. uncrossmatched emergency blood release form (no number) k. air medical blood component disposition log - form 842.3 l. references: i. air methods blood products administration for hemorrhagic shock ii. maher, p., et al (2017). logistical concerns for prehospital blood product use by air medical services. air medical journal 36:263-267

title: Warming of Blood Products & Intravenous Fluids
normalizedText: warming of blood products & intravenous fluids issue date: 09/01/2020 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 6/8/2021 developed by: air care team approved by: christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 cccxxxvii. purpose: to provide guidance on warming blood products and intravenous fluids using the approved belmont buddy lite warming device. cccxxxviii. definitions: none cccxxxix. department guideline: a. set-up i. the disposable set must be inserted into the device before priming. ii. fit the disposable set so red marker on the device aligns with red marking of the set. iii. close the heater so a click is heard. b. priming i. prime blood giving set with 100ml of saline. ii. add 3-way tap if required to end blood giving set (for intraosseous). iii. connect giving set to blue end of the buddy disposable set. iv. prime the set by running fluid through. v. tap the heater (to discourage bubbles). vi. there is no need to remove the distal red cap for priming. c. operation i. turn the heater on - a blue light confirms operation. ii. connect the red end directly to the patient's cannula. iii. do not have any other bungs/stoppers, 3-way taps, extension sets etc. between the heater and the cannula (to minimize heat loss). iv. secure the heater to the sheet with the green clip. v. start infusing. a pressure bag may increase flow rate. guideline title: warming of blood products & intravenous fluids guideline number: guid-3203-pr026 vi. if pushing through via 3-way tap and syringe, be smooth and gentle. d. battery charge i. indicator is on the base of the unit. ii. battery will power warmer for 3 liters of cool fluid. e. be aware i. temperature achieved depends on flow rate and initial fluid temp. ii. a pressure release valve operates at 300 mmhg. iii. alarms are indicated by red led on battery housing and will usually relate to "out of temperature" range caused by low or high flow rates. alarm is cleared by turning off and on again. cccxl. documentation: ems charts cccxli. references: none

title: Pelvic Circumferential Compression Binder
normalizedText: pelvic circumferential compression binder guideline title: pelvic circumferential compression binder guideline number: guid- 3203-pr027 issue date: 12/26/2020 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 6/8/2021 developed by: air care team approved by: christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 cccxlii. purpose: to provide circumferential compression to reduce and stabilize a suspected pelvic ring disruption. the potential benefits of a pelvic binder include lower mortality rate, reduce blood loss, and aid in pain management. cccxliii. definitions: none cccxliv. department guideline: a. equipment i. appropriately sized commercial pelvic device "pelvicbinder" 1. adult 2. pediatric: hip circumference range 14-32 inches -or- ii. standard bed sheet b. indications i. suspected or proven unstable pelvic fracture demonstrated on radiograph ii. mechanism of injury consistent with possible pelvic fracture with physical exam 1. abrasions and contusions around the pelvic area 2. hematoma above the inguinal ligament, to the scrotum, or the thigh iii. mechanism of injury consistent with possible pelvic fracture with hemorrhagic shock without another source of hemorrhage. c. contraindications i. there are no reported contraindications to using pelvic circumferential compression. d. procedure i. gather equipment and supplies. anticipate need for complete spinal immobilization. ii. consider placing device on patient outside of the ems rescue truck, on a stretcher, with patient elevated at waist height. iii. assess pelvic area, distal circulation, sensation, and motor function of lower extremities. iv. remove any clothing/objects from pelvic area. in males, ensure genitalia are elevated out of groin area/not between legs. unclick the backboard straps. v. consider utilizing the 'lift and slide' under method or the 'sawing' method with 3 providers to place device. vi. if using pelvicbinder commercial device 1. place the pelvic binder beneath the patient and center at the level of trochanters. 2. bring the sewed end of the binder over the front of pelvis first, then the loose end 3. place the loose end of the plates on the cloth material. 4. use the gap with of the two ends as a cutting guide. "measure twice, cut once." 5. cut the excess material on the medial side of the plates. 6. after cutting you should have a 4-5-inch gap between the two plates. 7. attach the loose end of the binder plates to the belts. 8. pull the handle with cord to achieve pelvic reduction. the cord will lock into place at the point you stop pulling. 9. secure the loose cord into the cord holder. 10. if the binder needs to be loosened, pull slight tension on the cord and handle and lock into place. 11. pelvicbinder commercial device is radiolucent, mri compatible, & latex free. vii. if using pediatric pelvicbinder 1. it is half the width and length of standard pelvicbinder viii. if using sheet 1. fold the sheet smoothly (do not roll the sheet). 2. place the sheet under the patient's pelvis so it is centered over the greater trochanters, where the head of the femur attaches to the pelvis. 3. wrap and twist the two running ends of the sheet around the patient's pelvis. 4. once tightened, cross the running ends and tie/clamp them to maintain tension. ix. reassess distal circulation, sensation, and motor function of lower extremities. assess for any blood pooling in groin region. x. if mobility of the lower extremities is not limited by injuries or severe discomfort, consider binding the feet together with tape or sheet to maintain pelvic anatomical position. binders can work better if the lower extremities are also kept close together. sometimes fractures make this impractical or impossible. xi. re-click backboard straps if initially unclicked to secure patient to board. xii. avoid repeated assessments by manual compression of an unstable pelvis. cccxlv. documentation: ems charts cccxlvi. references: a. pelvicbinder medical products: https://www.pelvicbinder.com b. black, s.r., sathy, a.k., jo, c., wiley, m.r., minei, j.p., & starr, a.j. (2016). improved survival after pelvic fracture: 13-year experience at a single trauma center using a multidisciplinary institutional protocol. j orthop trauma, 30(1), 22-8. doi: 10.1097/bot.0000000000000443. pmid: 26360539.

title: Hemostatic Dressing – QuickClot Gauze
normalizedText: hemostatic dressing - quickclot gauze guideline title: hemostatic dressing - quickclot gauze guideline number: guid- 3203-pr028 issue date: 12/26/2020 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 6/8/2021 developed by: air care team approved by: christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 cccxlvii. purpose: hemorrhage is the most common cause of preventable death in trauma. this product stops bleeding significantly faster and more effectively than standard gauze when applied with manual pressure. quickclot products are hemostatics impregnated with kaolin, an inorganic mineral that accelerates the body's natural clotting ability. cccxlviii. definitions: none cccxlix. department guideline: a. apply direct pressure over the wound to slow bleeding. b. attempt to find the source of bleeding by wiping away any pooled blood. c. open product package using the existing tear points. keep the empty package. d. begin packing wound with one end of the gauze. e. it is critical to pack wound deeply so the gauze makes contact with the arterial or venous breech. f. do not simply cover the exterior wound with the gauze. g. continue packing the wound until the entire cavity is filled. h. large wound may require more than one package if quickclot gauze. i. utilize excess gauze to apply pressure directly over wound until bleeding is controlled. use firm consistent pressure with your hand. do not move gauze to check site. this can take 3 minutes. j. if the wound continues to bleed despite perceived adequate packing or if blood soaks through the gauze, remove the previous gauze placed. wipe the area of pooled blood and find the source of bleeding. use a new package of quickclot and repack the wound. k. once the bleeding is controlled, bandage the wound tightly to maintain pressure on the gauze and to guard against further contamination. l. tuck the package of the product into the gauze to alert receiving medical personnel that the hemostatic agent has been used. cccl. documentation: ems charts cccli. references: https://quikclot.com

title: Prone Positioning
normalizedText: prone positioning guideline title: prone positioning guideline number: guid-3203- pr029 issue date: 1/8/2021 replaces dept. policy: revision dates: 6/8/2021 developed by: air care team approved by: christopher hunter, md air care team medical director approved by: signature: signature: department numbers: 3203 ccclii. purpose: prone positioning of patients with acute respiratory distress syndrome (ards) may lead to improved oxygenation and decreased mortality. the mechanism of benefit from prone positioning is multifactorial, including improved lung recruitment, improved oxygenation, and reduced pleural pressure gradient across the lungs, leading to more effective distribution of tidal volume. cccliii. definitions: a. act - air care team b. ards - acute respiratory distress syndrome c. fpm - flight paramedic d. frn - flight registered nurse cccliv. department guideline: a. prone positioning of mechanically ventilated patient initiated by sending physician i. assessment/inclusion criteria 1. severe ards 2. other pulmonary conditions believed to be improved by repositioning ii. assessment/exclusion criteria 1. inability to tolerate positioning b. many prone patients are unstable and have several lines, tubes, and monitors, take care to be deliberate in movement and transfer c. prepare patient for transfer i. flight nurse leads crew with "time out" and brief of intended course of action ii. connect patient to act ventilator per guid-pr024 - ventilation with a mechanical ventilator. 1. use most appropriate transport settings to maintain current clinical needs 2. patient should be on act ventilator minimum of 10 minutes prior to transfer to assess for negative changes iii. transition patient to act monitor. place ecg electrodes to posterior thorax. iv. drape lines up and over right shoulder v. place defibrillator pads in the best possible position; anterior- posterior or anterior-lateral position. posterior-lateral and bi-axillary can be acceptable vi. ensure all lines and tubes are secure vii. consider analgesia & sedation management per guid-3203-g005 analgesia and sedation management or guid-g007 - propofol diprivan (if ordered by sending facility) to maintain rass scale -4. 1. consider rocuronium (zemuron) 0.6-1.2mg/kg ivp/io (adult) or 0.6mg/kg ivp (peds) for improved ventilator synchronicity during transport if patient is intubated/mechanically ventilated and has adequate sedation. **do not give a paralytic drug if patient cannot be sedated** viii. place patient's arms at their side (neutral position) ix. position the transport stretcher next to the hospital bed x. place padding parallel to the patient's chest and hips on stretcher, if not already underneath and coming with the patient xi. have intubation and airway management equipment at bedside and prepared prior to moving patient xii. have a minimum of 5 staff at bedside to safely move patient d. transfer patient i. act fpm at head of bed (hob), controlling movement and securing ett 1. act fpm at hob will be intubator, if needed ii. use transfer sheet or board for movement iii. any participant will call "stop" during the procedure if concerned iv. act frn + hospital staff next to our stretcher v. 2 hospital staff next to hospital bed vi. patient movement coordinated from provider at hob vii. move patient in slow, controlled stages across bed to stretcher viii. be mindful of endotracheal tube (ett), circuit, iv lines, and tubes during move ix. move small amount, reposition lines / tubes as needed; repeat x. patient's face is toward right shoulder, with ett/circuit off right shoulder e. post-transfer assessment i. reassess patient (vital signs/hemodynamic /dysrhythmias, etco2, worsening gas exchange, lines/tubes,) ii. observe patient for clinical change for minimum 10 minutes after moving iii. check patient placement & padding: eyes closed, ears not folded, head not hyperextended, no pressure on eyes/nose. pad face as needed. (reassess en route) iv. assure sedation, analgesia, and paralysis is uninterrupted en route f. resuscitation and cardiac arrest i. turning a critically ill prone patient in an emergency is associated with significant risk. there is risk of displacement of the endotracheal tube, removal of lines or tubes, as well as injury to the patient and staff. the time delay associated with the procedure would also inevitably delay effective chest compressions and defibrillation. literature has shown posterior compressions to be at least as effective as supine compressions if performed correctly. ii. if the prone positioned patient suffers cardiac arrest during transport, resuscitation should be attempted in the prone position, with posterior compressions; otherwise following normal acls guidelines appropriate for the scenario. 1. compressions: a. the heel of the compressor's hand should be placed in the center of the upper back, at the level of the 7th thoracic vertebrae. (between scapulae) g. notify receiving facility of positioning to ensure adequate resources will be available to transfer patient upon arrival ccclv. documentation: ccclvi. references: a. drahnak, d. m. & custer, n. (2015). prone positioning of patients with acute respiratory distress syndrome. criticalcarenurse, 35(6), 29-37. b. bell, l. (2020). ards, covid-19 and pronation therapy. american association of critical-care nurses. https://www.aacn.org/blog/ards-covid- 19-and-pronation-therapy
